passages.text: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.

passages.g: Uncontrolled thoughts naturally resist constraint and seek escape.
passages.text: As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground, our thought trembles all over in order to escape the dominion of Mara (the tempter).

passages.g: Mental discipline leads to well-being.
passages.text: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.

passages.g: Careful attention to your thoughts produces contentment.
passages.text: Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to perceive, very artful, and they rush wherever they list: thoughts well guarded bring happiness.

passages.g: Mental discipline frees you from being controlled by desire.
passages.text: Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about alone, is without a body, and hides in the chamber (of the heart), will be free from the bonds of Mara (the tempter).

passages.g: You need mental stability to understand truth.
passages.text: If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his peace of mind is troubled, his knowledge will never be perfect.

passages.g: Undistracted awareness and ethical clarity protect you from harm.
passages.text: If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not perplexed, if he has ceased to think of good or evil, then there is no fear for him while he is watchful.

passages.g: Recognizing impermanence and pursuing understanding are weapons against delusion.
passages.text: Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this thought firm like a fortress, one should attack Mara (the tempter) with the weapon of knowledge, one should watch him when conquered, and should never rest.

passages.g: All physical forms inevitably decay and become worthless.
passages.text: Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, without understanding, like a useless log.

passages.g: Your own uncontrolled mind causes greater damage to you than external enemies.
passages.text: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.

passages.g: Your own disciplined mind serves you better than family ever could.
passages.text: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relative; a well-directed mind will do us greater service.

passages.g: Finding and following the path of virtue requires the keen discernment of a wise person discovering a hidden truth.
passages.text: Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord of the departed), and the world of the gods? Who shall find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower?

passages.g: Those who genuinely practice the path achieve mastery over all domains of existence.
passages.text: The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower.

passages.g: Recognizing the illusory nature of experience and cultivating wisdom frees you from cycles of suffering.
passages.text: He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that it is as unsubstantial as a mirage, will break the flower-pointed arrow of Mara, and never see the king of death.

passages.g: Distraction and lack of mindfulness leave you vulnerable to death's arrival.
passages.text: Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind is distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

passages.g: Unmindful pleasure-seeking prevents you from living meaningfully.
passages.text: Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is distracted, before he is satiated in his pleasures.

passages.g: Live among others without taking more than you need or causing harm.
passages.text: As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower, or its colour or scent, so let a sage dwell in his village.

passages.g: Focus on correcting your own conduct rather than judging others.
passages.text: Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but his own misdeeds and negligences should a sage take notice of.

passages.g: Eloquence without integrity has no real value.
passages.text: Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.

passages.g: When your words match your deeds, you have genuine influence.
passages.text: But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent, are the fine and fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.

passages.g: A person can accomplish multiple meaningful things in a lifetime.
passages.text: As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so many good things may be achieved by a mortal when once he is born.

passages.g: Good character influences everyone around you regardless of obstacles.
passages.text: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that of) sandal-wood, or of Tagara and Mallika flowers; but the odour of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.

passages.g: Ethical character is more valuable than material refinements.
passages.text: Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these sorts of perfumes, the perfume of virtue is unsurpassed.

passages.g: Virtue's influence rises far like divine perfume, while material scents fade.
passages.text: Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood; — the perfume of those who possess virtue rises up to the gods as the highest.

passages.g: Those committed to virtue and wisdom escape being controlled by desire.
passages.text: Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without thoughtlessness, and who are emancipated through true knowledge, Mara, the tempter, never finds the way.

passages.g: Someone who pursues wisdom stands out noticeably in an ignorant world.
passages.text: As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness.

passages.g: When you're not engaged, time feels empty and tedious.
passages.text: Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.

passages.g: Solitude is better than associating with fools.
passages.text: If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.

passages.g: Possessing things is ultimately an illusion because you cannot keep or control them.
passages.text: "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?

passages.g: A fool who recognizes his foolishness has the beginnings of wisdom; a fool who thinks himself wise is beyond help.
passages.text: The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.

passages.g: Some people cannot or will not learn regardless of opportunity or instruction.
passages.text: If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.

passages.g: Intelligence allows rapid comprehension of truth.
passages.text: If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.

passages.g: Ignorance leads people to harm themselves through unethical conduct.
passages.text: Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.

passages.g: Actions that produce shame and regret are fundamentally flawed.
passages.text: That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.

passages.g: Good actions are those you remain proud of.
passages.text: No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.

passages.g: Harmful actions initially seem attractive but eventually bring pain.
passages.text: As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.

passages.g: Discipline alone, without understanding and wisdom, accomplishes nothing.
passages.text: Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of Kusa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.

passages.g: Hidden wrongdoing eventually manifests and causes harm.
passages.text: An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly); smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.

passages.g: When hidden wrongs come to light, they devastate you.
passages.text: And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.

passages.g: Desire for reputation and authority reflects foolishness.
passages.text: Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!

passages.g: The desire for influence and dominance grows the more you indulge it.
passages.text: "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done," thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.

passages.g: Spiritual freedom requires letting go of worldly ambition and status.
passages.text: "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana;" if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world.

passages.g: Seek out and learn from those who guide you toward truth and correct you honestly.
passages.text: If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures are to be found, who shows what is to be avoided, and administers reproofs, follow that wise man; it will be better, not worse, for those who follow him.

passages.g: Those who teach and admonish will be loved by the good and hated by the wicked.
passages.text: Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper! — he will be beloved of the good, by the bad he will be hated.

passages.g: Your companions shape your character, so associate with virtuous people.
passages.text: Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for friends: have virtuous people for friends, have for friends the best of men.

passages.g: Understanding and practicing wisdom leads to contentment.
passages.text: He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the sage rejoices always in the law, as preached by the elect (Ariyas).

passages.g: Through conscious effort, you actively shape your own character.
passages.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.

passages.g: Genuine wisdom makes you unmoved by praise or blame from others.
passages.text: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.

passages.g: Understanding truth brings serenity.
passages.text: Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become serene, like a deep, smooth, and still lake.

passages.g: Virtue enables you to respond to gain and loss without emotional instability.
passages.text: Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle, longing for pleasure; whether touched by happiness or sorrow wise people never appear elated or depressed.

passages.g: True goodness means wanting nothing selfishly and refusing to harm anyone for personal advantage.
passages.text: If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man wishes neither for a son, nor for wealth, nor for lordship, and if he does not wish for his own success by unfair means, then he is good, wise, and virtuous.

passages.g: Few people achieve enlightenment; most remain trapped in worldly existence.
passages.text: Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become Arhats); the other people here run up and down the shore.

passages.g: Those who follow the teachings can overcome death and suffering, however difficult.
passages.text: But those who, when the law has been well preached to them, follow the law, will pass across the dominion of death, however difficult to overcome.

passages.g: Wisdom comes through renouncing worldly life and attachments to find inner peace.
passages.text: A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.

passages.g: Freedom comes through knowledge, non-attachment, and conquering desire.
passages.text: Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of knowledge, who without clinging to anything, rejoice in freedom from attachment, whose appetites have been conquered, and who are full of light, are free (even) in this world.

passages.g: Freedom from suffering requires abandoning grief and all attachments.
passages.text: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.

passages.g: The wise abandon their homes with a clear, resolute mind.
passages.text: They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not happy in their abode; like swans who have left their lake, they leave their house and home.

passages.g: Those who achieve enlightenment through renunciation follow a mysterious path.
passages.text: Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), their path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.

passages.g: The path of one who has conquered desire is beyond others' understanding.
passages.text: He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in enjoyment, who has perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), his path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.

passages.g: One who masters their senses and overcomes desire exceeds all others in worth.
passages.text: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.

passages.g: One who practices duty with patience is freed from the cycle of rebirth.
passages.text: Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like Indra's bolt; he is like a lake without mud; no new births are in store for him.

passages.g: True knowledge brings inner peace reflected in quiet thoughts, words, and actions.
passages.text: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.

passages.g: The greatest person is one who has abandoned all attachments and desires.
passages.text: The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who has cut all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires, he is the greatest of men.

passages.g: Places where the enlightened live are naturally joyful.
passages.text: In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry land, wherever venerable persons (Arhanta) dwell, that place is delightful.

passages.g: Those free from desire find joy in places others find boring.
passages.text: Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there the passionless will find delight, for they look not for pleasures.

passages.g: One meaningful statement is worth more than a thousand meaningless words.
passages.text: Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

passages.g: One meaningful poem is worth more than a thousand meaningless verses.
passages.text: Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

passages.g: One word of truth is worth more than a hundred meaningless poems.
passages.text: Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

passages.g: Mastering yourself is a greater victory than conquering armies.
passages.text: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.

passages.g: Self-mastery is the ultimate victory, unshakeable and permanent.
passages.text: One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.

passages.g: A moment of respect for the truly wise is worth more than a hundred years of ritual sacrifice.
passages.text: If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

passages.g: One moment honoring the wise is worth more than a hundred years of religious practice.
passages.text: If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

passages.g: Respecting the righteous is worth more than a year of offerings.
passages.text: Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter (a farthing); reverence shown to the righteous is better.

passages.g: Respecting elders brings increased life, beauty, happiness, and power.
passages.text: He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four things will increase to him, viz. life, beauty, happiness, power.

passages.g: One day of virtue is worth more than a hundred years of vice.
passages.text: But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is virtuous and reflecting.

passages.g: One day of wisdom is worth more than a hundred years of ignorance.
passages.text: And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is wise and reflecting.

passages.g: One day of inner strength is worth more than a hundred years of weakness.
passages.text: And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one day is better if a man has attained firm strength.

passages.g: One day of understanding causality is worth more than a hundred years without it.
passages.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end, a life of one day is better if a man sees beginning and end.

passages.g: One day of spiritual insight is worth more than a hundred years of spiritual blindness.
passages.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place, a life of one day is better if a man sees the immortal place.

passages.g: One day of knowing the highest truth is worth more than a hundred years of ignorance.
passages.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a life of one day is better if a man sees the highest law.

passages.g: To pursue good, keep your mind away from evil; half-hearted virtue leads to vice.
passages.text: If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his thought away from evil; if a man does what is good slothfully, his mind delights in evil.

passages.g: Avoid repeating wrongs or taking pleasure in them, for evil brings suffering.
passages.text: If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not delight in sin: pain is the outcome of evil.

passages.g: Repeat good deeds and delight in them, for goodness brings happiness.
passages.text: If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight in it: happiness is the outcome of good.

passages.g: Evil may seem pleasant at first, but suffering comes when its effects mature.
passages.text: Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has not ripened; but when his evil deed has ripened, then does the evil-doer see evil.

passages.g: Good may seem burdensome at first, but happiness comes when its effects mature.
passages.text: Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not ripened; but when his good deed has ripened, then does the good man see happy days.

passages.g: Don't dismiss small evils; they accumulate gradually like water filling a vessel.
passages.text: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.

passages.g: Don't dismiss small goods; they accumulate gradually like water filling a vessel.
passages.text: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.

passages.g: Avoid wrongdoing with the same care you would avoid danger or poison.
passages.text: Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few companions and carries much wealth, avoids a dangerous road; as a man who loves life avoids poison.

passages.g: Evil only harms those who commit it; the innocent remain unharmed.
passages.text: He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand; poison does not affect one who has no wound; nor is there evil for one who does not commit evil.

passages.g: When you harm the innocent, the harm rebounds on you, not them.
passages.text: If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil falls back upon that fool, like light dust thrown up against the wind.

passages.g: People's fates depend on their actions and desires—some are reborn, some transcend the cycle.
passages.text: Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous people go to heaven; those who are free from all worldly desires attain Nirvana.

passages.g: No one can escape the consequences of wrongdoing anywhere in the world.
passages.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.

passages.g: No one can escape death regardless of where they hide.
passages.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where death could not overcome (the mortal).

passages.g: Since others fear harm as you do, don't harm them.
passages.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that you are like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.

passages.g: Since all beings love life as you do, don't kill them.
passages.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that thou art like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.

passages.g: Harming others in pursuit of your happiness prevents your own.
passages.text: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.

passages.g: Respecting others' desire for happiness leads to your own happiness.
passages.text: He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings who also long for happiness, will find happiness after death.

passages.g: Harsh speech provokes harsh responses; anger breeds pain in return.
passages.text: Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will answer thee in the same way. Angry speech is painful, blows for blows will touch thee.

passages.g: Silence and non-resistance lead to enlightenment and freedom from conflict.
passages.text: If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then thou hast reached Nirvana; contention is not known to thee.

passages.g: Age and death inevitably drive all human life toward its end.
passages.text: As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so do Age and Death drive the life of men.

passages.g: Wrongdoers suffer from the natural consequences of their own deeds.
passages.text: A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the wicked man burns by his own deeds, as if burnt by fire.

passages.g: Those who harm the innocent face severe consequences.
passages.text: He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon come to one of these ten states:

passages.g: Harming the innocent brings cruel suffering, injury, and mental anguish.
passages.text: He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy affliction, or loss of mind,

passages.g: Harming the innocent brings royal punishment, false accusations, and loss of family and wealth.
passages.text: Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation, or loss of relations, or destruction of treasures,

passages.g: Harming the innocent results in destruction of property and damnation.
passages.text: Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is destroyed, the fool will go to hell.

passages.g: External ascetic practices cannot purify you without conquering desire.
passages.text: Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, not sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.

passages.g: True spiritual attainment comes from inner peace and humility, not external appearance.
passages.text: He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings, he indeed is a Brahmana, an ascetic (sramana), a friar (bhikshu).

passages.g: Is there anyone so humble they accept correction without resentment?
passages.text: Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he does not mind reproof, as a well-trained horse the whip?

passages.g: Respond to criticism with openness; through virtue and practice you overcome suffering.
passages.text: Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active and lively, and by faith, by virtue, by energy, by meditation, by discernment of the law you will overcome this great pain (of reproof), perfect in knowledge and in behaviour, and never forgetful.

passages.g: Like craftspeople shape their materials, people shape their own character.
passages.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; good people fashion themselves.

passages.g: Why seek fleeting joy in a suffering world instead of seeking enlightenment?
passages.text: How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always burning? Why do you not seek a light, ye who are surrounded by darkness?

passages.g: The body is weak, diseased, and lacks any real substance or control.
passages.text: Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined together, sickly, full of many thoughts, which has no strength, no hold!

passages.g: All bodies decay and die; existence inevitably ends.
passages.text: This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of corruption breaks to pieces, life indeed ends in death.

passages.g: There is nothing pleasant in contemplating the decaying remains of the body.
passages.text: Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what pleasure is there in looking at them?

passages.g: The body is constructed from bones and flesh but inevitably decays, bringing aging, death, and mental delusion.
passages.text: After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered with flesh and blood, and there dwell in it old age and death, pride and deceit.

passages.g: The virtue of good people survives bodily death—a truth good people recognize about each other.
passages.text: The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also approaches destruction, but the virtue of good people never approaches destruction, — thus do the good say to the good.

passages.g: Without learning and growth, a person ages physically but remains ignorant.
passages.text: A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh grows, but his knowledge does not grow.

passages.g: Understanding your true nature ends the cycle of suffering and rebirth.
passages.text: Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.

passages.g: People who lack discipline and don't develop themselves in youth end up destitute.
passages.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, perish like old herons in a lake without fish.

passages.g: Those without discipline who wasted their youth live broken and full of regret.
passages.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, lie, like broken bows, sighing after the past.

passages.g: If you value yourself, maintain constant mindfulness and self-awareness.
passages.text: If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully; during one at least out of the three watches a wise man should be watchful.

passages.g: Teach by example: perfect yourself before teaching others, and you'll avoid suffering.
passages.text: Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let him teach others; thus a wise man will not suffer.

passages.g: True teaching requires living what you teach; controlling yourself is far harder than controlling others.
passages.text: If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being himself well subdued, he may subdue (others); one's own self is indeed difficult to subdue.

passages.g: You alone can master yourself; self-mastery is rare and truly valuable.
passages.text: Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord such as few can find.

passages.g: Wrongdoing you commit directly harms you, like a diamond shattering stone.
passages.text: The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the foolish, as a diamond breaks a precious stone.

passages.g: Wickedness entraps the wicked person like a plant strangling the tree it surrounds.
passages.text: He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that state where his enemy wishes him to be, as a creeper does with the tree which it surrounds.

passages.g: Wrongdoing comes easily, but genuine goodness requires real effort.
passages.text: Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what is beneficial and good, that is very difficult to do.

passages.g: Rejecting wisdom and following false teaching leads to self-destruction.
passages.text: The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat), of the elect (Ariya), of the virtuous, and follows false doctrine, he bears fruit to his own destruction, like the fruits of the Katthaka reed.

passages.g: Your own actions create your suffering or purity; you alone are responsible.
passages.text: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.

passages.g: Don't abandon your own duties for others; stay committed to your own responsibilities.
passages.text: Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's, however great; let a man, after he has discerned his own duty, be always attentive to his duty.

passages.g: Avoid vice, laziness, false doctrines, and entanglement with the world.
passages.text: Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness! Do not follow false doctrine! Be not a friend of the world.

passages.g: Pursue virtue with energy, and you'll find happiness in this life and beyond.
passages.text: Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.

passages.g: Virtue leads to happiness both now and in the future.
passages.text: Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.

passages.g: When you see the world as impermanent and illusory, death loses its power over you.
passages.text: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the king of death does not see him who thus looks down upon the world.

passages.g: The world is seductive but illusory; the wise see through it, fools are absorbed by it.
passages.text: Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot; the foolish are immersed in it, but the wise do not touch it.

passages.g: A person who reforms from recklessness brings clarity and light to the world.
passages.text: He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.

passages.g: Good deeds transform you and offset past wrongdoing.
passages.text: He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.

passages.g: Few people see clearly in this world; enlightenment is rare.
passages.text: This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to heaven, like birds escaped from the net.

passages.g: The wise transcend the world through wisdom and effort, overcoming all temptation.
passages.text: The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether by means of their miraculous power; the wise are led out of this world, when they have conquered Mara and his train.

passages.g: Once someone abandons honesty and mockingly rejects the spiritual path, they'll eventually commit any evil.
passages.text: If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs at another world, there is no evil he will not do.

passages.g: Generosity creates happiness; wise people understand it brings rewards in future lives.
passages.text: The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only do not praise liberality; a wise man rejoices in liberality, and through it becomes blessed in the other world.

passages.g: Spiritual progress is more valuable than earthly power or even heavenly pleasures.
passages.text: Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to heaven, better than lordship over all worlds, is the reward of the first step in holiness.

passages.g: An enlightened person cannot be defeated or diverted because they've transcended all attachments.
passages.text: He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no one in this world enters, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?

passages.g: Desire and craving have no power over the enlightened.
passages.text: He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?

passages.g: Enlightened people devoted to meditation are so elevated that even gods envy them.
passages.text: Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who are given to meditation, who are wise, and who delight in the repose of retirement (from the world).

passages.g: Human birth, mortality, access to truth, and enlightenment are all rare.
passages.text: Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the life of mortals, difficult is the hearing of the True Law, difficult is the birth of the Awakened (the attainment of Buddhahood).

passages.g: The core teaching of all enlightened beings is ethical living and mental purification.
passages.text: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of (all) the Awakened.

passages.g: True asceticism is patient endurance, not harming others.
passages.text: The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering the highest Nirvana; for he is not an anchorite (pravragita) who strikes others, he is not an ascetic (sramana) who insults others.

passages.g: Enlightenment comes through non-blame, non-violence, legal restraint, moderation, solitude, and elevated thought.
passages.text: Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law, to be moderate in eating, to sleep and sit alone, and to dwell on the highest thoughts, — this is the teaching of the Awakened.

passages.g: Desires can never be truly satisfied and always cause suffering; wisdom recognizes this.
passages.text: There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces; he who knows that lusts have a short taste and cause pain, he is wise;

passages.g: The enlightened person finds satisfaction only in eliminating all craving, even heavenly pleasures.
passages.text: Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the disciple who is fully awakened delights only in the destruction of all desires.

passages.g: People seek refuge from fear and suffering in external places and objects.
passages.text: Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and forests, to groves and sacred trees.

passages.g: External refuges—places or things—cannot truly protect you from suffering.
passages.text: But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a man is not delivered from all pains after having gone to that refuge.

passages.g: Enlightenment arises from taking refuge in Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha, and understanding suffering's cause and cessation.
passages.text: He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he who, with clear understanding, sees the four holy truths: —

passages.g: [Continuation of id 34—same idea about understanding the four truths and the eightfold path.]
passages.text: Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the eightfold holy way that leads to the quieting of pain; —

passages.g: Understanding the four noble truths is true refuge and frees you from all suffering.
passages.text: That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to that refuge, a man is delivered from all pain.

passages.g: Enlightened beings are rare; the culture around them flourishes.
passages.text: A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not born everywhere. Wherever such a sage is born, that race prospers.

passages.g: The enlightened, their teachings, spiritual community, and peaceful practitioners are sources of universal happiness.
passages.text: Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of the True Law, happy is peace in the church, happy is the devotion of those who are at peace.

passages.g: Honoring those who have conquered suffering creates immeasurable merit.
passages.text: He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody.

passages.g: Maintain inner peace and freedom from hatred even among those who hate you.
passages.text: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!

passages.g: Stay mentally healthy even among those whose minds are sick.
passages.text: Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing! among men who are ailing let us dwell free from ailments!

passages.g: Remain free from greed even surrounded by the greedy.
passages.text: Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy! among men who are greedy let us dwell free from greed!

passages.g: Attachment to nothing brings freedom and happiness like the gods.
passages.text: Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We shall be like the bright gods, feeding on happiness!

passages.g: Competitive victory creates hatred; contentment free from winning and losing brings happiness.
passages.text: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.

passages.g: Passion, hatred, the body's burden, and restlessness are life's worst afflictions; peace is supreme.
passages.text: There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like hatred; there is no pain like this body; there is no happiness higher than rest.

passages.g: Understanding that physical craving and bodily suffering can be transcended is enlightenment's highest happiness.
passages.text: Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains; if one knows this truly, that is Nirvana, the highest happiness.

passages.g: Health, contentment, trust, and spiritual peace are life's greatest gifts.
passages.text: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.

passages.g: Solitude and contemplation free you from fear and wrongdoing.
passages.text: He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is free from fear and free from sin, while he tastes the sweetness of drinking in the law.

passages.g: Being around the wise is good; avoiding fools brings happiness.
passages.text: The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is always happiness; if a man does not see fools, he will be truly happy.

passages.g: Association with fools causes suffering; company with the wise brings joy.
passages.text: He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company with fools, as with an enemy, is always painful; company with the wise is pleasure, like meeting with kinsfolk.

passages.g: Seek out and follow wise, disciplined, and learned people as guides.
passages.text: Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the learned, the much enduring, the dutiful, the elect; one ought to follow a good and wise man, as the moon follows the path of the stars.

passages.g: Pursuing pleasure instead of inner discipline leads to regret and envy of those with real self-cultivation.
passages.text: He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real aim (of life) and grasping at pleasure, will in time envy him who has exerted himself in meditation.

passages.g: Constantly seeking or avoiding specific experiences causes suffering.
passages.text: Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is unpleasant. Not to see what is pleasant is pain, and it is pain to see what is unpleasant.

passages.g: Emotional detachment from all things frees you from suffering.
passages.text: Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is evil. Those who love nothing and hate nothing, have no fetters.

passages.g: Freedom from craving pleasure eliminates grief and fear.
passages.text: From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is free from pleasure knows neither grief nor fear.

passages.g: Freedom from attachment eliminates grief and fear.
passages.text: From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is free from affection knows neither grief nor fear.

passages.g: Freedom from lustful desire eliminates grief and fear.
passages.text: From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from lust knows neither grief nor fear.

passages.g: Freedom from romantic love eliminates grief and fear.
passages.text: From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from love knows neither grief nor fear.

passages.g: Freedom from greed eliminates grief and fear.
passages.text: From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free from greed knows neither grief nor fear.

passages.g: Virtue, integrity, honesty, and right conduct earn respect.
passages.text: He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks the truth, and does what is his own business, him the world will hold dear.

passages.g: Those who pursue spiritual awakening with a clear mind and emotional freedom progress toward liberation.
passages.text: He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up, who is satisfied in his mind, and whose thoughts are not bewildered by love, he is called urdhvamsrotas (carried upwards by the stream).

passages.g: People welcome those returning safely from long absence.
passages.text: Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long away, and returns safe from afar.

passages.g: One's accumulated good deeds greet one in the afterlife like family welcoming a friend.
passages.text: In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and has gone from this world to the other; — as kinsmen receive a friend on his return.

passages.g: Releasing anger, pride, and attachment to possessions prevents suffering.
passages.text: Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome all bondage! No sufferings befall the man who is not attached to name and form, and who calls nothing his own.

passages.g: True strength comes from actively controlling anger, not passively holding it back.
passages.text: He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I call a real driver; other people are but holding the reins.

passages.g: Counter negative behavior with opposite virtues: love against anger, good against evil, generosity against greed, truth against lies.
passages.text: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth!

passages.g: Honesty, emotional control, and generosity lead to wisdom.
passages.text: Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.

passages.g: Those who harm no one and maintain self-discipline reach freedom from suffering.
passages.text: The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body, they will go to the unchangeable place (Nirvana), where, if they have gone, they will suffer no more.

passages.g: Constant mindfulness, learning, and pursuit of enlightenment overcome desire.
passages.text: Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who strive after Nirvana, their passions will come to an end.

passages.g: Everyone faces criticism no matter what they do, so criticism is inevitable and often arbitrary.
passages.text: This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day: `They blame him who sits silent, they blame him who speaks much, they also blame him who says little; there is no one on earth who is not blamed.'

passages.g: No one is universally praised or blamed; everyone experiences both approval and disapproval.
passages.text: There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man who is always blamed, or a man who is always praised.

passages.g: Those of genuine wisdom and virtue earn consistent respect even from the wisest.
passages.text: But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.

passages.g: Control physical impulses and direct them toward virtuous action.
passages.text: Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of the body, and with thy body practise virtue!

passages.g: Monitor and control your speech, avoiding harmful words and speaking truthfully.
passages.text: Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave the sins of the tongue, and practise virtue with thy tongue!

passages.g: Monitor and control your thoughts, directing them away from harm toward virtue.
passages.text: Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the sins of the mind, and practise virtue with thy mind!

passages.g: True wisdom is mastery over body, speech, and thought.
passages.text: The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the wise who control their mind, are indeed well controlled.

passages.g: Death approaches and only spiritual preparation matters.
passages.text: Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama) have come near to thee; thou standest at the door of thy departure, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.

passages.g: Strive for inner cultivation and purification to reach enlightenment.
passages.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt enter into the heavenly world of the elect (Ariya).

passages.g: Death is coming and only spiritual readiness matters, not material accumulation.
passages.text: Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama), there is no resting-place for thee on the road, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.

passages.g: Spiritual cultivation and purification lead to liberation from death and rebirth.
passages.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt not enter again into birth and decay.

passages.g: Self-improvement requires gradual, persistent effort to remove flaws bit by bit.
passages.text: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver one by one, little by little, and from time to time.

passages.g: One's wrongdoings inevitably lead to negative consequences.
passages.text: As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs from it, destroys it; thus do a transgressor's own works lead him to the evil path.

passages.g: Failure to maintain practice, property, health, and mindfulness creates weakness.
passages.text: The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses, non-repair; the taint of the body is sloth; the taint of a watchman, thoughtlessness.

passages.g: Moral failings corrupt anyone who practices them regardless of social position.
passages.text: Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a benefactor; tainted are all evil ways in this world and in the next.

passages.g: Ignorance is the deepest flaw; eradicate it and you become perfect.
passages.text: But there is a taint worse than all taints, — ignorance is the greatest taint. O mendicants! throw off that taint, and become taintless!

passages.g: Those without shame or morality live without restraint and carelessly.
passages.text: Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow hero, a mischief-maker, an insulting, bold, and wretched fellow.

passages.g: A virtuous life requires constant effort and restraint, making it harder than an amoral one.
passages.text: But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for what is pure, who is disinterested, quiet, spotless, and intelligent.

passages.g: Killing, lying, stealing, and adultery are grave transgressions.
passages.text: He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world takes what is not given him, who goes to another man's wife;

passages.g: Intoxication destroys your own foundation in life.
passages.text: And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors, he, even in this world, digs up his own root.

passages.g: Lack of restraint and moral weakness lead to prolonged suffering.
passages.text: O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take care that greediness and vice do not bring thee to grief for a long time!

passages.g: Resentment about others' fortunes prevents peace and contentment.
passages.text: The world gives according to their faith or according to their pleasure: if a man frets about the food and the drink given to others, he will find no rest either by day or by night.

passages.g: Eliminating envy and comparison brings lasting peace.
passages.text: He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, finds rest by day and by night.

passages.g: Desire, hatred, ignorance, and greed are the greatest destructive forces.
passages.text: There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred, there is no snare like folly, there is no torrent like greed.

passages.g: People easily see others' faults but are blind to their own.
passages.text: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.

passages.g: Focusing on others' faults increases your own anger and prevents liberation.
passages.text: If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always inclined to be offended, his own passions will grow, and he is far from the destruction of passions.

passages.g: Spiritual attainment requires inner transformation, not outward performance or appearance.
passages.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. The world delights in vanity, the Tathagatas (the Buddhas) are free from vanity.

passages.g: True spiritual strength comes from inner awakening, not external practices.
passages.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. No creatures are eternal; but the awakened (Buddha) are never shaken.

passages.g: Justice requires wisdom, fairness, and adherence to law—not force.
passages.text: A man is not just if he carries a matter by violence; no, he who distinguishes both right and wrong, who is learned and leads others, not by violence, but by law and equity, and who is guarded by the law and intelligent, he is called just.

passages.g: True learning is wisdom of character rather than verbal knowledge.
passages.text: A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient, free from hatred and fear, he is called learned.

passages.g: Living according to principles matters more than preaching them.
passages.text: A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even if a man has learnt little, but sees the law bodily, he is a supporter of the law, a man who never neglects the law.

passages.g: Age alone does not confer wisdom or seniority.
passages.text: A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be ripe, but he is called `Old-in-vain.'

passages.g: True seniority comes from virtue, wisdom, and integrity rather than age.
passages.text: He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation, he who is free from impurity and is wise, he is called an elder.

passages.g: Reputation cannot be earned through eloquence or appearance if character is flawed.
passages.text: An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by means of much talking only, or by the beauty of his complexion.

passages.g: True respect comes from having eliminated vice and cultivated wisdom.
passages.text: He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, he, when freed from hatred and wise, is called respectable.

passages.g: Spiritual status cannot be claimed through ritual while still enslaved to desire.
passages.text: Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood become a Samana; can a man be a Samana who is still held captive by desire and greediness?

passages.g: One who consistently suppresses wrongdoing in themselves is spiritually advanced.
passages.text: He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is called a Samana (a quiet man), because he has quieted all evil.

passages.g: True spiritual practice is living by principles, not just following external forms.
passages.text: A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others for alms; he who adopts the whole law is a Bhikshu, not he who only begs.

passages.g: The spiritually advanced transcend conventional morality through wisdom and live with clarity.
passages.text: He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge passes through the world, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.

passages.g: A true sage wisely discerns good from evil and acts accordingly, not merely someone who stays silent.
passages.text: A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.

passages.g: Virtue comes from compassion toward all beings.
passages.text: A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living creatures; because he has pity on all living creatures, therefore is a man called Ariya.

passages.g: Spiritual liberation requires complete freedom from desire, not just rituals, study, or ascetic practices.
passages.text: Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires.

passages.g: The eightfold path, acceptance of suffering's truth, freedom from desire, and wisdom are life's highest ideals.
passages.text: The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four words; the best of virtues passionlessness; the best of men he who has eyes to see.

passages.g: This path alone leads to mental clarity; everything else is delusion.
passages.text: This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying of intelligence. Go on this way! Everything else is the deceit of Mara (the tempter).

passages.g: Following this path ends suffering.
passages.text: If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was preached by me, when I had understood the removal of the thorns (in the flesh).

passages.g: Teachers can only show the way; you must do the work yourself to achieve freedom.
passages.text: You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.

passages.g: Understanding that everything eventually perishes brings peace and leads to purity.
passages.text: `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.

passages.g: Recognizing that all existence involves suffering brings acceptance and leads to purity.
passages.text: `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.

passages.g: Understanding that all forms are illusory brings peace and purity.
passages.text: `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.

passages.g: Laziness and lack of discipline prevent the pursuit of knowledge.
passages.text: He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak, that lazy and idle man will never find the way to knowledge.

passages.g: Controlling speech, mind, and bodily actions leads to wisdom.
passages.text: Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never commit any wrong with his body! Let a man but keep these three roads of action clear, and he will achieve the way which is taught by the wise.

passages.g: Effort sustains learning while laziness causes it to decay.
passages.text: Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge is lost; let a man who knows this double path of gain and loss thus place himself that knowledge may grow.

passages.g: You must completely overcome desire, not just resist individual temptations, to achieve freedom.
passages.text: Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger comes out of the forest (of lust). When you have cut down both the forest (of lust) and its undergrowth, then, Bhikshus, you will be rid of the forest and free!

passages.g: Attachment to romantic love enslaves the mind.
passages.text: So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is not destroyed, so long is his mind in bondage, as the calf that drinks milk is to its mother.

passages.g: Overcoming self-love leads to peace and enlightenment.
passages.text: Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand! Cherish the road of peace. Nirvana has been shown by Sugata (Buddha).

passages.g: Fools ignore mortality and plan as if they'll live forever.
passages.text: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.

passages.g: Death comes suddenly and unexpectedly for the distracted and the wealthy.
passages.text: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

passages.g: Family cannot protect you when death arrives.
passages.text: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.

passages.g: Those who grasp mortality's reality should pursue enlightenment immediately.
passages.text: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana.

passages.g: Wise people sacrifice immediate gratification for greater long-term benefits.
passages.text: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.

passages.g: Harming others for your own gain traps you in hatred.
passages.text: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.

passages.g: Undisciplined people ignore their duties and indulge desires, making cravings grow.
passages.text: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.

passages.g: Mindfulness and adherence to right action lead to freedom from desire.
passages.text: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.

passages.g: True wisdom transcends conventional morality—inner purity persists beyond outward actions.
passages.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.

passages.g: True wisdom transcends conventional morality and judgment.
passages.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.

passages.g: Devoted practitioners maintain constant mindful awareness.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.

passages.g: Committed practitioners stay focused on moral and spiritual principles.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.

passages.g: Committed practitioners stay focused on community and tradition.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.

passages.g: Mindfulness of one's body leads to awareness and awakening.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.

passages.g: Dedicated practitioners cultivate compassion as a continuous practice.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.

passages.g: Dedicated practitioners make meditation a constant practice.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.

passages.g: All paths in life involve difficulty; choose carefully which hardship you're willing to endure.
passages.text: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.

passages.g: Virtue, fame, and wealth bring respect everywhere.
passages.text: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.

passages.g: Good character is visible and recognized; wickedness is hidden.
passages.text: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.

passages.g: Solitude and self-discipline lead to freedom from desire.
passages.text: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest.

passages.g: Lying and denying your wrongdoing both corrupt you equally.
passages.text: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.

passages.g: Wearing religious garb doesn't make you virtuous; only right conduct matters.
passages.text: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.

passages.g: It's worse to live dishonorably on others' charity than to endure extreme suffering.
passages.text: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.

passages.g: Lusting after another's spouse brings ruin: bad reputation, misery, punishment, and damnation.
passages.text: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife, — a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.

passages.g: Adultery leads to shame, legal punishment, and spiritual ruin, with only brief pleasure.
passages.text: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.

passages.g: Harsh or misguided self-discipline harms you and leads to ruin.
passages.text: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.

passages.g: Careless action, broken commitments, and halfhearted discipline yield no spiritual benefit.
passages.text: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.

passages.g: Commit fully to what you do; half-measures only spread confusion and desire.
passages.text: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.

passages.g: Avoid wrongdoing because regret follows; pursue good because it brings no regret.
passages.text: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.

passages.g: Maintain constant vigilance against vice; missed moments of opportunity lead to suffering.
passages.text: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.

passages.g: Misplaced shame and lack of proper shame lead to false beliefs and wrongdoing.
passages.text: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.

passages.g: Irrational fear and lack of appropriate caution lead to false beliefs and harm.
passages.text: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

passages.g: Misapplied moral rules corrupt belief and action.
passages.text: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

passages.g: Right understanding of what truly is and isn't harmful leads to truth and right action.
passages.text: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.

passages.g: Endure insults patiently; the world is naturally harsh.
passages.text: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.

passages.g: A person who bears insults calmly is as valuable as a trained elephant in battle.
passages.text: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.

passages.g: Self-mastery is greater than training any animal.
passages.text: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.

passages.g: Only self-discipline allows you to reach enlightenment.
passages.text: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.

passages.g: Captive, restless creatures long for their natural home and reject captive comfort.
passages.text: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.

passages.g: Indulgence in food and ease traps you in cycles of rebirth.
passages.text: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.

passages.g: I will control my wandering mind with discipline.
passages.text: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.

passages.g: Stay mentally vigilant and consciously pull yourself out of harmful patterns.
passages.text: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.

passages.g: A wise and virtuous companion helps you overcome life's challenges.
passages.text: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.

passages.g: Solitude with integrity is better than companionship with a fool.
passages.text: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind, — like an elephant in the forest.

passages.g: Solitude with virtue is better than foolish company.
passages.text: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.

passages.g: Friendship, pleasure, doing good deeds at life's end, and freedom from grief all bring their own joy.
passages.text: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.

passages.g: Being a parent, renunciate, or wise person each brings its own satisfaction.
passages.text: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.

passages.g: Virtue, faith, wisdom, and ethical conduct bring lasting satisfaction.
passages.text: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins.

passages.g: Undisciplined craving grows unchecked and drives compulsive cycles of wanting.
passages.text: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.

passages.g: Being consumed by craving intensifies suffering.
passages.text: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.

passages.g: Overcoming craving removes suffering.
passages.text: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.

passages.g: Eliminate craving at its root to escape repeated suffering.
passages.text: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'

passages.g: Suffering returns unless you destroy the root causes of craving.
passages.text: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.

passages.g: Intense, uncontrolled desire for pleasure overwhelms and controls you.
passages.text: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.

passages.g: Knowledge is how you cut off passion before it spreads.
passages.text: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.

passages.g: Chasing pleasure traps people in repeated cycles of suffering and death.
passages.text: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.

passages.g: Craving binds people in suffering through endless repetition.
passages.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.

passages.g: Escape craving by cultivating detachment and passionlessness.
passages.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.

passages.g: Returning to desire after tasting freedom is self-imposed bondage.
passages.text: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.

passages.g: Attachment to possessions and loved ones binds more strongly than physical chains.
passages.text: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.

passages.g: Emotional attachments are the strongest bonds; severing them brings freedom.
passages.text: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.

passages.g: Passionate people are trapped by their own desires until wisdom breaks free.
passages.text: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.

passages.g: Complete detachment from all desires ends suffering and the cycle of rebirth.
passages.text: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.

passages.g: Doubt and unchecked craving intensify suffering and strengthen one's bondage.
passages.text: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.

passages.g: Meditating on life's ugliness and impermanence destroys delusion and spiritual bondage.
passages.text: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, etc.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.

passages.g: Freedom from craving and harm brings peace and ends rebirth.
passages.text: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.

passages.g: The truly wise achieve complete detachment and understanding, ending rebirth.
passages.text: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.

passages.g: Complete freedom comes from mastering life and eliminating craving.
passages.text: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'

passages.g: Understanding truth brings deeper satisfaction than any material or sensory pleasure.
passages.text: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.

passages.g: Chasing pleasure without seeking meaning leads to self-destruction.
passages.text: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.

passages.g: Passion damages human flourishing; supporting those free from it has value.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.

passages.g: Hatred damages potential; supporting those free from it is worthwhile.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.

passages.g: Vanity harms human development; supporting those free from it matters.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.
