passages.g: Laziness leads to deterioration and ruin.
passages.text: And, lo, it was all grown over with thorns, and nettles had covered the face thereof, and the stone wall thereof was broken down. Then I saw, and considered it well: I looked upon it, and received instruction.

passages.g: Procrastination and idleness bring poverty.
passages.text: Yet a little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to sleep: So shall thy poverty come as one that travelleth; and thy want as an armed man.

passages.g: God's power includes mystery, while a king's strength lies in investigation and understanding.
passages.text: These are also proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out. It is the glory of God to conceal a thing: but the honour of kings is to search out a matter.

passages.g: The minds of kings are unknowable, and refinement removes impurities.
passages.text: The heaven for height, and the earth for depth, and the heart of kings is unsearchable. Take away the dross from the silver, and there shall come forth a vessel for the finer.

passages.g: A king's stability depends on removing wicked advisors, and discretion is proper before the powerful.
passages.text: Take away the wicked from before the king, and his throne shall be established in righteousness. Put not forth thyself in the presence of the king, and stand not in the place of great men:

passages.g: Wait to be invited to higher positions rather than presuming to seize them.
passages.text: For better it is that it be said unto thee, Come up hither; than that thou shouldest be put lower in the presence of the prince whom thine eyes have seen.

passages.g: Avoid hasty conflict and resolve disputes directly with the other person rather than involving third parties.
passages.text: Go not forth hastily to strive, lest thou know not what to do in the end thereof, when thy neighbour hath put thee to shame. Debate thy cause with thy neighbour himself; and discover not a secret to another:

passages.g: Spreading secrets brings shame, while well-chosen words are precious.
passages.text: Lest he that heareth it put thee to shame, and thine infamy turn not away. A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in pictures of silver.

passages.g: Wise correction received willingly is valuable and beautiful.
passages.text: As an earring of gold, and an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise reprover upon an obedient ear.

passages.g: Reliable messengers bring refreshment, while false boasts deliver nothing.
passages.text: As the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to them that send him: for he refresheth the soul of his masters. Whoso boasteth himself of a false gift is like clouds and wind without rain.

passages.g: Patience and gentle speech can persuade even the powerful, and moderation in pleasures prevents excess.
passages.text: By long forbearing is a prince persuaded, and a soft tongue breaketh the bone. Hast thou found honey? eat so much as is sufficient for thee, lest thou be filled therewith, and vomit it.

passages.g: Overstaying your welcome breeds resentment, and false witness causes serious harm.
passages.text: Withdraw thy foot from thy neighbour’s house; lest he be weary of thee, and so hate thee. A man that beareth false witness against his neighbour is a maul, and a sword, and a sharp arrow.

passages.g: Trusting an unfaithful person in crisis leaves you helpless.
passages.text: Confidence in an unfaithful man in time of trouble is like a broken tooth, and a foot out of joint.

passages.g: Forced cheerfulness wounds the sorrowful, but caring for enemies can transform them.
passages.text: As he that taketh away a garment in cold weather, and as vinegar upon nitre, so is he that singeth songs to an heavy heart. If thine enemy be hungry, give him bread to eat; and if he be thirsty, give him water to drink:

passages.g: Kindness to enemies ultimately shames them, and a stern look stops slanderers.
passages.text: For thou shalt heap coals of fire upon his head, and the LORD shall reward thee. The north wind driveth away rain: so doth an angry countenance a backbiting tongue.

passages.g: Solitude is preferable to living with conflict, and good news brings refreshment.
passages.text: It is better to dwell in the corner of the housetop, than with a brawling woman and in a wide house. As cold waters to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country.

passages.g: A righteous person submitting to the wicked corrupts justice, and excessive self-promotion undermines genuine honor.
passages.text: A righteous man falling down before the wicked is as a troubled fountain, and a corrupt spring. It is not good to eat much honey: so for men to search their own glory is not glory.

passages.g: Lack of self-control leaves you defenseless against harm.
passages.text: He that hath no rule over his own spirit is like a city that is broken down, and without walls.

passages.g: Fools do not deserve honor, and curses without cause do not take effect.
passages.text: As snow in summer, and as rain in harvest, so honour is not seemly for a fool. As the bird by wandering, as the swallow by flying, so the curse causeless shall not come.

passages.g: Fools require firm discipline, and engaging foolishly with them makes you foolish.
passages.text: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool’s back. Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest thou also be like unto him.

passages.g: Sometimes respond to fools to prevent them deceiving themselves, and entrusting fools with important tasks brings failure.
passages.text: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own conceit. He that sendeth a message by the hand of a fool cutteth off the feet, and drinketh damage.

passages.g: Wise sayings are useless from fools' mouths, and honoring a fool brings ruin.
passages.text: The legs of the lame are not equal: so is a parable in the mouth of fools. As he that bindeth a stone in a sling, so is he that giveth honour to a fool.

passages.g: Fools wound themselves with wisdom they cannot comprehend, and God ultimately rewards all according to their deeds.
passages.text: As a thorn goeth up into the hand of a drunkard, so is a parable in the mouths of fools. The great God that formed all things both rewardeth the fool, and rewardeth transgressors.

passages.g: Fools repeat their mistakes, and someone deluded about their own wisdom is more hopeless than a fool.
passages.text: As a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly. Seest thou a man wise in his own conceit? there is more hope of a fool than of him.

passages.g: Lazy people make excuses for inaction and remain inactive.
passages.text: The slothful man saith, There is a lion in the way; a lion is in the streets. As the door turneth upon his hinges, so doth the slothful upon his bed.

passages.g: Lazy people are too idle to even feed themselves, convinced of their own wisdom despite being worthless.
passages.text: The slothful hideth his hand in his bosom; it grieveth him to bring it again to his mouth. The sluggard is wiser in his own conceit than seven men that can render a reason.

passages.g: Interfering in others' disputes is as dangerous as grabbing a dog by the ears.
passages.text: He that passeth by, and meddleth with strife belonging not to him, is like one that taketh a dog by the ears. As a mad man who casteth firebrands, arrows, and death,

passages.g: Deception masked as jest is still harmful, and gossip fuels conflict.
passages.text: So is the man that deceiveth his neighbour, and saith, Am not I in sport? Where no wood is, there the fire goeth out: so where there is no talebearer, the strife ceaseth.

passages.g: Contentious people fan the flames of conflict, and gossip causes deep wounds.
passages.text: As coals are to burning coals, and wood to fire; so is a contentious man to kindle strife. The words of a talebearer are as wounds, and they go down into the innermost parts of the belly.

passages.g: Beautiful words from a wicked person are false pretense, and those who hate hide hatred beneath deception.
passages.text: Burning lips and a wicked heart are like a potsherd covered with silver dross. He that hateth dissembleth with his lips, and layeth up deceit within him;

passages.g: Do not trust fair words from a deceptive heart, and hidden wickedness is eventually exposed.
passages.text: When he speaketh fair, believe him not: for there are seven abominations in his heart. Whose hatred is covered by deceit, his wickedness shall be shewed before the whole congregation.

passages.g: Evil schemes backfire on those who devise them, and deceptive speech destroys.
passages.text: Whoso diggeth a pit shall fall therein: and he that rolleth a stone, it will return upon him. A lying tongue hateth those that are afflicted by it; and a flattering mouth worketh ruin.

passages.g: Do not boast about future plans since the future is uncertain, and let others praise you rather than praising yourself.
passages.text: Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth. Let another man praise thee, and not thine own mouth; a stranger, and not thine own lips.

passages.g: Fools' anger is destructive and envy is worse, while honest rebuke is better than secret affection.
passages.text: A stone is heavy, and the sand weighty; but a fool’s wrath is heavier than them both. Wrath is cruel, and anger is outrageous; but who is able to stand before envy? Open rebuke is better than secret love.

passages.g: Honest criticism from friends is valuable while flattery from enemies deceives, and need makes even bitter things palatable.
passages.text: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful. The full soul loatheth an honeycomb; but to the hungry soul every bitter thing is sweet.

passages.g: Straying from your place brings ruin, and wise counsel from friends brings comfort.
passages.text: As a bird that wandereth from her nest, so is a man that wandereth from his place. Ointment and perfume rejoice the heart: so doth the sweetness of a man’s friend by hearty counsel.

passages.g: Keep your old friendships and seek help from those nearby rather than distant family in times of need.
passages.text: Thine own friend, and thy father’s friend, forsake not; neither go into thy brother’s house in the day of thy calamity: for better is a neighbour that is near than a brother far off.

passages.g: Wisdom brings parental pride and enables you to avoid danger, while the simple-minded suffer.
passages.text: My son, be wise, and make my heart glad, that I may answer him that reproacheth me. A prudent man foreseeth the evil, and hideth himself; but the simple pass on, and are punished.

passages.g: Do not blindly become surety for strangers' debts, and loud early greetings can annoy.
passages.text: Take his garment that is surety for a stranger, and take a pledge of him for a strange woman. He that blesseth his friend with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, it shall be counted a curse to him.

passages.g: Living with a contentious woman is as unbearable as a constant leak.
passages.text: A continual dropping in a very rainy day and a contentious woman are alike. Whosoever hideth her hideth the wind, and the ointment of his right hand, which bewrayeth itself.

passages.g: Good friends sharpen each other's character, and faithful service brings honor.
passages.text: Iron sharpeneth iron; so a man sharpeneth the countenance of his friend. Whoso keepeth the fig tree shall eat the fruit thereof: so he that waiteth on his master shall be honoured.

passages.g: People's hearts mirror each other like faces in water, and human desires are never satisfied.
passages.text: As in water face answereth to face, so the heart of man to man. Hell and destruction are never full; so the eyes of man are never satisfied.

passages.g: A person is revealed by praise, and foolishness is inherent and unchangeable.
passages.text: As the fining pot for silver, and the furnace for gold; so is a man to his praise. Though thou shouldest bray a fool in a mortar among wheat with a pestle, yet will not his foolishness depart from him.

passages.g: Manage your property carefully, for riches and power are not permanent.
passages.text: Be thou diligent to know the state of thy flocks, and look well to thy herds. For riches are not for ever: and doth the crown endure to every generation?

passages.g: Pastoral wealth comes from diligent management of flocks and fields.
passages.text: The hay appeareth, and the tender grass sheweth itself, and herbs of the mountains are gathered. The lambs are for thy clothing, and the goats are the price of the field.

passages.g: Diligent pastoral management provides for all your household's needs.
passages.text: And thou shalt have goats’ milk enough for thy food, for the food of thy household, and for the maintenance for thy maidens.

passages.g: The righteous are fearless while the wicked flee from imaginary dangers, and a nation's stability depends on wise, knowledgeable leadership.
passages.text: The wicked flee when no man pursueth: but the righteous are bold as a lion. For the transgression of a land many are the princes thereof: but by a man of understanding and knowledge the state thereof shall be prolonged.

passages.g: A poor person oppressing others like them brings ruin, and abandoning law enables wickedness.
passages.text: A poor man that oppresseth the poor is like a sweeping rain which leaveth no food. They that forsake the law praise the wicked: but such as keep the law contend with them.

passages.g: Integrity matters more than wealth, and seeking moral wisdom matters more than raw intelligence.
passages.text: Evil men understand not judgment: but they that seek the LORD understand all things. Better is the poor that walketh in his uprightness, than he that is perverse in his ways, though he be rich.

passages.g: Following principles shows wisdom and honors family; unjust wealth is ultimately lost.
passages.text: Whoso keepeth the law is a wise son: but he that is a companion of riotous men shameth his father. He that by usury and unjust gain increaseth his substance, he shall gather it for him that will pity the poor.

passages.g: Refusing to listen to moral teaching invalidates spiritual practice.
passages.text: He that turneth away his ear from hearing the law, even his prayer shall be abomination.

passages.g: Those who corrupt the virtuous harm themselves; the virtuous are rewarded.
passages.text: Whoso causeth the righteous to go astray in an evil way, he shall fall himself into his own pit: but the upright shall have good things in possession.

passages.g: Wealth breeds false confidence while true wisdom can emerge from humble people; righteous leadership creates flourishing, wicked leadership creates fear.
passages.text: The rich man is wise in his own conceit; but the poor that hath understanding searcheth him out. When righteous men do rejoice, there is great glory: but when the wicked rise, a man is hidden.

passages.g: Acknowledging and correcting your wrongs leads to success; caution keeps you safe while stubbornness brings harm.
passages.text: He that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy. Happy is the man that feareth alway: but he that hardeneth his heart shall fall into mischief.

passages.g: Wicked leaders prey on the vulnerable; incompetence in power causes oppression, while rejecting greed extends life.
passages.text: As a roaring lion, and a ranging bear; so is a wicked ruler over the poor people. The prince that wanteth understanding is also a great oppressor: but he that hateth covetousness shall prolong his days.

passages.g: Violence destroys the perpetrator; upright conduct saves you while corruption leads to sudden destruction.
passages.text: A man that doeth violence to the blood of any person shall flee to the pit; let no man stay him. Whoso walketh uprightly shall be saved: but he that is perverse in his ways shall fall at once.

passages.g: Focused work leads to abundance; pursuing worthless things leads to poverty.
passages.text: He that tilleth his land shall have plenty of bread: but he that followeth after vain persons shall have poverty enough.

passages.g: Faithfulness leads to abundance while rushing to wealth corrupts your integrity; favoritism corrupts principles.
passages.text: A faithful man shall abound with blessings: but he that maketh haste to be rich shall not be innocent. To have respect of persons is not good: for for a piece of bread that man will transgress.

passages.g: Greed blinds you to consequences; honest correction is more respected than flattery.
passages.text: He that hasteth to be rich hath an evil eye, and considereth not that poverty shall come upon him. He that rebuketh a man afterwards shall find more favour than he that flattereth with the tongue.

passages.g: Exploiting family while denying wrongdoing associates you with destruction.
passages.text: Whoso robbeth his father or his mother, and saith, It is no transgression; the same is the companion of a destroyer.

passages.g: Arrogance creates conflict while humility leads to thriving; self-reliance is foolish but wisdom saves you.
passages.text: He that is of a proud heart stirreth up strife: but he that putteth his trust in the LORD shall be made fat. He that trusteth in his own heart is a fool: but whoso walketh wisely, he shall be delivered.

passages.g: Generosity protects you from need while indifference brings consequences; wicked leadership causes fear while righteous leadership enables flourishing.
passages.text: He that giveth unto the poor shall not lack: but he that hideth his eyes shall have many a curse. When the wicked rise, men hide themselves: but when they perish, the righteous increase.

passages.g: Refusing to accept correction leads to sudden downfall; righteous leadership creates public joy while wicked leadership creates mourning.
passages.text: He, that being often reproved hardeneth his neck, shall suddenly be destroyed, and that without remedy. When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice: but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.

passages.g: Pursuing wisdom honors family while bad company wastes resources; fair judgment stabilizes society while corruption destabilizes it.
passages.text: Whoso loveth wisdom rejoiceth his father: but he that keepeth company with harlots spendeth his substance. The king by judgment establisheth the land: but he that receiveth gifts overthroweth it.

passages.g: Flattery sets traps; wrongdoing traps you while righteousness brings joy.
passages.text: A man that flattereth his neighbour spreadeth a net for his feet. In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare: but the righteous doth sing and rejoice.

passages.g: The virtuous show concern for the vulnerable while the wicked ignore them; mockery destabilizes communities while wisdom prevents conflict.
passages.text: The righteous considereth the cause of the poor: but the wicked regardeth not to know it. Scornful men bring a city into a snare: but wise men turn away wrath.

passages.g: Arguing with a fool yields no resolution; violent people oppose the virtuous.
passages.text: If a wise man contendeth with a foolish man, whether he rage or laugh, there is no rest. The bloodthirsty hate the upright: but the just seek his soul.

passages.g: Foolish people overshare while wise people reflect before speaking; a corrupt leader enables corruption in his organization.
passages.text: A fool uttereth all his mind: but a wise man keepeth it in till afterwards. If a ruler hearken to lies, all his servants are wicked. The poor and the deceitful man meet together: the LORD lighteneth both their eyes.

passages.g: Fair treatment of the vulnerable creates lasting political stability; correction teaches wisdom while neglect harms children.
passages.text: The king that faithfully judgeth the poor, his throne shall be established for ever. The rod and reproof give wisdom: but a child left to himself bringeth his mother to shame.

passages.g: Widespread wrongdoing enables more wrongdoing but virtue eventually triumphs; guiding your child brings peace and joy.
passages.text: When the wicked are multiplied, transgression increaseth: but the righteous shall see their fall. Correct thy son, and he shall give thee rest; yea, he shall give delight unto thy soul.

passages.g: Communities need direction and purpose; following principles brings happiness, but some people won't accept correction even when they understand.
passages.text: Where there is no vision, the people perish: but he that keepeth the law, happy is he. A servant will not be corrected by words: for though he understand he will not answer.

passages.g: Impulsive speech is worse than foolishness; raising someone with kindness creates loyalty and belonging.
passages.text: Seest thou a man that is hasty in his words? there is more hope of a fool than of him. He that delicately bringeth up his servant from a child shall have him become his son at the length.

passages.g: Anger creates conflict and enables wrongdoing; pride leads to downfall while humility leads to honor.
passages.text: An angry man stirreth up strife, and a furious man aboundeth in transgression. A man’s pride shall bring him low: but honour shall uphold the humble in spirit.

passages.g: Complicity in wrongdoing harms yourself and enables continued harm; people-pleasing traps you while trusting in principles protects you.
passages.text: Whoso is partner with a thief hateth his own soul: he heareth cursing, and bewrayeth it not. The fear of man bringeth a snare: but whoso putteth his trust in the LORD shall be safe.

passages.g: True judgment transcends political favor; the virtuous and corrupt are fundamentally opposed.
passages.text: Many seek the ruler’s favour; but every man’s judgment cometh from the LORD. An unjust man is an abomination to the just: and he that is upright in the way is abomination to the wicked.

passages.g: I acknowledge my own ignorance and lack of understanding.
passages.text: The words of Agur the son of Jakeh, even the prophecy: the man spake unto Ithiel, even unto Ithiel and Ucal, Surely I am more brutish than any man, and have not the understanding of a man.

passages.g: I lack wisdom and divine knowledge.
passages.text: I neither learned wisdom, nor have the knowledge of the holy.

passages.g: Only divine power can accomplish cosmic feats like gathering wind and establishing the earth.
passages.text: Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended? who hath gathered the wind in his fists? who hath bound the waters in a garment? who hath established all the ends of the earth? what is his name, and what is his son’s name, if thou canst tell?

passages.g: Divine guidance is trustworthy and protective; don't distort it or you'll be exposed as a liar.
passages.text: Every word of God is pure: he is a shield unto them that put their trust in him. Add thou not unto his words, lest he reprove thee, and thou be found a liar.

passages.g: I ask to be free from deception and extremes—neither poverty nor riches, just what I need.
passages.text: Two things have I required of thee; deny me them not before I die: Remove far from me vanity and lies: give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with food convenient for me:

passages.g: Extreme wealth or poverty can lead to sin; don't falsely accuse someone to their superior.
passages.text: Lest I be full, and deny thee, and say, Who is the LORD? or lest I be poor, and steal, and take the name of my God in vain. Accuse not a servant unto his master, lest he curse thee, and thou be found guilty.

passages.g: Some people show no respect to their parents; some believe they're virtuous while actually being corrupt.
passages.text: There is a generation that curseth their father, and doth not bless their mother. There is a generation that are pure in their own eyes, and yet is not washed from their filthiness.

passages.g: Arrogant people display their superiority through haughty looks.
passages.text: There is a generation, O how lofty are their eyes! and their eyelids are lifted up.

passages.g: Some people ruthlessly exploit the vulnerable for their own gain.
passages.text: There is a generation, whose teeth are as swords, and their jaw teeth as knives, to devour the poor from off the earth, and the needy from among men.

passages.g: Greed is insatiable—some desires never reach satisfaction.
passages.text: The horseleach hath two daughters, crying, Give, give. There are three things that are never satisfied, yea, four things say not, It is enough:

passages.g: Death, infertility, drought, and fire never reach satisfaction.
passages.text: The grave; and the barren womb; the earth that is not filled with water; and the fire that saith not, It is enough.

passages.g: Mocking your parents brings severe consequences.
passages.text: The eye that mocketh at his father, and despiseth to obey his mother, the ravens of the valley shall pick it out, and the young eagles shall eat it.

passages.g: There are mysterious things I don't understand.
passages.text: There be three things which are too wonderful for me, yea, four which I know not:

passages.g: The paths of an eagle in air, a serpent on rock, a ship in sea, and a man with a young woman all leave no visible trace.
passages.text: The way of an eagle in the air; the way of a serpent upon a rock; the way of a ship in the midst of the sea; and the way of a man with a maid.

passages.g: An adulterous woman denies wrongdoing just as untraceable paths leave no evidence; some disturbances destabilize society.
passages.text: Such is the way of an adulterous woman; she eateth, and wipeth her mouth, and saith, I have done no wickedness. For three things the earth is disquieted, and for four which it cannot bear:

passages.g: Four unbearable disturbances: a servant becoming a ruler, a fool gaining abundance, an unlikeable woman getting married, or a servant inheriting from her master.
passages.text: For a servant when he reigneth; and a fool when he is filled with meat; For an odious woman when she is married; and an handmaid that is heir to her mistress.

passages.g: Small creatures demonstrate wisdom through industriousness.
passages.text: There be four things which are little upon the earth, but they are exceeding wise: The ants are a people not strong, yet they prepare their meat in the summer;

passages.g: Weak creatures succeed through cleverness: building shelter, coordinating action, and gaining access to power.
passages.text: The conies are but a feeble folk, yet make they their houses in the rocks; The locusts have no king, yet go they forth all of them by bands; The spider taketh hold with her hands, and is in kings’ palaces.

passages.g: Confident, purposeful bearing is admirable: the lion, greyhound, goat, and unchallenged king all move with dignity.
passages.text: There be three things which go well, yea, four are comely in going: A lion which is strongest among beasts, and turneth not away for any; A greyhound; an he goat also; and a king, against whom there is no rising up.

passages.g: When you realize you've acted foolishly or harbored evil thoughts, stay silent.
passages.text: If thou hast done foolishly in lifting up thyself, or if thou hast thought evil, lay thine hand upon thy mouth.

passages.g: Just as pressure creates results from milk and noses, forcing anger creates conflict.
passages.text: Surely the churning of milk bringeth forth butter, and the wringing of the nose bringeth forth blood: so the forcing of wrath bringeth forth strife.

passages.g: Don't let women or destructive pursuits control you and your power.
passages.text: The words of king Lemuel, the prophecy that his mother taught him. What, my son? and what, the son of my womb? and what, the son of my vows? Give not thy strength unto women, nor thy ways to that which destroyeth kings.

passages.g: Leaders shouldn't indulge in intoxication because it corrupts judgment and harms the vulnerable.
passages.text: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine; nor for princes strong drink: Lest they drink, and forget the law, and pervert the judgment of any of the afflicted.

passages.g: For the hopeless or despairing, intoxication can be a mercy.
passages.text: Give strong drink unto him that is ready to perish, and wine unto those that be of heavy hearts. Let him drink, and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.

passages.g: Advocate for the voiceless, judge fairly, and defend the vulnerable.
passages.text: Open thy mouth for the dumb in the cause of all such as are appointed to destruction. Open thy mouth, judge righteously, and plead the cause of the poor and needy.

passages.g: A woman of integrity is invaluable; she enables her husband's security and brings lasting good.
passages.text: Who can find a virtuous woman? for her price is far above rubies. The heart of her husband doth safely trust in her, so that he shall have no need of spoil. She will do him good and not evil all the days of her life.

passages.g: She works industriously and trades widely for resources.
passages.text: She seeketh wool, and flax, and worketh willingly with her hands. She is like the merchants’ ships; she bringeth her food from afar.

passages.g: She works tirelessly to provide for others and invests in productive assets.
passages.text: She riseth also while it is yet night, and giveth meat to her household, and a portion to her maidens. She considereth a field, and buyeth it: with the fruit of her hands she planteth a vineyard.

passages.g: She's physically capable, her work is profitable, and she works constantly.
passages.text: She girdeth her loins with strength, and strengtheneth her arms. She perceiveth that her merchandise is good: her candle goeth not out by night. She layeth her hands to the spindle, and her hands hold the distaff.

passages.g: She's generous to the vulnerable and has well-provided for her family's security.
passages.text: She stretcheth out her hand to the poor; yea, she reacheth forth her hands to the needy. She is not afraid of the snow for her household: for all her household are clothed with scarlet.

passages.g: She lives well and her success enables her husband's social prominence.
passages.text: She maketh herself coverings of tapestry; her clothing is silk and purple. Her husband is known in the gates, when he sitteth among the elders of the land.

passages.g: She produces goods for trade, embodies strength and honor, and achieves enduring joy.
passages.text: She maketh fine linen, and selleth it; and delivereth girdles unto the merchant. Strength and honour are her clothing; and she shall rejoice in time to come.

passages.g: She speaks with wisdom and kindness, manages her household well, and refuses idleness.
passages.text: She openeth her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue is the law of kindness. She looketh well to the ways of her household, and eateth not the bread of idleness.

passages.g: Character and integrity earn lasting respect from family.
passages.text: Her children arise up, and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praiseth her. Many daughters have done virtuously, but thou excellest them all.

passages.g: Character matters more than appearance; good works speak for themselves.
passages.text: Favour is deceitful, and beauty is vain: but a woman that feareth the LORD, she shall be praised. Give her of the fruit of her hands; and let her own works praise her in the gates.

passages.g: Human labor produces no lasting gain; the world endures while generations pass.
passages.text: The words of the Preacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem. Vanity of vanities, saith the Preacher, vanity of vanities; all is vanity. What profit hath a man of all his labour which he taketh under the sun? One generation passeth away, and another generation cometh: but the earth abideth for ever.

passages.g: Natural phenomena repeat in endless cycles.
passages.text: The sun also ariseth, and the sun goeth down, and hasteth to his place where he arose. The wind goeth toward the south, and turneth about unto the north; it whirleth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits.

passages.g: Constant natural activity continues endlessly without producing lasting results.
passages.text: All the rivers run into the sea; yet the sea is not full; unto the place from whence the rivers come, thither they return again. All things are full of labour; man cannot utter it: the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.

passages.g: Nothing is genuinely new; history repeats itself.
passages.text: The thing that hath been, it is that which shall be; and that which is done is that which shall be done: and there is no new thing under the sun. Is there any thing whereof it may be said, See, this is new? it hath been already of old time, which was before us.

passages.g: Future generations will forget those who came before.
passages.text: There is no remembrance of former things; neither shall there be any remembrance of things that are to come with those that shall come after. I the Preacher was king over Israel in Jerusalem.

passages.g: Pursuing wisdom to understand everything leads only to frustration.
passages.text: And I gave my heart to seek and search out by wisdom concerning all things that are done under heaven: this sore travail hath God given to the sons of man to be exercised therewith. I have seen all the works that are done under the sun; and, behold, all is vanity and vexation of spirit.

passages.g: Some damaged things cannot be fixed; knowledge often frustrates rather than satisfies.
passages.text: That which is crooked cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbered. I communed with mine own heart, saying, Lo, I am come to great estate, and have gotten more wisdom than all they that have been before me in Jerusalem: yea, my heart had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.

passages.g: Greater knowledge increases suffering.
passages.text: And I gave my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly: I perceived that this also is vexation of spirit. For in much wisdom is much grief: and he that increaseth knowledge increaseth sorrow.

passages.g: Seeking pleasure is ultimately futile.
passages.text: I said in mine heart, Go to now, I will prove thee with mirth, therefore enjoy pleasure: and, behold, this also is vanity. I said of laughter, It is mad: and of mirth, What doeth it?

passages.g: Indulging in all desires and building monuments doesn't bring fulfillment.
passages.text: I sought in mine heart to give myself unto wine, yet acquainting mine heart with wisdom; and to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was that good for the sons of men, which they should do under the heaven all the days of their life. I made me great works; I builded me houses; I planted me vineyards:

passages.g: Accumulating wealth and possessions becomes an endless pursuit.
passages.text: I made me gardens and orchards, and I planted trees in them of all kind of fruits: I made me pools of water, to water therewith the wood that bringeth forth trees: I got me servants and maidens, and had servants born in my house; also I had great possessions of great and small cattle above all that were in Jerusalem before me:

passages.g: Acquiring everything yields some satisfaction only if wisdom accompanies it.
passages.text: I gathered me also silver and gold, and the peculiar treasure of kings and of the provinces: I gat me men singers and women singers, and the delights of the sons of men, as musical instruments, and that of all sorts. So I was great, and increased more than all that were before me in Jerusalem: also my wisdom remained with me.

passages.g: Finding joy in indulgence becomes the only reward for all labor.
passages.text: And whatsoever mine eyes desired I kept not from them, I withheld not my heart from any joy; for my heart rejoiced in all my labour: and this was my portion of all my labour.

passages.g: Even wisdom is meaningless since both the wise and foolish face the same end.
passages.text: Then I looked on all the works that my hands had wrought, and on the labour that I had laboured to do: and, behold, all was vanity and vexation of spirit, and there was no profit under the sun. And I turned myself to behold wisdom, and madness, and folly: for what can the man do that cometh after the king? even that which hath been already done.

passages.g: Wisdom is objectively superior to folly, yet both die the same way.
passages.text: Then I saw that wisdom excelleth folly, as far as light excelleth darkness. The wise man’s eyes are in his head; but the fool walketh in darkness: and I myself perceived also that one event happeneth to them all.

passages.g: Death makes wisdom's advantage pointless since both wise and foolish are equally forgotten.
passages.text: Then said I in my heart, As it happeneth to the fool, so it happeneth even to me; and why was I then more wise? Then I said in my heart, that this also is vanity. For there is no remembrance of the wise more than of the fool for ever; seeing that which now is in the days to come shall all be forgotten. And how dieth the wise man? as the fool.

passages.g: All labor produces only frustration and nothing of value.
passages.text: Therefore I hated life; because the work that is wrought under the sun is grievous unto me: for all is vanity and vexation of spirit. Yea, I hated all my labour which I had taken under the sun: because I should leave it unto the man that shall be after me.

passages.g: All your effort is pointless if you cannot control who benefits from it after you die.
passages.text: And who knoweth whether he shall be a wise man or a fool? yet shall he have rule over all my labour wherein I have laboured, and wherein I have shewed myself wise under the sun. This is also vanity. Therefore I went about to cause my heart to despair of all the labour which I took under the sun.

passages.g: Hard work often benefits only those who inherit it without having labored.
passages.text: For there is a man whose labour is in wisdom, and in knowledge, and in equity; yet to a man that hath not laboured therein shall he leave it for his portion. This also is vanity and a great evil. For what hath man of all his labour, and of the vexation of his heart, wherein he hath laboured under the sun?

passages.g: Simple contentment with food and work is life's only genuine good.
passages.text: For all his days are sorrows, and his travail grief; yea, his heart taketh not rest in the night. This is also vanity. There is nothing better for a man, than that he should eat and drink, and that he should make his soul enjoy good in his labour. This also I saw, that it was from the hand of God.

passages.g: Virtue sometimes brings joy while vice brings endless toil without reward.
passages.text: For who can eat, or who else can hasten hereunto, more than I? For God giveth to a man that is good in his sight wisdom, and knowledge, and joy: but to the sinner he giveth travail, to gather and to heap up, that he may give to him that is good before God. This also is vanity and vexation of spirit.

passages.g: Every activity has its proper time: birth and death, planting and harvest, weeping and laughter.
passages.text: To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven: A time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up that which is planted; A time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up; A time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;

passages.g: Every action has its opposite that is equally appropriate in its own season.
passages.text: A time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing; A time to get, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away; A time to rend, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak; A time to love, and a time to hate; a time of war, and a time of peace.

passages.g: Life's activities are beautiful and purposeful but ultimately incomprehensible; labor yields no lasting profit.
passages.text: What profit hath he that worketh in that wherein he laboureth? I have seen the travail, which God hath given to the sons of men to be exercised in it. He hath made every thing beautiful in his time: also he hath set the world in their heart, so that no man can find out the work that God maketh from the beginning to the end.

passages.g: The only good is to find joy and do good in your daily work and life.
passages.text: I know that there is no good in them, but for a man to rejoice, and to do good in his life. And also that every man should eat and drink, and enjoy the good of all his labour, it is the gift of God.

passages.g: Divine order is eternal and unchanging; history repeats itself.
passages.text: I know that, whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever: nothing can be put to it, nor any thing taken from it: and God doeth it, that men should fear before him. That which hath been is now; and that which is to be hath already been; and God requireth that which is past.

passages.g: Even judicial systems fail to maintain justice; God alone will judge fairly.
passages.text: And moreover I saw under the sun the place of judgment, that wickedness was there; and the place of righteousness, that iniquity was there. I said in mine heart, God shall judge the righteous and the wicked: for there is a time there for every purpose and for every work.

passages.g: Humans are essentially no different from animals.
passages.text: I said in mine heart concerning the estate of the sons of men, that God might manifest them, and that they might see that they themselves are beasts.

passages.g: Humans and animals die identically and return to dust equally.
passages.text: For that which befalleth the sons of men befalleth beasts; even one thing befalleth them: as the one dieth, so dieth the other; yea, they have all one breath; so that a man hath no preeminence above a beast: for all is vanity. All go unto one place; all are of the dust, and all turn to dust again.

passages.g: Human superiority over animals is unknowable; find joy in your own work instead.
passages.text: Who knoweth the spirit of man that goeth upward, and the spirit of the beast that goeth downward to the earth? Wherefore I perceive that there is nothing better, than that a man should rejoice in his own works; for that is his portion: for who shall bring him to see what shall be after him?

passages.g: The suffering of the oppressed is unbearable; non-existence is preferable to witnessing such evil.
passages.text: So I returned, and considered all the oppressions that are done under the sun: and behold the tears of such as were oppressed, and they had no comforter; and on the side of their oppressors there was power; but they had no comforter. Wherefore I praised the dead which are already dead more than the living which are yet alive.

passages.g: Non-existence is better than witnessing injustice; people envy each other's work.
passages.text: Yea, better is he than both they, which hath not yet been, who hath not seen the evil work that is done under the sun. Again, I considered all travail, and every right work, that for this a man is envied of his neighbour. This is also vanity and vexation of spirit. The fool foldeth his hands together, and eateth his own flesh.

passages.g: Modest peace is better than abundant struggle and anxiety.
passages.text: Better is an handful with quietness, than both the hands full with travail and vexation of spirit. Then I returned, and I saw vanity under the sun.

passages.g: Solitary pursuit of wealth is pointless and never brings satisfaction.
passages.text: There is one alone, and there is not a second; yea, he hath neither child nor brother: yet is there no end of all his labour; neither is his eye satisfied with riches; neither saith he, For whom do I labour, and bereave my soul of good? This is also vanity, yea, it is a sore travail.

passages.g: Partnership and companionship are better than solitude because partners help each other.
passages.text: Two are better than one; because they have a good reward for their labour. For if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow: but woe to him that is alone when he falleth; for he hath not another to help him up. Again, if two lie together, then they have heat: but how can one be warm alone?

passages.g: Cooperation and wisdom outweigh power and status; a wise youth beats a foolish king.
passages.text: And if one prevail against him, two shall withstand him; and a threefold cord is not quickly broken. Better is a poor and a wise child than an old and foolish king, who will no more be admonished. For out of prison he cometh to reign; whereas also he that is born in his kingdom becometh poor.

passages.g: Leadership and accomplishment fade into obscurity as people are forgotten.
passages.text: I considered all the living which walk under the sun, with the second child that shall stand up in his stead. There is no end of all the people, even of all that have been before them: they also that come after shall not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and vexation of spirit.

passages.g: In worship, listen humbly rather than speak carelessly; don't make hasty vows.
passages.text: Keep thy foot when thou goest to the house of God, and be more ready to hear, than to give the sacrifice of fools: for they consider not that they do evil. Be not rash with thy mouth, and let not thine heart be hasty to utter any thing before God: for God is in heaven, and thou upon earth: therefore let thy words be few.

passages.g: Many words and unfulfilled vows are foolish; better not to vow than to break your word.
passages.text: For a dream cometh through the multitude of business; and a fool’s voice is known by multitude of words. When thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it; for he hath no pleasure in fools: pay that which thou hast vowed. Better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay.

passages.g: Excessive talk risks causing sin before God; fear God instead of speaking much.
passages.text: Suffer not thy mouth to cause thy flesh to sin; neither say thou before the angel, that it was an error: wherefore should God be angry at thy voice, and destroy the work of thine hands? For in the multitude of dreams and many words there are also divers vanities: but fear thou God.

passages.g: Systemic oppression exists at multiple levels but ultimately serves everyone since the land's produce belongs to all.
passages.text: If thou seest the oppression of the poor, and violent perverting of judgment and justice in a province, marvel not at the matter: for he that is higher than the highest regardeth; and there be higher than they. Moreover the profit of the earth is for all: the king himself is served by the field.

passages.g: Greed for money is insatiable; wealth attracts those who consume it.
passages.text: He that loveth silver shall not be satisfied with silver; nor he that loveth abundance with increase: this is also vanity. When goods increase, they are increased that eat them: and what good is there to the owners thereof, saving the beholding of them with their eyes?

passages.g: Wealth causes anxiety and sleeplessness; riches easily perish or leave heirs nothing.
passages.text: The sleep of a labouring man is sweet, whether he eat little or much: but the abundance of the rich will not suffer him to sleep. There is a sore evil which I have seen under the sun, namely, riches kept for the owners thereof to their hurt. But those riches perish by evil travail: and he begetteth a son, and there is nothing in his hand.

passages.g: We leave the world as naked as we entered it; we cannot take our labor with us.
passages.text: As he came forth of his mother’s womb, naked shall he return to go as he came, and shall take nothing of his labour, which he may carry away in his hand. And this also is a sore evil, that in all points as he came, so shall he go: and what profit hath he that hath laboured for the wind?

passages.g: Simple contentment with food and work is the only real good in life.
passages.text: All his days also he eateth in darkness, and he hath much sorrow and wrath with his sickness. Behold that which I have seen: it is good and comely for one to eat and to drink, and to enjoy the good of all his labour that he taketh under the sun all the days of his life, which God giveth him: for it is his portion.

passages.g: The ability to enjoy your wealth is what truly matters; joy makes life pass unremembered.
passages.text: Every man also to whom God hath given riches and wealth, and hath given him power to eat thereof, and to take his portion, and to rejoice in his labour; this is the gift of God. For he shall not much remember the days of his life; because God answereth him in the joy of his heart.

passages.g: Possessing wealth you cannot use is a terrible curse worse than poverty.
passages.text: There is an evil which I have seen under the sun, and it is common among men: A man to whom God hath given riches, wealth, and honour, so that he wanteth nothing for his soul of all that he desireth, yet God giveth him not power to eat thereof, but a stranger eateth it: this is vanity, and it is an evil disease.

passages.g: Quantity of life means nothing without fulfillment and dignified death; non-existence is better.
passages.text: If a man beget an hundred children, and live many years, so that the days of his years be many, and his soul be not filled with good, and also that he have no burial; I say, that an untimely birth is better than he. For he cometh in with vanity, and departeth in darkness, and his name shall be covered with darkness.

passages.g: Long life brings no good if unfulfilled; work only maintains appetite without satisfying it.
passages.text: Moreover he hath not seen the sun, nor known any thing: this hath more rest than the other. Yea, though he live a thousand years twice told, yet hath he seen no good: do not all go to one place? All the labour of man is for his mouth, and yet the appetite is not filled.

passages.g: The wise and fool end equally; accepting what you see is better than endless craving.
passages.text: For what hath the wise more than the fool? what hath the poor, that knoweth to walk before the living? Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the desire: this is also vanity and vexation of spirit. That which hath been is named already, and it is known that it is man: neither may he contend with him that is mightier than he.

passages.g: Life is fleeting and obscure; we cannot truly know what is good for us.
passages.text: Seeing there be many things that increase vanity, what is man the better? For who knoweth what is good for man in this life, all the days of his vain life which he spendeth as a shadow? for who can tell a man what shall be after him under the sun?

passages.g: Reputation matters more than pleasure; reflection on death matters more than celebration.
passages.text: A good name is better than precious ointment; and the day of death than the day of one’s birth. It is better to go to the house of mourning, than to go to the house of feasting: for that is the end of all men; and the living will lay it to his heart. Sorrow is better than laughter: for by the sadness of the countenance the heart is made better.

passages.g: Wisdom lies in sobering reflection; harsh truth is better than empty flattery.
passages.text: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning; but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth. It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise, than for a man to hear the song of fools. For as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fool: this also is vanity.

passages.g: Injustice corrupts even the wise; patience and self-control are superior to pride and anger.
passages.text: Surely oppression maketh a wise man mad; and a gift destroyeth the heart. Better is the end of a thing than the beginning thereof: and the patient in spirit is better than the proud in spirit. Be not hasty in thy spirit to be angry: for anger resteth in the bosom of fools.

passages.g: Nostalgia for the past is unwise; wisdom provides better protection than wealth.
passages.text: Say not thou, What is the cause that the former days were better than these? for thou dost not enquire wisely concerning this. Wisdom is good with an inheritance: and by it there is profit to them that see the sun. For wisdom is a defence, and money is a defence: but the excellency of knowledge is, that wisdom giveth life to them that have it.

passages.g: We cannot undo the world's design; balance joy in good times with reflection in adversity.
passages.text: Consider the work of God: for who can make that straight, which he hath made crooked? In the day of prosperity be joyful, but in the day of adversity consider: God also hath set the one over against the other, to the end that man should find nothing after him.

passages.g: The world is unjust and excessive virtue will not protect you from suffering.
passages.text: All things have I seen in the days of my vanity: there is a just man that perisheth in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man that prolongeth his life in his wickedness. Be not righteous over much; neither make thyself over wise: why shouldest thou destroy thyself?

passages.g: Balance virtue with practicality; fearing God gives wisdom the strength to prevail.
passages.text: Be not over much wicked, neither be thou foolish: why shouldest thou die before thy time? It is good that thou shouldest take hold of this; yea, also from this withdraw not thine hand: for he that feareth God shall come forth of them all. Wisdom strengtheneth the wise more than ten mighty men which are in the city.

passages.g: Everyone acts wrongly sometimes, so you shouldn't judge others harshly, and real wisdom is extraordinarily difficult to attain.
passages.text: For there is not a just man upon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not. Also take no heed unto all words that are spoken; lest thou hear thy servant curse thee: For oftentimes also thine own heart knoweth that thou thyself likewise hast cursed others. All this have I proved by wisdom: I said, I will be wise; but it was far from me.

passages.g: Searching for deep understanding and truth about life's nature requires relentless investigation.
passages.text: That which is far off, and exceeding deep, who can find it out? I applied mine heart to know, and to search, and to seek out wisdom, and the reason of things, and to know the wickedness of folly, even of foolishness and madness:

passages.g: Some people are destructively manipulative, but those devoted to goodness can escape their traps.
passages.text: And I find more bitter than death the woman, whose heart is snares and nets, and her hands as bands: whoso pleaseth God shall escape from her; but the sinner shall be taken by her. Behold, this have I found, saith the preacher, counting one by one, to find out the account:

passages.g: Genuinely virtuous people are vanishingly rare, though humans are made capable of goodness.
passages.text: Which yet my soul seeketh, but I find not: one man among a thousand have I found; but a woman among all those have I not found. Lo, this only have I found, that God hath made man upright; but they have sought out many inventions.

passages.g: Wisdom conveys composure and authority, and intelligent obedience to legitimate power protects you.
passages.text: Who is as the wise man? and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing? a man’s wisdom maketh his face to shine, and the boldness of his face shall be changed. I counsel thee to keep the king’s commandment, and that in regard of the oath of God. Be not hasty to go out of his sight: stand not in an evil thing; for he doeth whatsoever pleaseth him.

passages.g: The powerful can act freely while others suffer, but wisdom lies in discerning the right moment to act.
passages.text: Where the word of a king is, there is power: and who may say unto him, What doest thou? Whoso keepeth the commandment shall feel no evil thing: and a wise man’s heart discerneth both time and judgment. Because to every purpose there is time and judgment, therefore the misery of man is great upon him.

passages.g: You cannot foresee or control your future, and death is inescapable regardless of your morality.
passages.text: For he knoweth not that which shall be: for who can tell him when it shall be? There is no man that hath power over the spirit to retain the spirit; neither hath he power in the day of death: and there is no discharge in that war; neither shall wickedness deliver those that are given to it.

passages.g: Power corrupts those who wield it, and even notorious people fade from memory.
passages.text: All this have I seen, and applied my heart unto every work that is done under the sun: there is a time wherein one man ruleth over another to his own hurt. And so I saw the wicked buried, who had come and gone from the place of the holy, and they were forgotten in the city where they had so done: this is also vanity.

passages.g: When wrongdoing goes unpunished, people persist in it, yet those who do right ultimately prosper.
passages.text: Because sentence against an evil work is not executed speedily, therefore the heart of the sons of men is fully set in them to do evil. Though a sinner do evil an hundred times, and his days be prolonged, yet surely I know that it shall be well with them that fear God, which fear before him:

passages.g: People who ignore moral law won't prosper or live long.
passages.text: But it shall not be well with the wicked, neither shall he prolong his days, which are as a shadow; because he feareth not before God.

passages.g: Injustice pervades reality—the virtuous sometimes suffer as the wicked do, while the wicked sometimes flourish as the virtuous would.
passages.text: There is a vanity which is done upon the earth; that there be just men, unto whom it happeneth according to the work of the wicked; again, there be wicked men, to whom it happeneth according to the work of the righteous: I said that this also is vanity.

passages.g: The best available good in life is taking pleasure in eating, drinking, and enjoying your work.
passages.text: Then I commended mirth, because a man hath no better thing under the sun, than to eat, and to drink, and to be merry: for that shall abide with him of his labour the days of his life, which God giveth him under the sun.

passages.g: The search for wisdom can rob you of sleep and peace.
passages.text: When I applied mine heart to know wisdom, and to see the business that is done upon the earth: (for also there is that neither day nor night seeth sleep with his eyes:)

passages.g: Human effort cannot fully understand how reality and the world actually work.
passages.text: Then I beheld all the work of God, that a man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun: because though a man labour to seek it out, yet he shall not find it; yea farther; though a wise man think to know it, yet shall he not be able to find it.

passages.g: Your fate and moral standing are ultimately beyond your judgment and in divine hands.
passages.text: For all this I considered in my heart even to declare all this, that the righteous, and the wise, and their works, are in the hand of God: no man knoweth either love or hatred by all that is before them.

passages.g: Death treats everyone identically—the good and bad, the righteous and unrighteous all face the same end.
passages.text: All things come alike to all: there is one event to the righteous, and to the wicked; to the good and to the clean, and to the unclean; to him that sacrificeth, and to him that sacrificeth not: as is the good, so is the sinner; and he that sweareth, as he that feareth an oath.

passages.g: Everyone faces certain death despite their virtue; humans naturally harbor malice, yet consciousness itself exceeds oblivion.
passages.text: This is an evil among all things that are done under the sun, that there is one event unto all: yea, also the heart of the sons of men is full of evil, and madness is in their heart while they live, and after that they go to the dead. For to him that is joined to all the living there is hope: for a living dog is better than a dead lion.

passages.g: Death brings total unconsciousness and oblivion—you lose all stake in future events and your memory fades.
passages.text: For the living know that they shall die: but the dead know not any thing, neither have they any more a reward; for the memory of them is forgotten. Also their love, and their hatred, and their envy, is now perished; neither have they any more a portion for ever in any thing that is done under the sun.

passages.g: Accept and delight in life's simple goods—nourishment, drink, and grooming—as legitimate pleasure.
passages.text: Go thy way, eat thy bread with joy, and drink thy wine with a merry heart; for God now accepteth thy works. Let thy garments be always white; and let thy head lack no ointment.

passages.g: Romantic love and partnership are your rightful portion and reward in life.
passages.text: Live joyfully with the wife whom thou lovest all the days of the life of thy vanity, which he hath given thee under the sun, all the days of thy vanity: for that is thy portion in this life, and in thy labour which thou takest under the sun.

passages.g: Do your work with all your might while alive, because death ends all action and possibility.
passages.text: Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might; for there is no work, nor device, nor knowledge, nor wisdom, in the grave, whither thou goest.

passages.g: Talent and effort don't guarantee success—circumstance and timing override ability.
passages.text: I returned, and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, neither yet bread to the wise, nor yet riches to men of understanding, nor yet favour to men of skill; but time and chance happeneth to them all.

passages.g: You're trapped by fate as fish by nets, and disaster strikes without warning.
passages.text: For man also knoweth not his time: as the fishes that are taken in an evil net, and as the birds that are caught in the snare; so are the sons of men snared in an evil time, when it falleth suddenly upon them. This wisdom have I seen also under the sun, and it seemed great unto me:

passages.g: Wisdom can rescue communities, but society neglects to honor those without wealth and status.
passages.text: There was a little city, and few men within it; and there came a great king against it, and besieged it, and built great bulwarks against it: Now there was found in it a poor wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city; yet no man remembered that same poor man.

passages.g: Wisdom outweighs power but is scorned when it lacks resources; a small failure destroys a hard-won reputation for competence.
passages.text: Then said I, Wisdom is better than strength: nevertheless the poor man’s wisdom is despised, and his words are not heard. The words of wise men are heard in quiet more than the cry of him that ruleth among fools. Wisdom is better than weapons of war: but one sinner destroyeth much good.

passages.g: A single foolish act can ruin the wise person's reputation, and fools inevitably expose themselves through their behavior.
passages.text: Dead flies cause the ointment of the apothecary to send forth a stinking savour: so doth a little folly him that is in reputation for wisdom and honour. A wise man’s heart is at his right hand; but a fool’s heart at his left. Yea also, when he that is a fool walketh by the way, his wisdom faileth him, and he saith to every one that he is a fool.

passages.g: Don't abandon your position when authorities are angry, and social order often inverts with the unfit in power.
passages.text: If the spirit of the ruler rise up against thee, leave not thy place; for yielding pacifieth great offences. There is an evil which I have seen under the sun, as an error which proceedeth from the ruler: Folly is set in great dignity, and the rich sit in low place. I have seen servants upon horses, and princes walking as servants upon the earth.

passages.g: Careless actions produce proportional harm, while wise planning prevents wasted effort and danger.
passages.text: He that diggeth a pit shall fall into it; and whoso breaketh an hedge, a serpent shall bite him. Whoso removeth stones shall be hurt therewith; and he that cleaveth wood shall be endangered thereby. If the iron be blunt, and he do not whet the edge, then must he put to more strength: but wisdom is profitable to direct.

passages.g: Careless speech is as deadly as snakes; the wise speak with grace while fools destroy themselves through words.
passages.text: Surely the serpent will bite without enchantment; and a babbler is no better. The words of a wise man’s mouth are gracious; but the lips of a fool will swallow up himself. The beginning of the words of his mouth is foolishness: and the end of his talk is mischievous madness.

passages.g: Pointless talking wastes everyone's energy and accomplishes nothing, and immature or dissolute leadership damages society.
passages.text: A fool also is full of words: a man cannot tell what shall be; and what shall be after him, who can tell him? The labour of the foolish wearieth every one of them, because he knoweth not how to go to the city. Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child, and thy princes eat in the morning!

passages.g: Competent, temperate leadership strengthens society; negligence ruins it; while merriment is valid, money ultimately answers practical needs.
passages.text: Blessed art thou, O land, when thy king is the son of nobles, and thy princes eat in due season, for strength, and not for drunkenness! By much slothfulness the building decayeth; and through idleness of the hands the house droppeth through. A feast is made for laughter, and wine maketh merry: but money answereth all things.

passages.g: Criticize the powerful only in private, as gossip spreads beyond your control.
passages.text: Curse not the king, no not in thy thought; and curse not the rich in thy bedchamber: for a bird of the air shall carry the voice, and that which hath wings shall tell the matter.

passages.g: Give generously to many because you don't know what hardship will force you to need others' help.
passages.text: Cast thy bread upon the waters: for thou shalt find it after many days. Give a portion to seven, and also to eight; for thou knowest not what evil shall be upon the earth.

passages.g: Nature unfolds according to its own logic, and excessive caution prevents necessary action.
passages.text: If the clouds be full of rain, they empty themselves upon the earth: and if the tree fall toward the south, or toward the north, in the place where the tree falleth, there it shall be. He that observeth the wind shall not sow; and he that regardeth the clouds shall not reap.

passages.g: Just as human development in the womb is a mystery to you, so are God's works—accept what you cannot understand.
passages.text: As thou knowest not what is the way of the spirit, nor how the bones do grow in the womb of her that is with child: even so thou knowest not the works of God who maketh all.

passages.g: Attempt multiple approaches since you can't predict which will succeed, and cherish good times knowing suffering will return.
passages.text: In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening withhold not thine hand: for thou knowest not whether shall prosper, either this or that, or whether they both shall be alike good. Truly the light is sweet, and a pleasant thing it is for the eyes to behold the sun:

passages.g: Even a long and happy life is brief before death and ultimately empty.
passages.text: But if a man live many years, and rejoice in them all; yet let him remember the days of darkness; for they shall be many. All that cometh is vanity.

passages.g: Pursue youthful desires freely but know that all your actions face final judgment.
passages.text: Rejoice, O young man, in thy youth; and let thy heart cheer thee in the days of thy youth, and walk in the ways of thine heart, and in the sight of thine eyes: but know thou, that for all these things God will bring thee into judgment. Therefore remove sorrow from thy heart, and put away evil from thy flesh: for childhood and youth are vanity.

passages.g: Build spiritual grounding while young, before age and decline make it harder.
passages.text: Remember now thy Creator in the days of thy youth, while the evil days come not, nor the years draw nigh, when thou shalt say, I have no pleasure in them; While the sun, or the light, or the moon, or the stars, be not darkened, nor the clouds return after the rain:

passages.g: Your hands tremble and your strength weakens as you age.
passages.text: In the day when the keepers of the house shall tremble, and the strong men shall bow themselves, and the grinders cease because they are few, and those that look out of the windows be darkened,

passages.g: Your hearing fails, you wake at the slightest sound, and your voice becomes feeble.
passages.text: And the doors shall be shut in the streets, when the sound of the grinding is low, and he shall rise up at the voice of the bird, and all the daughters of musick shall be brought low;

passages.g: Fear and physical decline increase with age, vigor fades, desire ceases, and death approaches.
passages.text: Also when they shall be afraid of that which is high, and fears shall be in the way, and the almond tree shall flourish, and the grasshopper shall be a burden, and desire shall fail: because man goeth to his long home, and the mourners go about the streets:

passages.g: Your body decays and returns to earth while your spirit returns to God; everything is temporary.
passages.text: Or ever the silver cord be loosed, or the golden bowl be broken, or the pitcher be broken at the fountain, or the wheel broken at the cistern. Then shall the dust return to the earth as it was: and the spirit shall return unto God who gave it. Vanity of vanities, saith the preacher; all is vanity.

passages.g: The wise teacher collects and communicates true knowledge to educate people.
passages.text: And moreover, because the preacher was wise, he still taught the people knowledge; yea, he gave good heed, and sought out, and set in order many proverbs. The preacher sought to find out acceptable words: and that which was written was upright, even words of truth.

passages.g: Wisdom cuts sharply and sticks in memory through authoritative teaching, though endless learning wears out the body.
passages.text: The words of the wise are as goads, and as nails fastened by the masters of assemblies, which are given from one shepherd. And further, by these, my son, be admonished: of making many books there is no end; and much study is a weariness of the flesh.

passages.g: Your life's ultimate duty is to revere God and keep his laws, knowing all will be judged.
passages.text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil.

passages.g: Spiritual blessing comes through humility, grief over wrongdoing, gentleness, hunger for justice, and mercy.
passages.text: And seeing the multitudes, he went up into a mountain: and when he was set, his disciples came unto him: And he opened his mouth, and taught them, saying, Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted.

passages.g: Peacemaking and suffering wrongly for righteousness bring spiritual vindication and divine blessing.
passages.text: Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled. Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God.

passages.g: Celebrate when persecuted for faithfulness, as you join history's spiritual exemplars.
passages.text: Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God. Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness’ sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.

passages.g: Rejoice when persecuted and reviled for righteousness, as great spiritual reward awaits you.
passages.text: Blessed are ye, when men shall revile you, and persecute you, and shall say all manner of evil against you falsely, for my sake. Rejoice, and be exceeding glad: for great is your reward in heaven: for so persecuted they the prophets which were before you.

passages.g: You embody goodness and must display it openly so others witness virtue and are drawn toward it.
passages.text: Ye are the salt of the earth: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be salted? it is thenceforth good for nothing, but to be cast out, and to be trodden under foot of men. Ye are the light of the world. A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid.

passages.g: Live your values visibly so your good actions guide others toward what's right and good.
passages.text: Neither do men light a candle, and put it under a bushel, but on a candlestick; and it giveth light unto all that are in the house. Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.

passages.g: I come to fulfill rather than reject the former moral law.
passages.text: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil. For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled.

passages.g: Spiritual rank depends on both practicing and teaching moral law.
passages.text: Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called the least in the kingdom of heaven: but whosoever shall do and teach them, the same shall be called great in the kingdom of heaven.

passages.g: True righteousness exceeds surface compliance and rule-following.
passages.text: For I say unto you, That except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven. Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not kill; and whosoever shall kill shall be in danger of the judgment:

passages.g: Unjust anger toward others is morally equivalent to murder; contempt and insult are gravely culpable.
passages.text: But I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment: and whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council: but whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire.

passages.g: Make peace with those you've wronged before observing ritual or making offerings.
passages.text: Therefore if thou bring thy gift to the altar, and there rememberest that thy brother hath ought against thee; Leave there thy gift before the altar, and go thy way; first be reconciled to thy brother, and then come and offer thy gift.

passages.g: Settle disputes quickly or you'll face escalating legal punishment and imprisonment.
passages.text: Agree with thine adversary quickly, whiles thou art in the way with him; lest at any time the adversary deliver thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and thou be cast into prison.

passages.g: Repay your debts completely or remain trapped in obligation; internal desire for another's spouse betrays commitment.
passages.text: Verily I say unto thee, Thou shalt by no means come out thence, till thou hast paid the uttermost farthing. Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not commit adultery:

passages.g: Desiring another's spouse sexually is as much a betrayal of commitment as the physical act.
passages.text: But I say unto you, That whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart.

passages.g: It's better to sacrifice something valuable than to allow it to destroy you.
passages.text: And if thy right eye offend thee, pluck it out, and cast it from thee: for it is profitable for thee that one of thy members should perish, and not that thy whole body should be cast into hell.

passages.g: Cut away whatever causes you to sin; marriage has strict rules and shouldn't be dissolved lightly.
passages.text: And if thy right hand offend thee, cut it off, and cast it from thee: for it is profitable for thee that one of thy members should perish, and not that thy whole body should be cast into hell. It hath been said, Whosoever shall put away his wife, let him give her a writing of divorcement:

passages.g: Divorce without infidelity as cause, and remarriage to a divorced person, are both serious wrongs.
passages.text: But I say unto you, That whosoever shall put away his wife, saving for the cause of fornication, causeth her to commit adultery: and whosoever shall marry her that is divorced committeth adultery.

passages.g: Never swear oaths or invoke sacred things to back up your words.
passages.text: Again, ye have heard that it hath been said by them of old time, Thou shalt not forswear thyself, but shalt perform unto the Lord thine oaths: But I say unto you, Swear not at all; neither by heaven; for it is God’s throne:

passages.g: Plain yes or no is all that's honest; anything more comes from dishonesty.
passages.text: Nor by the earth; for it is his footstool: neither by Jerusalem; for it is the city of the great King. Neither shalt thou swear by thy head, because thou canst not make one hair white or black. But let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay: for whatsoever is more than these cometh of evil.

passages.g: Don't retaliate when wronged; instead endure further harm and give more generously.
passages.text: Ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth: But I say unto you, That ye resist not evil: but whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also. And if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloak also.

passages.g: Give generously to anyone who asks and help those who demand your service.
passages.text: And whosoever shall compel thee to go a mile, go with him twain. Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn not thou away. Ye have heard that it hath been said, Thou shalt love thy neighbour, and hate thine enemy.

passages.g: Love your enemies and pray for those who harm you.
passages.text: But I say unto you, Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you;

passages.g: God treats everyone equally; loving only those who love you is unremarkable.
