passages.text: That ye may be the children of your Father which is in heaven: for he maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust. For if ye love them which love you, what reward have ye? do not even the publicans the same?

passages.g: Being kind only to your own group isn't special; aim to be as impartial as God.
passages.text: And if ye salute your brethren only, what do ye more than others? do not even the publicans so? Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect.

passages.g: Don't give charity publicly for recognition.
passages.text: Take heed that ye do not your alms before men, to be seen of them: otherwise ye have no reward of your Father which is in heaven.

passages.g: Keep charity secret; those who give publicly have already received their only reward.
passages.text: Therefore when thou doest thine alms, do not sound a trumpet before thee, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, that they may have glory of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward. But when thou doest alms, let not thy left hand know what thy right hand doeth:

passages.g: Secret charity receives genuine reward from God.
passages.text: That thine alms may be in secret: and thy Father which seeth in secret himself shall reward thee openly.

passages.g: Don't pray publicly for attention; it brings only public recognition, nothing more.
passages.text: And when thou prayest, thou shalt not be as the hypocrites are: for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and in the corners of the streets, that they may be seen of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

passages.g: Pray alone in private; God sees and will reward you genuinely.
passages.text: But thou, when thou prayest, enter into thy closet, and when thou hast shut thy door, pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly.

passages.g: Meaningless repetition in prayer doesn't work; God knows your needs before you ask.
passages.text: But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking. Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him.

passages.g: Prayer involves honoring God, requesting His kingdom to come, asking for daily needs, seeking forgiveness, and requesting protection from temptation.
passages.text: After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come, Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.

passages.g: You must forgive others if you want God to forgive you.
passages.text: And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil: For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen. For if ye forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you:

passages.g: If you don't forgive others, God won't forgive you; don't publicly display your self-denial.
passages.text: But if ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses. Moreover when ye fast, be not, as the hypocrites, of a sad countenance: for they disfigure their faces, that they may appear unto men to fast. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

passages.g: Keep your self-denial hidden rather than making it visible for admiration.
passages.text: But thou, when thou fastest, anoint thine head, and wash thy face; That thou appear not unto men to fast, but unto thy Father which is in secret: and thy Father, which seeth in secret, shall reward thee openly.

passages.g: Don't accumulate material wealth; focus on spiritual development instead.
passages.text: Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal: But lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal:

passages.g: What you prioritize (money or God) determines your values; single-minded focus produces clarity.
passages.text: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also. The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light.

passages.g: If your focus is corrupted, you live in moral blindness.
passages.text: But if thine eye be evil, thy whole body shall be full of darkness. If therefore the light that is in thee be darkness, how great is that darkness!

passages.g: You can't serve both God and money equally; choose one as your primary loyalty.
passages.text: No man can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.

passages.g: Stop worrying about basic survival needs; life itself is more important than possessions.
passages.text: Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink; nor yet for your body, what ye shall put on. Is not the life more than meat, and the body than raiment?

passages.g: Wild creatures don't work but are provided for; you're worth more than them, and worry doesn't help.
passages.text: Behold the fowls of the air: for they sow not, neither do they reap, nor gather into barns; yet your heavenly Father feedeth them. Are ye not much better than they? Which of you by taking thought can add one cubit unto his stature?

passages.g: Wildflowers don't labor yet are more beautifully clothed than kings; don't worry about clothing.
passages.text: And why take ye thought for raiment? Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither do they spin: And yet I say unto you, That even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.

passages.g: If God decorates temporary plants so beautifully, He will clothe you; stop worrying about survival.
passages.text: Wherefore, if God so clothe the grass of the field, which to day is, and to morrow is cast into the oven, shall he not much more clothe you, O ye of little faith? Therefore take no thought, saying, What shall we eat? or, What shall we drink? or, Wherewithal shall we be clothed?

passages.g: Seek God's kingdom first and your material needs will follow.
passages.text: (For after all these things do the Gentiles seek:) for your heavenly Father knoweth that ye have need of all these things. But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.

passages.g: Don't worry about tomorrow; today has enough problems.
passages.text: Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof.

passages.g: Don't judge others because you'll be judged by the same standard; fix your own faults first.
passages.text: Judge not, that ye be not judged. For with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again. And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but considerest not the beam that is in thine own eye?

passages.g: Stop being a hypocrite—fix your own serious flaws before criticizing someone else's minor ones.
passages.text: Or how wilt thou say to thy brother, Let me pull out the mote out of thine eye; and, behold, a beam is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, first cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother’s eye.

passages.g: Don't offer sacred things to those who won't respect them; persistence in asking, seeking, and knocking brings results.
passages.text: Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine, lest they trample them under their feet, and turn again and rend you. Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you:

passages.g: Asking brings receiving, seeking brings finding, knocking brings doors opening; even imperfect parents give good things to their children.
passages.text: For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened. Or what man is there of you, whom if his son ask bread, will he give him a stone? Or if he ask a fish, will he give him a serpent?

passages.g: Even flawed people give their children good things; God gives better to those who ask; treat others as you want to be treated.
passages.text: If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask him? Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets.

passages.g: The difficult path leads to life, the easy path to destruction; most take the easy path.
passages.text: Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and many there be which go in thereat: Because strait is the gate, and narrow is the way, which leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it.

passages.g: Beware of false teachers who look respectable but cause harm; you know them by their results.
passages.text: Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep’s clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves. Ye shall know them by their fruits. Do men gather grapes of thorns, or figs of thistles? Even so every good tree bringeth forth good fruit; but a corrupt tree bringeth forth evil fruit.

passages.g: Good nature can't produce bad results and bad nature can't produce good; harmful people are ultimately destroyed.
passages.text: A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit, neither can a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit. Every tree that bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire. Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them.

passages.g: Just claiming to follow God doesn't grant entry; only those who obey God's will gain entry.
passages.text: Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom of heaven; but he that doeth the will of my Father which is in heaven.

passages.g: People will claim religious accomplishments, but God rejects those who acted wrongly despite their claims.
passages.text: Many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in thy name? and in thy name have cast out devils? and in thy name done many wonderful works? And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity.

passages.g: Those who hear and follow these teachings are like those building on rock—they survive any crisis.
passages.text: Therefore whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, I will liken him unto a wise man, which built his house upon a rock: And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell not: for it was founded upon a rock.

passages.g: Those who hear but don't follow are like those building on sand—they collapse under stress.
passages.text: And every one that heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a foolish man, which built his house upon the sand: And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell: and great was the fall of it.

passages.g: People were amazed by his teaching because he spoke with actual authority, unlike conventional teachers.
passages.text: And it came to pass, when Jesus had ended these sayings, the people were astonished at his doctrine: For he taught them as one having authority, and not as the scribes.

passages.g: A teacher gathers a large audience to share wisdom through stories.
passages.text: The same day went Jesus out of the house, and sat by the sea side. And great multitudes were gathered together unto him, so that he went into a ship, and sat; and the whole multitude stood on the shore. And he spake many things unto them in parables, saying, Behold, a sower went forth to sow;

passages.g: The receptiveness of the ground determines whether seeds will germinate and grow.
passages.text: And when he sowed, some seeds fell by the way side, and the fowls came and devoured them up: Some fell upon stony places, where they had not much earth: and forthwith they sprung up, because they had no deepness of earth:

passages.g: The same teaching produces different outcomes depending on how deeply people receive and retain it.
passages.text: And when the sun was up, they were scorched; and because they had no root, they withered away. And some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprung up, and choked them: But other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some sixtyfold, some thirtyfold.

passages.g: Understanding requires openness; those without receptivity won't comprehend even when exposed to truth.
passages.text: Who hath ears to hear, let him hear. And the disciples came, and said unto him, Why speakest thou unto them in parables? He answered and said unto them, Because it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given.

passages.g: Those ready to understand gain greater insight; those closed off lose even what they have.
passages.text: For whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and he shall have more abundance: but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath. Therefore speak I to them in parables: because they seeing see not; and hearing they hear not, neither do they understand.

passages.g: People with hardened hearts perceive truth but refuse to understand it.
passages.text: And in them is fulfilled the prophecy of Esaias, which saith, By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and shall not perceive:

passages.g: People deliberately close themselves to truth to avoid the discomfort of change.
passages.text: For this people’s heart is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes they have closed; lest at any time they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears, and should understand with their heart, and should be converted, and I should heal them.

passages.g: Direct access to truth is rare and precious, something the righteous of previous eras longed for.
passages.text: But blessed are your eyes, for they see: and your ears, for they hear. For verily I say unto you, That many prophets and righteous men have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them.

passages.g: Without retention of understanding, external influences easily destroy what was initially received.
passages.text: Hear ye therefore the parable of the sower. When any one heareth the word of the kingdom, and understandeth it not, then cometh the wicked one, and catcheth away that which was sown in his heart. This is he which received seed by the way side.

passages.g: Belief without deep roots collapses when faced with difficulty or opposition.
passages.text: But he that received the seed into stony places, the same is he that heareth the word, and anon with joy receiveth it; Yet hath he not root in himself, but dureth for a while: for when tribulation or persecution ariseth because of the word, by and by he is offended.

passages.g: Preoccupation with worldly concerns prevents spiritual development.
passages.text: He also that received seed among the thorns is he that heareth the word; and the care of this world, and the deceitfulness of riches, choke the word, and he becometh unfruitful.

passages.g: Understanding combined with receptivity produces abundant results.
passages.text: But he that received seed into the good ground is he that heareth the word, and understandeth it; which also beareth fruit, and bringeth forth, some an hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.

passages.g: Good work can be deliberately undermined by those opposed to it.
passages.text: Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field: But while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way.

passages.g: Corruption becomes visible only after growth has begun.
passages.text: But when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also. So the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? from whence then hath it tares?

passages.g: Attempting to forcibly separate good from bad causes damage to what you're trying to preserve.
passages.text: He said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said unto him, Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up? But he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them.

passages.g: It is better to let good and bad coexist until natural judgment separates them.
passages.text: Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn.

passages.g: Something small can develop into something vastly larger.
passages.text: Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is like to a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and sowed in his field:

passages.g: Small beginnings can grow into something large and influential.
passages.text: Which indeed is the least of all seeds: but when it is grown, it is the greatest among herbs, and becometh a tree, so that the birds of the air come and lodge in the branches thereof.

passages.g: A small influence permeates and transforms the whole.
passages.text: Another parable spake he unto them; The kingdom of heaven is like unto leaven, which a woman took, and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened. All these things spake Jesus unto the multitude in parables; and without a parable spake he not unto them:

passages.g: Truth reveals what was previously hidden.
passages.text: That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, saying, I will open my mouth in parables; I will utter things which have been kept secret from the foundation of the world.

passages.g: Understanding the source of a problem helps explain its nature.
passages.text: Then Jesus sent the multitude away, and went into the house: and his disciples came unto him, saying, Declare unto us the parable of the tares of the field. He answered and said unto them, He that soweth the good seed is the Son of man;

passages.g: Good and evil will eventually be separated and judged according to their nature.
passages.text: The field is the world; the good seed are the children of the kingdom; but the tares are the children of the wicked one; The enemy that sowed them is the devil; the harvest is the end of the world; and the reapers are the angels.

passages.g: Those who commit wrong face judgment and separation.
passages.text: As therefore the tares are gathered and burned in the fire; so shall it be in the end of this world. The Son of man shall send forth his angels, and they shall gather out of his kingdom all things that offend, and them which do iniquity;

passages.g: The righteous are vindicated while the wicked face punishment.
passages.text: And shall cast them into a furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth. Then shall the righteous shine forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father. Who hath ears to hear, let him hear.

passages.g: Spiritual truth is worth surrendering all material possessions and status for.
passages.text: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto treasure hid in a field; the which when a man hath found, he hideth, and for joy thereof goeth and selleth all that he hath, and buyeth that field. Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a merchant man, seeking goodly pearls:

passages.g: Finding something of great value justifies total sacrifice of lesser goods.
passages.text: Who, when he had found one pearl of great price, went and sold all that he had, and bought it. Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a net, that was cast into the sea, and gathered of every kind:

passages.g: Eventually good and bad are separated and judged differently.
passages.text: Which, when it was full, they drew to shore, and sat down, and gathered the good into vessels, but cast the bad away. So shall it be at the end of the world: the angels shall come forth, and sever the wicked from among the just,

passages.g: Judgment brings final separation and irreversible consequence.
passages.text: And shall cast them into the furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth. Jesus saith unto them, Have ye understood all these things? They say unto him, Yea, Lord.

passages.g: One skilled in spiritual truth integrates both new insights and established wisdom.
passages.text: Then said he unto them, Therefore every scribe which is instructed unto the kingdom of heaven is like unto a man that is an householder, which bringeth forth out of his treasure things new and old. And it came to pass, that when Jesus had finished these parables, he departed thence.

passages.g: People struggle to recognize extraordinary ability in those they know through ordinary circumstances.
passages.text: And when he was come into his own country, he taught them in their synagogue, insomuch that they were astonished, and said, Whence hath this man this wisdom, and these mighty works?

passages.g: Ordinary family origins make extraordinary claims seem implausible.
passages.text: Is not this the carpenter’s son? is not his mother called Mary? and his brethren, James, and Joses, and Simon, and Judas? And his sisters, are they not all with us? Whence then hath this man all these things?

passages.g: Familiarity and skepticism prevent people from receiving truth from those they know.
passages.text: And they were offended in him. But Jesus said unto them, A prophet is not without honour, save in his own country, and in his own house. And he did not many mighty works there because of their unbelief.

passages.g: True greatness is not what people naturally assume.
passages.text: At the same time came the disciples unto Jesus, saying, Who is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven? And Jesus called a little child unto him, and set him in the midst of them,

passages.g: Humility and openness like a child's are necessary to enter the spiritual kingdom.
passages.text: And said, Verily I say unto you, Except ye be converted, and become as little children, ye shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven. Whosoever therefore shall humble himself as this little child, the same is greatest in the kingdom of heaven.

passages.g: How you treat vulnerable believers has profound moral weight; harming them is gravely wrong.
passages.text: And whoso shall receive one such little child in my name receiveth me. But whoso shall offend one of these little ones which believe in me, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea.

passages.g: Conflict is inevitable, but those who cause it bear moral responsibility.
passages.text: Woe unto the world because of offences! for it must needs be that offences come; but woe to that man by whom the offence cometh!

passages.g: Eliminate sources of your own wrongdoing rather than preserve yourself while sinning.
passages.text: Wherefore if thy hand or thy foot offend thee, cut them off, and cast them from thee: it is better for thee to enter into life halt or maimed, rather than having two hands or two feet to be cast into everlasting fire.

passages.g: Radical self-correction is preferable to compromised existence.
passages.text: And if thine eye offend thee, pluck it out, and cast it from thee: it is better for thee to enter into life with one eye, rather than having two eyes to be cast into hell fire.

passages.g: There is profound value in seeking and saving even one lost person.
passages.text: Take heed that ye despise not one of these little ones; for I say unto you, That in heaven their angels do always behold the face of my Father which is in heaven. For the Son of man is come to save that which was lost.

passages.g: Individual worth is significant enough to warrant abandoning the many to save the one.
passages.text: How think ye? if a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray, doth he not leave the ninety and nine, and goeth into the mountains, and seeketh that which is gone astray?

passages.g: Finding a lost person brings greater joy than keeping what you already safely possess.
passages.text: And if so be that he find it, verily I say unto you, he rejoiceth more of that sheep, than of the ninety and nine which went not astray. Even so it is not the will of your Father which is in heaven, that one of these little ones should perish.

passages.g: Address wrongs privately first; involve others only if that fails.
passages.text: Moreover if thy brother shall trespass against thee, go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone: if he shall hear thee, thou hast gained thy brother. But if he will not hear thee, then take with thee one or two more, that in the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established.

passages.g: As a last resort, separate from those who refuse correction.
passages.text: And if he shall neglect to hear them, tell it unto the church: but if he neglect to hear the church, let him be unto thee as an heathen man and a publican.

passages.g: Collective decisions have binding authority.
passages.text: Verily I say unto you, Whatsoever ye shall bind on earth shall be bound in heaven: and whatsoever ye shall loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven.

passages.g: Unified intention among people has power; genuine agreement creates spiritual presence.
passages.text: Again I say unto you, That if two of you shall agree on earth as touching any thing that they shall ask, it shall be done for them of my Father which is in heaven. For where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them.

passages.g: Forgiveness should be unlimited and habitual, not rationed.
passages.text: Then came Peter to him, and said, Lord, how oft shall my brother sin against me, and I forgive him? till seven times? Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times: but, Until seventy times seven.

passages.g: A reckoning comes when accounts must be settled.
passages.text: Therefore is the kingdom of heaven likened unto a certain king, which would take account of his servants. And when he had begun to reckon, one was brought unto him, which owed him ten thousand talents.

passages.g: Desperation drives promises of repayment.
passages.text: But forasmuch as he had not to pay, his lord commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and children, and all that he had, and payment to be made. The servant therefore fell down, and worshipped him, saying, Lord, have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.

passages.g: Forgive others as abundantly as you have been forgiven.
passages.text: Then the lord of that servant was moved with compassion, and loosed him, and forgave him the debt. But the same servant went out, and found one of his fellowservants, which owed him an hundred pence: and he laid hands on him, and took him by the throat, saying, Pay me that thou owest.

passages.g: Someone forgiven can still refuse forgiveness to others.
passages.text: And his fellowservant fell down at his feet, and besought him, saying, Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all. And he would not: but went and cast him into prison, till he should pay the debt.

passages.g: Having received forgiveness, one is obligated to extend it to others.
passages.text: So when his fellowservants saw what was done, they were very sorry, and came and told unto their lord all that was done. Then his lord, after that he had called him, said unto him, O thou wicked servant, I forgave thee all that debt, because thou desiredst me:

passages.g: If you receive forgiveness for great wrongs, you must extend it to others.
passages.text: Shouldest not thou also have had compassion on thy fellowservant, even as I had pity on thee? And his lord was wroth, and delivered him to the tormentors, till he should pay all that was due unto him.

passages.g: Failure to forgive others undermines your own forgiveness.
passages.text: So likewise shall my heavenly Father do also unto you, if ye from your hearts forgive not every one his brother their trespasses.

passages.g: Preparation and foresight determine readiness for critical moments.
passages.text: Then shall the kingdom of heaven be likened unto ten virgins, which took their lamps, and went forth to meet the bridegroom. And five of them were wise, and five were foolish. They that were foolish took their lamps, and took no oil with them: But the wise took oil in their vessels with their lamps.

passages.g: You cannot borrow others' preparedness to compensate for your own failure to prepare.
passages.text: While the bridegroom tarried, they all slumbered and slept. And at midnight there was a cry made, Behold, the bridegroom cometh; go ye out to meet him. Then all those virgins arose, and trimmed their lamps. And the foolish said unto the wise, Give us of your oil; for our lamps are gone out.

passages.g: Those unprepared when opportunity arrives miss it permanently.
passages.text: But the wise answered, saying, Not so; lest there be not enough for us and you: but go ye rather to them that sell, and buy for yourselves. And while they went to buy, the bridegroom came; and they that were ready went in with him to the marriage: and the door was shut.

passages.g: Missing your moment carries irreversible consequences; vigilance is required.
passages.text: Afterward came also the other virgins, saying, Lord, Lord, open to us. But he answered and said, Verily I say unto you, I know you not. Watch therefore, for ye know neither the day nor the hour wherein the Son of man cometh.

passages.g: Resources are distributed according to each person's capacity to use them.
passages.text: For the kingdom of heaven is as a man travelling into a far country, who called his own servants, and delivered unto them his goods. And unto one he gave five talents, to another two, and to another one; to every man according to his several ability; and straightway took his journey.

passages.g: Some people develop what they're given while others waste their gifts through inaction.
passages.text: Then he that had received the five talents went and traded with the same, and made them other five talents. And likewise he that had received two, he also gained other two. But he that had received one went and digged in the earth, and hid his lord’s money.

passages.g: Faithfulness is demonstrated by what you accomplish with what you've been entrusted.
passages.text: After a long time the lord of those servants cometh, and reckoneth with them. And so he that had received five talents came and brought other five talents, saying, Lord, thou deliveredst unto me five talents: behold, I have gained beside them five talents more.

passages.g: Diligence in small responsibilities earns greater opportunity and reward.
passages.text: His lord said unto him, Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things: enter thou into the joy of thy lord.

passages.g: The value of diligence doesn't depend on the scale of resources managed.
passages.text: He also that had received two talents came and said, Lord, thou deliveredst unto me two talents: behold, I have gained two other talents beside them.

passages.g: Equal diligence deserves equal reward regardless of starting resources.
passages.text: His lord said unto him, Well done, good and faithful servant; thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things: enter thou into the joy of thy lord.

passages.g: Inaction stems from misunderstanding authority rather than genuine principle.
passages.text: Then he which had received the one talent came and said, Lord, I knew thee that thou art an hard man, reaping where thou hast not sown, and gathering where thou hast not strawed: And I was afraid, and went and hid thy talent in the earth: lo, there thou hast that is thine.

passages.g: Minimum expectation is putting resources to work, not simply preserving them.
passages.text: His lord answered and said unto him, Thou wicked and slothful servant, thou knewest that I reap where I sowed not, and gather where I have not strawed: Thou oughtest therefore to have put my money to the exchangers, and then at my coming I should have received mine own with usury.

passages.g: Resources flow toward those who use them productively and away from those who don't.
passages.text: Take therefore the talent from him, and give it unto him which hath ten talents. For unto every one that hath shall be given, and he shall have abundance: but from him that hath not shall be taken away even that which he hath.

passages.g: Failure to develop your gifts has ultimate consequences.
passages.text: And cast ye the unprofitable servant into outer darkness: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth. When the Son of man shall come in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then shall he sit upon the throne of his glory:

passages.g: All people will eventually be separated and judged by their character.
passages.text: And before him shall be gathered all nations: and he shall separate them one from another, as a shepherd divideth his sheep from the goats: And he shall set the sheep on his right hand, but the goats on the left.

passages.g: True righteousness manifests as practical care for vulnerable people.
passages.text: Then shall the King say unto them on his right hand, Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world: For I was an hungred, and ye gave me meat: I was thirsty, and ye gave me drink: I was a stranger, and ye took me in:

passages.g: Caring for the sick, imprisoned, and destitute defines moral goodness.
passages.text: Naked, and ye clothed me: I was sick, and ye visited me: I was in prison, and ye came unto me. Then shall the righteous answer him, saying, Lord, when saw we thee an hungred, and fed thee? or thirsty, and gave thee drink? When saw we thee a stranger, and took thee in? or naked, and clothed thee?

passages.g: Compassion toward the vulnerable is equivalent to reverence for the sacred.
passages.text: Or when saw we thee sick, or in prison, and came unto thee? And the King shall answer and say unto them, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these my brethren, ye have done it unto me.

passages.g: Indifference to others' suffering is the fundamental moral failure.
passages.text: Then shall he say also unto them on the left hand, Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the devil and his angels: For I was an hungred, and ye gave me no meat: I was thirsty, and ye gave me no drink:

passages.g: Ignorance of others' need does not excuse failure to help them.
passages.text: I was a stranger, and ye took me not in: naked, and ye clothed me not: sick, and in prison, and ye visited me not. Then shall they also answer him, saying, Lord, when saw we thee an hungred, or athirst, or a stranger, or naked, or sick, or in prison, and did not minister unto thee?

passages.g: Your ultimate fate depends on how you treated vulnerable people.
passages.text: Then shall he answer them, saying, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye did it not to one of the least of these, ye did it not to me. And these shall go away into everlasting punishment: but the righteous into life eternal.

passages.g: Rigid rule-enforcement can override genuine human need.
passages.text: And it came to pass on the second sabbath after the first, that he went through the corn fields; and his disciples plucked the ears of corn, and did eat, rubbing them in their hands. And certain of the Pharisees said unto them, Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath days?

passages.g: Necessity justifies breaking ceremonial rules.
passages.text: And Jesus answering them said, Have ye not read so much as this, what David did, when himself was an hungred, and they which were with him;

passages.g: Spiritual authority transcends ritual law.
passages.text: How he went into the house of God, and did take and eat the shewbread, and gave also to them that were with him; which it is not lawful to eat but for the priests alone? And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.

passages.g: Legalistic scrutiny is often driven by hostile intent rather than principle.
passages.text: And it came to pass also on another sabbath, that he entered into the synagogue and taught: and there was a man whose right hand was withered. And the scribes and Pharisees watched him, whether he would heal on the sabbath day; that they might find an accusation against him.

passages.g: The moral question is whether rules serve human flourishing or prevent it.
passages.text: But he knew their thoughts, and said to the man which had the withered hand, Rise up, and stand forth in the midst. And he arose and stood forth. Then said Jesus unto them, I will ask you one thing; Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?

passages.g: Those invested in rigid systems oppose genuine good that violates their rules.
passages.text: And looking round about upon them all, he said unto the man, Stretch forth thy hand. And he did so: and his hand was restored whole as the other. And they were filled with madness; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus.

passages.g: Spiritual commitment involves deliberate choice of community.
passages.text: And it came to pass in those days, that he went out into a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God. And when it was day, he called unto him his disciples: and of them he chose twelve, whom also he named apostles;

passages.g: Betrayal originates from within one's inner circle.
passages.text: Simon, (whom he also named Peter,) and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew, Matthew and Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes, And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also was the traitor.

passages.g: Authentic spiritual power draws multitudes seeking healing and truth.
passages.text: And he came down with them, and stood in the plain, and the company of his disciples, and a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him, and to be healed of their diseases;

passages.g: Wholeness and healing radiate from genuine sources of spiritual authority.
passages.text: And they that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed. And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all. And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said, Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God.

passages.g: Present hardship and persecution for righteousness are accompanied by spiritual reward and vindication.
passages.text: Blessed are ye that hunger now: for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh. Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you, and when they shall separate you from their company, and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man’s sake.

passages.g: Material wealth provides the only comfort the wealthy will ever receive.
passages.text: Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy: for, behold, your reward is great in heaven: for in the like manner did their fathers unto the prophets. But woe unto you that are rich! for ye have received your consolation.

passages.g: Worldly success can be spiritually empty; true virtue requires loving even enemies.
passages.text: Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now! for ye shall mourn and weep. Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets. But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you,

passages.g: Genuine virtue shows itself through generosity and non-retaliation despite mistreatment.
passages.text: Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you. And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloak forbid not to take thy coat also.

passages.g: Moral goodness requires extending kindness beyond those who reciprocate.
passages.text: Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again. And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise. For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? for sinners also love those that love them.

passages.g: Helping only those who help you back is mere self-interest, not virtue.
passages.text: And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? for sinners also do even the same. And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? for sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again.

passages.g: Unconditional kindness to enemies mirrors divine nature and constitutes spiritual kinship with the sacred.
passages.text: But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil. Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful.

passages.g: You will be forgiven in the measure that you forgive others.
passages.text: Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven:

passages.g: What you give returns to you in equal measure—generosity begets generosity, stinginess begets stinginess.
passages.text: Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again.

passages.g: The blind cannot lead the blind; growth requires learning from someone trustworthy.
passages.text: And he spake a parable unto them, Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch? The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master.

passages.g: We consistently ignore our own major faults while fixating on others' minor ones.
passages.text: And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?

passages.g: Correct your own character before judging or helping others with theirs.
passages.text: Either how canst thou say to thy brother, Brother, let me pull out the mote that is in thine eye, when thou thyself beholdest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly to pull out the mote that is in thy brother’s eye.

passages.g: What a person produces reveals their true nature.
passages.text: For a good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit; neither doth a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit. For every tree is known by his own fruit. For of thorns men do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes.

passages.g: Speech and actions reveal what is stored in the heart; profession without practice is hypocrisy.
passages.text: A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil: for of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh. And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?

passages.g: True discipleship requires both hearing and doing, not mere agreement.
passages.text: Whosoever cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them, I will shew you to whom he is like:

passages.g: Solid principles and deep foundations withstand life's crises.
passages.text: He is like a man which built an house, and digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock: and when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently upon that house, and could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock.

passages.g: Knowledge without practice leaves you unprepared when difficulty arrives.
passages.text: But he that heareth, and doeth not, is like a man that without a foundation built an house upon the earth; against which the stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell; and the ruin of that house was great.

passages.g: Hypocrisy is a corrupting influence that permeates everything.
passages.text: In the mean time, when there were gathered together an innumerable multitude of people, insomuch that they trode one upon another, he began to say unto his disciples first of all, Beware ye of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.

passages.g: Hidden things will eventually be revealed publicly.
passages.text: For there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed; neither hid, that shall not be known. Therefore whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light; and that which ye have spoken in the ear in closets shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.

passages.g: Spiritual integrity matters more than physical survival.
passages.text: And I say unto you my friends, Be not afraid of them that kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do. But I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear: Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him.

passages.g: God's care is detailed and comprehensive; you have immense value.
passages.text: Are not five sparrows sold for two farthings, and not one of them is forgotten before God? But even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not therefore: ye are of more value than many sparrows.

passages.g: Your public stance toward truth has eternal consequences.
passages.text: Also I say unto you, Whosoever shall confess me before men, him shall the Son of man also confess before the angels of God: But he that denieth me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.

passages.g: Some transgressions exceed the reach of forgiveness.
passages.text: And whosoever shall speak a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that blasphemeth against the Holy Ghost it shall not be forgiven.

passages.g: Trust is more reliable than anxious self-preparation.
passages.text: And when they bring you unto the synagogues, and unto magistrates, and powers, take ye no thought how or what thing ye shall answer, or what ye shall say: For the Holy Ghost shall teach you in the same hour what ye ought to say.

passages.g: Spiritual teachers do not settle worldly property disputes.
passages.text: And one of the company said unto him, Master, speak to my brother, that he divide the inheritance with me. And he said unto him, Man, who made me a judge or a divider over you?

passages.g: Security and meaning cannot be found in material abundance.
passages.text: And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man’s life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth. And he spake a parable unto them, saying, The ground of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully:

passages.g: The wealthy are preoccupied with accumulating and securing more.
passages.text: And he thought within himself, saying, What shall I do, because I have no room where to bestow my fruits? And he said, This will I do: I will pull down my barns, and build greater; and there will I bestow all my fruits and my goods.

passages.g: Death renders all accumulation pointless; possessions cannot buy security from mortality.
passages.text: And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry. But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee: then whose shall those things be, which thou hast provided?

passages.g: Selfish accumulation is futile because death is inevitable; obsessive worry about survival undermines spiritual life.
passages.text: So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God. And he said unto his disciples, Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on. The life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment.

passages.g: Don't worry about survival; all creatures are cared for.
passages.text: Consider the ravens: for they neither sow nor reap; which neither have storehouse nor barn; and God feedeth them: how much more are ye better than the fowls? And which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?

passages.g: Stop worrying about the future; nature thrives without anxious effort.
passages.text: If ye then be not able to do that thing which is least, why take ye thought for the rest? Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.

passages.g: If God provides for temporary plants, he'll certainly provide for you.
passages.text: If then God so clothe the grass, which is to day in the field, and to morrow is cast into the oven; how much more will he clothe you, O ye of little faith? And seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink, neither be ye of doubtful mind.

passages.g: Pursue spiritual fulfillment first, and your material needs will follow.
passages.text: For all these things do the nations of the world seek after: and your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things. But rather seek ye the kingdom of God; and all these things shall be added unto you. Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father’s good pleasure to give you the kingdom.

passages.g: Build lasting spiritual wealth through generosity instead of accumulating possessions, and stay spiritually alert.
passages.text: Sell that ye have, and give alms; provide yourselves bags which wax not old, a treasure in the heavens that faileth not, where no thief approacheth, neither moth corrupteth. For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also. Let your loins be girded about, and your lights burning;

passages.g: Stay alert and ready, like servants waiting for their master's return.
passages.text: And ye yourselves like unto men that wait for their lord, when he will return from the wedding; that when he cometh and knocketh, they may open unto him immediately.

passages.g: Those who remain vigilant will be honored and well cared for.
passages.text: Blessed are those servants, whom the lord when he cometh shall find watching: verily I say unto you, that he shall gird himself, and make them to sit down to meat, and will come forth and serve them.

passages.g: Constant readiness is essential because you don't know when judgment arrives.
passages.text: And if he shall come in the second watch, or come in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants. And this know, that if the goodman of the house had known what hour the thief would come, he would have watched, and not have suffered his house to be broken through.

passages.g: Always be ready because reckoning comes at a time you won't expect.
passages.text: Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not. Then Peter said unto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable unto us, or even to all?

passages.g: A faithful manager who does his duties well will be greatly rewarded.
passages.text: And the Lord said, Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall make ruler over his household, to give them their portion of meat in due season? Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing.

passages.g: A steward who misuses his position while thinking his master won't return will face ruin.
passages.text: Of a truth I say unto you, that he will make him ruler over all that he hath. But and if that servant say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming; and shall begin to beat the menservants and maidens, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken;

passages.g: Judgment comes suddenly to those who've stopped being accountable.
passages.text: The lord of that servant will come in a day when he looketh not for him, and at an hour when he is not aware, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the unbelievers.

passages.g: Knowing what's expected but failing to do it brings severe punishment.
passages.text: And that servant, which knew his lord’s will, and prepared not himself, neither did according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

passages.g: Ignorance reduces culpability; greater knowledge demands greater accountability.
passages.text: But he that knew not, and did commit things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more.

passages.g: My purpose is to bring transformative upheaval and division, not earthly peace.
passages.text: I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled? But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished! Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division:

passages.g: Spiritual commitment divides families into opposing sides.
passages.text: For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three.

passages.g: Parents and children, in-laws and relatives split against each other over spiritual truth.
passages.text: The father shall be divided against the son, and the son against the father; the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother; the mother in law against her daughter in law, and the daughter in law against her mother in law.

passages.g: You can accurately predict rain from clouds and heat from wind.
passages.text: And he said also to the people, When ye see a cloud rise out of the west, straightway ye say, There cometh a shower; and so it is. And when ye see the south wind blow, ye say, There will be heat; and it cometh to pass.

passages.g: Yet you can't recognize the moral and spiritual reality unfolding before you.
passages.text: Ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky and of the earth; but how is it that ye do not discern this time? Yea, and why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?

passages.g: Before a dispute reaches judgment, settle it yourself or lose everything.
passages.text: When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison.

passages.g: You'll be imprisoned until you've paid every last cent owed.
passages.text: I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite.

passages.g: A man with edema came before Jesus at a Pharisee's house on the sabbath.
passages.text: And it came to pass, as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharisees to eat bread on the sabbath day, that they watched him. And, behold, there was a certain man before him which had the dropsy.

passages.g: Healing people matters more than keeping religious rules about the sabbath.
passages.text: And Jesus answering spake unto the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day? And they held their peace. And he took him, and healed him, and let him go;

passages.g: You would rescue a trapped animal on the sabbath, so healing people should take priority.
passages.text: And answered them, saying, Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day? And they could not answer him again to these things.

passages.g: Don't take the best seat at a wedding; wait to be invited higher.
passages.text: And he put forth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked how they chose out the chief rooms; saying unto them, When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not down in the highest room; lest a more honourable man than thou be bidden of him;

passages.g: Taking the highest seat risks public humiliation when a more honored guest arrives.
passages.text: And he that bade thee and him come and say to thee, Give this man place; and thou begin with shame to take the lowest room.

passages.g: Take the lowest seat first so the host can honor you by inviting you higher.
passages.text: But when thou art bidden, go and sit down in the lowest room; that when he that bade thee cometh, he may say unto thee, Friend, go up higher: then shalt thou have worship in the presence of them that sit at meat with thee.

passages.g: Self-promotion leads to humiliation; humility leads to honor.
passages.text: For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.

passages.g: Don't invite friends or wealthy neighbors to feasts since they can repay you.
passages.text: Then said he also to him that bade him, When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, neither thy kinsmen, nor thy rich neighbours; lest they also bid thee again, and a recompence be made thee.

passages.g: Invite the poor and disabled instead; you'll be rewarded at the resurrection since they can't repay you.
passages.text: But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind: And thou shalt be blessed; for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just.

passages.g: A wealthy man prepared a great feast and sent invitations to many guests.
passages.text: And when one of them that sat at meat with him heard these things, he said unto him, Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God. Then said he unto him, A certain man made a great supper, and bade many:

passages.g: When the feast was ready, all the invited guests made excuses and refused to come.
passages.text: And sent his servant at supper time to say to them that were bidden, Come; for all things are now ready. And they all with one consent began to make excuse. The first said unto him, I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it: I pray thee have me excused.

passages.g: More guests declined because they had purchased land, livestock, or married.
passages.text: And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee have me excused. And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.

passages.g: The angry master ordered his servant to bring in the poor, disabled, and blind from the streets instead.
passages.text: So that servant came, and shewed his lord these things. Then the master of the house being angry said to his servant, Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind.

passages.g: After they filled the house, the master commanded the servant to compel more people from roads and hedges to come.
passages.text: And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room. And the lord said unto the servant, Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.

passages.g: Those originally invited will taste nothing of the feast.
passages.text: For I say unto you, That none of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper. And there went great multitudes with him: and he turned, and said unto them,

passages.g: To follow me, you must abandon loyalty to family and accept hardship.
passages.text: If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple. And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come after me, cannot be my disciple.

passages.g: Before starting a major project, count whether you have the resources to finish it.
passages.text: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it? Lest haply, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it begin to mock him,

passages.g: A king assesses whether he can defeat his opponent before going to war.
passages.text: Saying, This man began to build, and was not able to finish. Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?

passages.g: Complete spiritual commitment requires giving up all your possessions.
passages.text: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sendeth an ambassage, and desireth conditions of peace. So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple. Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?

passages.g: Salt that has lost its flavor is useless and gets thrown away.
passages.text: It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill; but men cast it out. He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.

passages.g: Tax collectors and sinners gathered to listen to Jesus; religious leaders complained he welcomed them.
passages.text: Then drew near unto him all the publicans and sinners for to hear him. And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them. And he spake this parable unto them, saying,

passages.g: A shepherd leaves ninety-nine sheep to search for one that's lost.
passages.text: What man of you, having an hundred sheep, if he lose one of them, doth not leave the ninety and nine in the wilderness, and go after that which is lost, until he find it? And when he hath found it, he layeth it on his shoulders, rejoicing.

passages.g: When he finds the lost sheep, he calls friends together to celebrate.
passages.text: And when he cometh home, he calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying unto them, Rejoice with me; for I have found my sheep which was lost.

passages.g: Heaven rejoices more over one repenting sinner than over ninety-nine righteous people who don't need to repent.
passages.text: I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance.

passages.g: A woman lights a candle and searches carefully until she finds one lost coin.
passages.text: Either what woman having ten pieces of silver, if she lose one piece, doth not light a candle, and sweep the house, and seek diligently till she find it?

passages.g: She calls friends to celebrate; heaven similarly rejoices when one sinner repents.
passages.text: And when she hath found it, she calleth her friends and her neighbours together, saying, Rejoice with me; for I have found the piece which I had lost. Likewise, I say unto you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth. And he said, A certain man had two sons:

passages.g: A man's younger son demanded his inheritance early, and the father gave it to him.
passages.text: And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he divided unto them his living. And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living.

passages.g: The younger son wasted all his wealth and ended up starving while tending pigs.
passages.text: And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land; and he began to be in want. And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.

passages.g: Desperate and hungry, he realized his father's servants lived better than he did.
passages.text: And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat: and no man gave unto him. And when he came to himself, he said, How many hired servants of my father’s have bread enough and to spare, and I perish with hunger!

passages.g: He decided to return and ask his father to make him a hired servant rather than a son.
passages.text: I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee, And am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants.

passages.g: His father saw him coming, ran to embrace him, and the son confessed his sin.
passages.text: And he arose, and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. And the son said unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son.

passages.g: The father ordered celebration with new clothes, a ring, and a feast.
passages.text: But the father said to his servants, Bring forth the best robe, and put it on him; and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet: And bring hither the fatted calf, and kill it; and let us eat, and be merry:

passages.g: The father celebrated his son's return while his elder son in the fields heard music and dancing.
passages.text: For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to be merry. Now his elder son was in the field: and as he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard musick and dancing. And he called one of the servants, and asked what these things meant.

passages.g: Learning his younger brother had returned, the elder son became angry and refused to enter.
passages.text: And he said unto him, Thy brother is come; and thy father hath killed the fatted calf, because he hath received him safe and sound. And he was angry, and would not go in: therefore came his father out, and intreated him.

passages.g: The elder son complained that despite years of faithful obedience, he never received a celebration.
passages.text: And he answering said to his father, Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment: and yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends:

passages.g: The father reminded him that all his possessions are the elder son's and he's always present.
passages.text: But as soon as this thy son was come, which hath devoured thy living with harlots, thou hast killed for him the fatted calf. And he said unto him, Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine.

passages.g: The father explained that restoration of the lost is cause for celebration—the dead son is alive again.
passages.text: It was meet that we should make merry, and be glad: for this thy brother was dead, and is alive again; and was lost, and is found.

passages.g: A steward was accused of mismanaging his master's property and ordered to give account.
passages.text: And he said also unto his disciples, There was a certain rich man, which had a steward; and the same was accused unto him that he had wasted his goods. And he called him, and said unto him, How is it that I hear this of thee? give an account of thy stewardship; for thou mayest be no longer steward.

passages.g: Facing dismissal, the steward schemed to manipulate the master's debtors to secure his own future.
passages.text: Then the steward said within himself, What shall I do? for my lord taketh away from me the stewardship: I cannot dig; to beg I am ashamed. I am resolved what to do, that, when I am put out of the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.

passages.g: A steward reduces debtors' obligations.
passages.text: So he called every one of his lord’s debtors unto him, and said unto the first, How much owest thou unto my lord? And he said, An hundred measures of oil. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and sit down quickly, and write fifty.

passages.g: A steward reduces another debtor's obligations by half.
passages.text: Then said he to another, And how much owest thou? And he said, An hundred measures of wheat. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and write fourscore.

passages.g: People pursuing worldly advantage show more shrewdness than the spiritually minded.
passages.text: And the lord commended the unjust steward, because he had done wisely: for the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light.

passages.g: Use resources to build relationships for future security, and character is consistent across small and large decisions.
passages.text: And I say unto you, Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations. He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much.

passages.g: If you mishandle others' trust, you won't be trusted with your own.
passages.text: If therefore ye have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches? And if ye have not been faithful in that which is another man’s, who shall give you that which is your own?

passages.g: You cannot genuinely serve two contradictory masters or values.
passages.text: No servant can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon. And the Pharisees also, who were covetous, heard all these things: and they derided him.

passages.g: What people highly value is often worthless to God.
passages.text: And he said unto them, Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God.

passages.g: The prophetic era has ended and the kingdom is now proclaimed, but the law's authority remains absolute.
passages.text: The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached, and every man presseth into it. And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail.

passages.g: Divorcing to remarry constitutes adultery.
passages.text: Whosoever putteth away his wife, and marrieth another, committeth adultery: and whosoever marrieth her that is put away from her husband committeth adultery. There was a certain rich man, which was clothed in purple and fine linen, and fared sumptuously every day:

passages.g: A poor man sits ignored at a wealthy man's gate.
passages.text: And there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, which was laid at his gate, full of sores, And desiring to be fed with the crumbs which fell from the rich man’s table: moreover the dogs came and licked his sores.

passages.g: Death reverses earthly fortune: the scorned become honored while the privileged become tormented.
passages.text: And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham’s bosom: the rich man also died, and was buried; And in hell he lift up his eyes, being in torments, and seeth Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom.

passages.g: The formerly wealthy man begs for relief from his torment.
passages.text: And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame.

passages.g: Those comfortable in life suffer afterward; those who suffered become comforted.
passages.text: But Abraham said, Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented.

passages.g: The barrier between states after death is permanent and unbridgeable.
passages.text: And beside all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed: so that they which would pass from hence to you cannot; neither can they pass to us, that would come from thence. Then he said, I pray thee therefore, father, that thou wouldest send him to my father’s house:

passages.g: Those who ignore clear instruction won't be convinced by signs from the dead.
passages.text: For I have five brethren; that he may testify unto them, lest they also come into this place of torment. Abraham saith unto him, They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them. And he said, Nay, father Abraham: but if one went unto them from the dead, they will repent.

passages.g: Signs from the dead won't convince those who reject scripture.
passages.text: And he said unto him, If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead.

passages.g: His love persists to the very end.
passages.text: Now before the feast of the passover, when Jesus knew that his hour was come that he should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved his own which were in the world, he loved them unto the end.

passages.g: He foresees the betrayal even as he knows all things are in his hands.
passages.text: And supper being ended, the devil having now put into the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, to betray him; Jesus knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he was come from God, and went to God;

passages.g: The leader performs humble service, washing his followers' feet.
passages.text: He riseth from supper, and laid aside his garments; and took a towel, and girded himself. After that he poureth water into a bason, and began to wash the disciples’ feet, and to wipe them with the towel wherewith he was girded.

passages.g: A follower questions why the leader would serve him in this way.
passages.text: Then cometh he to Simon Peter: and Peter saith unto him, Lord, dost thou wash my feet? Jesus answered and said unto him, What I do thou knowest not now; but thou shalt know hereafter.

passages.g: Accepting the leader's humbling service is necessary to remain connected to him.
passages.text: Peter saith unto him, Thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus answered him, If I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me. Simon Peter saith unto him, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.

passages.g: One fully cleansed needs only foot-washing to stay clean; not all present are spiritually clean.
passages.text: Jesus saith to him, He that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet, but is clean every whit: and ye are clean, but not all. For he knew who should betray him; therefore said he, Ye are not all clean.

passages.g: If the leader serves humbly, followers must serve one another.
passages.text: So after he had washed their feet, and had taken his garments, and was set down again, he said unto them, Know ye what I have done to you? Ye call me Master and Lord: and ye say well; for so I am. If I then, your Lord and Master, have washed your feet; ye also ought to wash one another’s feet.

passages.g: Those who practice humble service find blessing.
passages.text: For I have given you an example, that ye should do as I have done to you. Verily, verily, I say unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord; neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him. If ye know these things, happy are ye if ye do them.

passages.g: One follower will betray him to fulfill prophecy.
passages.text: I speak not of you all: I know whom I have chosen: but that the scripture may be fulfilled, He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me. Now I tell you before it come, that, when it is come to pass, ye may believe that I am he.

passages.g: Receiving an emissary is equivalent to receiving the sender; one follower will betray him.
passages.text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that receiveth whomsoever I send receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me. When Jesus had thus said, he was troubled in spirit, and testified, and said, Verily, verily, I say unto you, that one of you shall betray me.

passages.g: Followers look to each other, uncertain who will betray.
passages.text: Then the disciples looked one on another, doubting of whom he spake. Now there was leaning on Jesus’ bosom one of his disciples, whom Jesus loved. Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him, that he should ask who it should be of whom he spake.

passages.g: The beloved follower asks to know who will betray him.
passages.text: He then lying on Jesus’ breast saith unto him, Lord, who is it? Jesus answered, He it is, to whom I shall give a sop, when I have dipped it. And when he had dipped the sop, he gave it to Judas Iscariot, the son of Simon.

passages.g: Evil enters the betrayer, who is told to act immediately.
passages.text: And after the sop Satan entered into him. Then said Jesus unto him, That thou doest, do quickly. Now no man at the table knew for what intent he spake this unto him.

passages.g: Others misinterpret the instruction as being about provisions or charity.
passages.text: For some of them thought, because Judas had the bag, that Jesus had said unto him, Buy those things that we have need of against the feast; or, that he should give something to the poor. He then having received the sop went immediately out: and it was night.

passages.g: After the betrayer leaves, he speaks of his glorification and the Father's glorification in him.
passages.text: Therefore, when he was gone out, Jesus said, Now is the Son of man glorified, and God is glorified in him. If God be glorified in him, God shall also glorify him in himself, and shall straightway glorify him.

passages.g: The essential teaching is to love one another as he loves you.
passages.text: Little children, yet a little while I am with you. Ye shall seek me: and as I said unto the Jews, Whither I go, ye cannot come; so now I say to you. A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.

passages.g: Love for each other marks his followers; a follower asks where he is going.
passages.text: By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another. Simon Peter said unto him, Lord, whither goest thou? Jesus answered him, Whither I go, thou canst not follow me now; but thou shalt follow me afterwards.

passages.g: A follower claims he will die for him; he foretells the follower's denial.
passages.text: Peter said unto him, Lord, why cannot I follow thee now? I will lay down my life for thy sake. Jesus answered him, Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake? Verily, verily, I say unto thee, The cock shall not crow, till thou hast denied me thrice.

passages.g: Don't be anxious about uncertainty; he is preparing a home for you.
passages.text: Let not your heart be troubled: ye believe in God, believe also in me. In my Father’s house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.

passages.g: He prepares a home and will return for you; a follower asks how to find the way.
