passages.text: Whoso performeth — diligent, content — The work allotted him, whate'er it be, Lays hold of perfectness! Hear how a man Findeth perfection, being so content: He findeth it through worship — wrought by work — Of Him that is the Source of all which lives, Of HIM by Whom the universe was stretched. Better thine own work is, though done with fault, Than doing others' work, ev'n excellently. He shall not fall in sin who fronts the task Set him by Nature's hand! Let no man leave His natural duty, Prince! though it bear blame! For every work hath blame, as every flame Is wrapped in smoke!

passages.g: Freedom from action's consequences comes only through performing duties with complete detachment from ego and results.
passages.text: Only that man attains Perfect surcease of work whose work was wrought With mind unfettered, soul wholly subdued, Desires for ever dead, results renounced.

passages.g: Through discipline, detachment from sensory desire, solitude, and meditation, one transcends suffering and achieves union with the divine.
passages.text: Learn from me, Son of Kunti! also this, How one, attaining perfect peace, attains BRAHM, the supreme, the highest height of all! Devoted — with a heart grown pure, restrained In lordly self-control, forgoing wiles Of song and senses, freed from love and hate, Dwelling 'mid solitudes, in diet spare, With body, speech, and will tamed to obey, Ever to holy meditation vowed, From passions liberate, quit of the Self, Of arrogance, impatience, anger, pride; Freed from surroundings, quiet, lacking nought — Such an one grows to oneness with the BRAHM; Such an one, growing one with BRAHM, serene, Sorrows no more, desires no more;

passages.g: Devoted love of and trust in the divine leads to eternal peace and invulnerability.
passages.text: his soul, Equally loving all that lives, loves well Me, Who have made them, and attains to Me. By this same love and worship doth he know Me as I am, how high and wonderful, And knowing, straightway enters into Me. And whatsoever deeds he doeth — fixed In Me, as in his refuge — he hath won For ever and for ever by My grace Th' Eternal Rest! So win thou! In thy thoughts Do all thou dost for Me! Renounce for Me! Sacrifice heart and mind and will to Me! Live in the faith of Me! In faith of Me All dangers thou shalt vanquish, by My grace; But, trusting to thyself and heeding not, Thou can'st but perish! If this day thou say'st, Relying on thyself, "I will not fight!" Vain will the purpose prove!

passages.g: All action is ultimately orchestrated by a divine principle; peace comes through surrendering to rather than resisting this force.
passages.text: thy qualities Would spur thee to the war. What thou dost shun, Misled by fair illusions, thou wouldst seek Against thy will, when the task comes to thee Waking the promptings in thy nature set. There lives a Master in the hearts of men Maketh their deeds, by subtle pulling — strings, Dance to what tune HE will. With all thy soul Trust Him, and take Him for thy succour, Prince! So — only so, Arjuna! — shalt thou gain — By grace of Him — the uttermost repose, The Eternal Place!

passages.g: Complete devotion and love toward the divine secures union with it.
passages.text: Thus hath been opened thee This Truth of Truths, the Mystery more hid Than any secret mystery. Meditate! And — as thou wilt — then act! Nay! but once more Take My last word, My utmost meaning have! Precious thou art to Me; right well-beloved! Listen! I tell thee for thy comfort this. Give Me thy heart! adore Me! serve Me! cling In faith and love and reverence to Me! So shalt thou come to Me! I promise true, For thou art sweet to Me!

passages.g: Ultimate liberation comes through complete surrender to the divine rather than through adherence to external rules and duties.
passages.text: And let go those — Rites and writ duties! Fly to Me alone! Make Me thy single refuge! I will free Thy soul from all its sins! Be of good cheer!

passages.g: When doubt is resolved through teaching, one gains clarity and commitment.
passages.text: Hath this been heard by thee, O Indian Prince! With mind intent? hath all the ignorance — Which bred thy trouble — vanished, My Arjun? Arjuna. Trouble and ignorance are gone! the Light Hath come unto me, by Thy favour, Lord! Now am I fixed! my doubt is fled away! According to Thy word, so will I do! Sanjaya. Thus gathered I the gracious speech of Krishna, O my King! Thus have I told, with heart a-thrill, this wise and wondrous thing By great Vyasa's learning writ, how Krishna's self made known The Yoga, being Yoga's Lord. So is the high truth shown! And aye, when I remember, O Lord my King, again Arjuna and the God in talk, and all this holy strain, Great is my gladness:

passages.g: This dialogue embodies the highest spiritual wisdom and brings blessing.
passages.text: when I muse that splendour, passing speech, Of Hari, visible and plain, there is no tongue to reach My marvel and my love and bliss. O Archer-Prince! all hail! O Krishna, Lord of Yoga! surely there shall not fail Blessing, and victory, and power, for Thy most mighty sake, Where this song comes of Arjun, and how with God he spake.

passages.g: This concludes the Bhagavad Gita's eighteenth chapter.
passages.text: HERE ENDS, WITH CHAPTER XVIII., Entitled "Mokshasanyasayog," Or "The Book of Religion by Deliverance and Renunciation," THE BHAGAVAD-GITA. Some repetitionary lines are here omitted. Technical phrases of Vedic religion. The whole of this passage is highly involved and difficult to render. I feel convinced sankhyanan and yoginan must be transposed here in sense.

passages.g: Spiritual truth transcends worldly attachments and material existence.
passages.text: I am doubtful of accuracy here. A name of the sun. Without desire of fruit. That is,"joy and sorrow, success and failure, heat and cold,"etc. i.e., the body. The Sanskrit has this play on the double meaning of Atman. So in original. Beings of low and devilish nature. Krishna.

passages.g: Translator's note on textual emendation and stylistic choices.
passages.text: I read here janma, "birth;" not jara,"age" I have discarded ten lines of Sanskrit text here as an undoubted interpolation by some Vedantist The Sanskrit poem here rises to an elevation of style and manner which I have endeavoured to mark by change of metre. Ahinsa. The nectar of immortality. Called "The Jap."

passages.g: Translator's note on Sanskrit forms and variant manuscripts.
passages.text: The compound form of Sanskrit words. "Kamalapatraksha" These are all divine or deified orders of the Hindoo Pantheon. "Hail to Thee, God of Gods! Be favourable!" The wind. "Not peering about,"anapeksha. The Calcutta edition of the Mahabharata has these three opening lines. This is the nearest possible version of Kshetrakshetrajnayojnanan yat tajnan matan mama.

passages.g: Sacred stories offer spiritual instruction beyond literal interpretation.
passages.text: I omit two lines of the Sanskrit here, evidently interpolated by some Vedantist. Wombs. I do not consider the Sanskrit verses here-which are somewhat freely rendered — "an attack on the authority of the Vedas," with Mr Davies, but a beautiful lyrical episode, a new "Parable of the fig-tree." I omit a verse here, evidently interpolated.

passages.g: Translator's note on mythological references and terminology.
passages.text: "Of the Asuras," lit. I omit the ten concluding shlokas, with Mr Davis. Rakshasas and Yakshas are unembodied but capricious beings of great power, gifts, and beauty, same times also of benignity. These are spirits of evil wandering ghosts. Yatayaman, food which has remained after the watches of the night. In India this would probably "go bad."

passages.g: Translator's note: Concluding verses omitted as of uncertain authenticity.
passages.text: I omit the concluding shlokas, as of very doubtful authenticity.

passages.g: Filial piety and respect for family bonds form the foundation of all ethical conduct and social order.
passages.text: The philosopher Yu said, 'They are few who, being filial and fraternal, are fond of offending against their superiors. There have been none, who, not liking to offend against their superiors, have been fond of stirring up confusion. 'The superior man bends his attention to what is radical. That being established, all practical courses naturally grow up. Filial piety and fraternal submission! — are they not the root of all benevolent actions?'

passages.g: Genuine virtue doesn't require eloquence or attractive appearance.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom associated with true virtue.'

passages.g: Daily self-examination of faithfulness, sincerity, and learning is essential to moral development.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'I daily examine myself on three points: — whether, in transacting business for others, I may have been not faithful; — whether, in intercourse with friends, I may have been not sincere; — whether I may have not mastered and practised the instructions of my teacher.'

passages.g: Good governance requires integrity, sincerity, frugality, and genuine care for the people.
passages.text: The Master said, To rule a country of a thousand chariots, there must be reverent attention to business, and sincerity; economy in expenditure, and love for men; and the employment of the people at the proper seasons.'

passages.g: Young people should first learn respect for family and virtue before pursuing formal academic study.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A youth, when at home, should be filial, and, abroad, respectful to his elders. He should be earnest and truthful. He should overflow in love to all, and cultivate the friendship of the good. When he has time and opportunity, after the performance of these things, he should employ them in polite studies.'

passages.g: True learning is measured by genuine virtue in conduct toward parents, ruler, and friends, not by book-knowledge alone.
passages.text: Tsze-hsia said, 'If a man withdraws his mind from the love of beauty, and applies it as sincerely to the love of the virtuous; if, in serving his parents, he can exert his utmost strength; if, in serving his prince, he can devote his life; if, in his intercourse with his friends, his words are sincere: — although men say that he has not learned, I will certainly say that he has.'

passages.g: Scholars must be serious, maintain high standards of sincerity, befriend only equals, and readily correct their faults.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the scholar be not grave, he will not call forth any veneration, and his learning will not be solid. 'Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles. 'Have no friends not equal to yourself. 'When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them.'

passages.g: Honoring deceased parents through careful ritual and sacrifice maintains social virtue and proper conduct.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Let there be a careful attention to perform the funeral rites to parents, and let them be followed when long gone with the ceremonies of sacrifice; — then the virtue of the people will resume its proper excellence.'

passages.g: A person of genuine virtue naturally draws information and cooperation through their character rather than through direct questioning.
passages.text: Tsze-ch'in asked Tsze-kung, saying, 'When our master comes to any country, he does not fail to learn all about its government. Does he ask his information? or is it given to him?' Tsze-kung said, 'Our master is benign, upright, courteous, temperate, and complaisant, and thus he gets his information. The master's mode of asking information! — is it not different from that of other men?'

passages.g: Show respect for your father's values while he lives; after his death, maintaining his ways for three years demonstrates true filial piety.
passages.text: The Master said, 'While a man's father is alive, look at the bent of his will; when his father is dead, look at his conduct. If for three years he does not alter from the way of his father, he may be called filial.'

passages.g: Social conduct should feel natural and graceful but cannot abandon the underlying rules that give propriety meaning.
passages.text: The philosopher Yu said, 'In practising the rules of propriety, a natural ease is to be prized. In the ways prescribed by the ancient kings, this is the excellent quality, and in things small and great we follow them. 'Yet it is not to be observed in all cases. If one, knowing how such ease should be prized, manifests it, without regulating it by the rules of propriety, this likewise is not to be done.'

passages.g: Agreements based on righteousness endure; respectful behavior prevents shame; wise relationships provide moral guidance.
passages.text: The philosopher Yu said, 'When agreements are made according to what is right, what is spoken can be made good. When respect is shown according to what is proper, one keeps far from shame and disgrace. When the parties upon whom a man leans are proper persons to be intimate with, he can make them his guides and masters.'

passages.g: True love of learning involves disciplining desires, speaking carefully, and surrounding oneself with virtuous people.
passages.text: The Master said, 'He who aims to be a man of complete virtue in his food does not seek to gratify his appetite, nor in his dwelling place does he seek the appliances of ease; he is earnest in what he is doing, and careful in his speech; he frequents the company of men of principle that he may be rectified: — such a person may be said indeed to love to learn.'

passages.g: Virtue surpasses circumstance; a person of principle who is poor or rich surpasses one who merely avoids superficial faults.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'What do you pronounce concerning the poor man who yet does not flatter, and the rich man who is not proud?' The Master replied, 'They will do; but they are not equal to him, who, though poor, is yet cheerful, and to him, who, though rich, loves the rules of propriety.' Tsze-kung replied, 'It is said in the Book of Poetry, "As you cut and then file, as you carve and then polish." — The meaning is the same, I apprehend, as that which you have just expressed.' The Master said, 'With one like Ts'ze, I can begin to talk about the odes. I told him one point, and he knew its proper sequence.'

passages.g: Don't worry about being unknown; worry about not understanding others.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I will not be afflicted at men's not knowing me; I will be afflicted that I do not know men.'

passages.g: A virtuous leader is like the north star—steady, with all things naturally aligning toward them.
passages.text: The Master said, 'He who exercises government by means of his virtue may be compared to the north polar star, which keeps its place and all the stars turn towards it.'

passages.g: All virtuous instruction can be reduced to one principle: maintaining an upright and sincere mind.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In the Book of Poetry are three hundred pieces, but the design of them all may be embraced in one sentence — "Having no depraved thoughts."'

passages.g: Laws and punishments only create external compliance; virtue and propriety create internal shame and genuine goodness.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the people be led by laws, and uniformity sought to be given them by punishments, they will try to avoid the punishment, but have no sense of shame. 'If they be led by virtue, and uniformity sought to be given them by the rules of propriety, they will have the sense of shame, and moreover will become good.'

passages.g: Moral development progresses through stages: learning in youth, firmness at thirty, certainty at forty, understanding heaven's will at fifty, receptive wisdom at sixty, and spontaneous virtue at seventy.
passages.text: The Master said, 'At fifteen, I had my mind bent on learning. 'At thirty, I stood firm. 'At forty, I had no doubts. 'At fifty, I knew the decrees of Heaven. 'At sixty, my ear was an obedient organ for the reception of truth. 'At seventy, I could follow what my heart desired, without transgressing what was right.'

passages.g: Filial piety means treating parents with consistent respect and ceremony while alive and after death.
passages.text: Mang I asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'It is not being disobedient.' Soon after, as Fan Ch'ih was driving him, the Master told him, saying, 'Mang-sun asked me what filial piety was, and I answered him, — "not being disobedient."' Fan Ch'ih said, 'What did you mean?' The Master replied, 'That parents, when alive, be served according to propriety; that, when dead, they should be buried according to propriety; and that they should be sacrificed to according to propriety.'

passages.g: Parents naturally care deeply for their children's welfare.
passages.text: Mang Wu asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'Parents are anxious lest their children should be sick.'

passages.g: True filial piety requires reverence and respect, not merely material support.
passages.text: Tsze-yu asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'The filial piety of now-a-days means the support of one's parents. But dogs and horses likewise are able to do something in the way of support; — without reverence, what is there to distinguish the one support given from the other?'

passages.g: Genuine filial piety shows through respectful demeanor and willingness to handle the family's burdensome tasks.
passages.text: Tsze-hsia asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'The difficulty is with the countenance. If, when their elders have any troublesome affairs, the young take the toil of them, and if, when the young have wine and food, they set them before their elders, is THIS to be considered filial piety?'

passages.g: Silent understanding demonstrated through conduct runs deeper than verbal facility and apparent obedience.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I have talked with Hui for a whole day, and he has not made any objection to anything I said; — as if he were stupid. He has retired, and I have examined his conduct when away from me, and found him able to illustrate my teachings. Hui! — He is not stupid.'

passages.g: Character cannot be concealed; it's revealed through deeds, motives, and values.
passages.text: The Master said, 'See what a man does. 'Mark his motives. 'Examine in what things he rests. 'How can a man conceal his character? How can a man conceal his character?'

passages.g: A good teacher continuously reviews existing knowledge while acquiring new understanding.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If a man keeps cherishing his old knowledge, so as continually to be acquiring new, he may be a teacher of others.'

passages.g: An accomplished scholar is versatile and broadly capable, not narrowly specialized like a tool.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The accomplished scholar is not a utensil.'

passages.g: Superior people act first and speak according to their deeds.
passages.text: Tsze-kung asked what constituted the superior man. The Master said, 'He acts before he speaks, and afterwards speaks according to his actions.'

passages.g: Excellence shows through universal fairness and inclusion; pettiness shows through favoritism and faction.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man is catholic and no partisan. The mean man is partisan and not catholic.'

passages.g: Learning without thinking wastes effort, and thinking without learning is dangerous.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Learning without thought is labour lost; thought without learning is perilous.'

passages.g: Studying unfamiliar foreign doctrines is harmful.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The study of strange doctrines is injurious indeed!'

passages.g: True knowledge requires honest acknowledgment of both what you understand and what you do not.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Yu, shall I teach you what knowledge is? When you know a thing, to hold that you know it; and when you do not know a thing, to allow that you do not know it; — this is knowledge.'

passages.g: Careful discernment in learning and cautious action, combined with measured speech, leads to success and fewer regrets.
passages.text: Tsze-chang was learning with a view to official emolument. The Master said, 'Hear much and put aside the points of which you stand in doubt, while you speak cautiously at the same time of the others: — then you will afford few occasions for blame. See much and put aside the things which seem perilous, while you are cautious at the same time in carrying the others into practice: — then you will have few occasions for repentance. When one gives few occasions for blame in his words, and few occasions for repentance in his conduct, he is in the way to get emolument.'

passages.g: Leaders who promote virtuous people gain the people's support; those who promote corrupt people lose it.
passages.text: The Duke Ai asked, saying, 'What should be done in order to secure the submission of the people?' Confucius replied, 'Advance the upright and set aside the crooked, then the people will submit. Advance the crooked and set aside the upright, then the people will not submit.'

passages.g: Leaders inspire reverence, loyalty, and virtue through their own character, dignity, kindness, and example.
passages.text: Chi K'ang asked how to cause the people to reverence their ruler, to be faithful to him, and to go on to nerve themselves to virtue. The Master said, 'Let him preside over them with gravity; — then they will reverence him. Let him be filial and kind to all; — then they will be faithful to him. Let him advance the good and teach the incompetent; — then they will eagerly seek to be virtuous.'

passages.g: Moral perfection in family relationships inherently constitutes governance and does not require formal position.
passages.text: Some one addressed Confucius, saying, 'Sir, why are you not engaged in the government?' The Master said, 'What does the Shu-ching say of filial piety? — "You are filial, you discharge your brotherly duties. These qualities are displayed in government." This then also constitutes the exercise of government. Why must there be THAT — making one be in the government?'

passages.g: A person cannot function without truthfulness, which is essential to all meaningful activity.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I do not know how a man without truthfulness is to get on. How can a large carriage be made to go without the cross-bar for yoking the oxen to, or a small carriage without the arrangement for yoking the horses?'

passages.g: Historical patterns and changes allow prediction of future outcomes across generations.
passages.text: Tsze-chang asked whether the affairs of ten ages after could be known. Confucius said, 'The Yin dynasty followed the regulations of the Hsia: wherein it took from or added to them may be known. The Chau dynasty has followed the regulations of Yin: wherein it took from or added to them may be known. Some other may follow the Chau, but though it should be at the distance of a hundred ages, its affairs may be known.'

passages.g: Acting outside your proper sphere is false, and failing to act on known moral truth is cowardice.
passages.text: The Master said, 'For a man to sacrifice to a spirit which does not belong to him is flattery. 'To see what is right and not to do it is want of courage.'

passages.g: Someone willing to commit one transgression shows willingness to commit any.
passages.text: Confucius said of the head of the Chi family, who had eight rows of pantomimes in his area, 'If he can bear to do this, what may he not bear to do?'

passages.g: Those without proper authority should not perform rituals or use symbols reserved for their superiors.
passages.text: The three families used the YUNG ode, while the vessels were being removed, at the conclusion of the sacrifice. The Master said, '"Assisting are the princes; — the son of heaven looks profound and grave:" — what application can these words have in the hall of the three families?'

passages.g: Ritual and music without inner virtue are meaningless.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what has he to do with the rites of propriety? If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what has he to do with music?'

passages.g: The sincerity behind ceremony matters more than perfection of form.
passages.text: Lin Fang asked what was the first thing to be attended to in ceremonies. The Master said, 'A great question indeed! 'In festive ceremonies, it is better to be sparing than extravagant. In the ceremonies of mourning, it is better that there be deep sorrow than a minute attention to observances.'

passages.g: Uncivilized tribes organize differently than civilized states.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The rude tribes of the east and north have their princes, and are not like the States of our great land which are without them.'

passages.g: An inappropriate sacrifice reveals the offerer's lack of understanding of propriety.
passages.text: The chief of the Chi family was about to sacrifice to the T'ai mountain. The Master said to Zan Yu, 'Can you not save him from this?' He answered, 'I cannot.' Confucius said, 'Alas! will you say that the T'ai mountain is not so discerning as Lin Fang?'

passages.g: The virtuous person avoids conflict while remaining noble even in competition.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The student of virtue has no contentions. If it be said he cannot avoid them, shall this be in archery? But he bows complaisantly to his competitors; thus he ascends the hall, descends, and exacts the forfeit of drinking. In his contention, he is still the Chun-tsze.'

passages.g: Proper foundation and technique must precede the application of skill; character must precede ceremony.
passages.text: Tsze-hsia asked, saying, 'What is the meaning of the passage — "The pretty dimples of her artful smile! The well- defined black and white of her eye! The plain ground for the colours?"' The Master said, 'The business of laying on the colours follows (the preparation of) the plain ground.' 'Ceremonies then are a subsequent thing?' The Master said, 'It is Shang who can bring out my meaning. Now I can begin to talk about the odes with him.'

passages.g: Historical knowledge is limited by the survival of records and wise people's testimony.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I could describe the ceremonies of the Hsia dynasty, but Chi cannot sufficiently attest my words. I could describe the ceremonies of the Yin dynasty, but Sung cannot sufficiently attest my words. (They cannot do so) because of the insufficiency of their records and wise men. If those were sufficient, I could adduce them in support of my words.'

passages.g: The essential part of sacrifice is the initial sincere offering; what follows is secondary.
passages.text: The Master said, 'At the great sacrifice, after the pouring out of the libation, I have no wish to look on.'

passages.g: Deep understanding of ritual meaning provides the master key to effortless governance.
passages.text: Some one asked the meaning of the great sacrifice. The Master said, 'I do not know. He who knew its meaning would find it as easy to govern the kingdom as to look on this; — pointing to his palm.

passages.g: One must approach ritual with complete sincerity and presence as if spiritual reality were literally manifest.
passages.text: He sacrificed to the dead, as if they were present. He sacrificed to the spirits, as if the spirits were present. The Master said, 'I consider my not being present at the sacrifice, as if I did not sacrifice.'

passages.g: One cannot evade moral accountability through ritual maneuvering; offending the sacred leaves one without recourse.
passages.text: Wang-sun Chia asked, saying, 'What is the meaning of the saying, "It is better to pay court to the furnace than to the south-west corner?"' The Master said, 'Not so. He who offends against Heaven has none to whom he can pray.'

passages.g: One learns best by studying multiple predecessors and synthesizing their best practices.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Chau had the advantage of viewing the two past dynasties. How complete and elegant are its regulations! I follow Chau.'

passages.g: Genuine inquiry and learning demonstrate proper conduct.
passages.text: The Master, when he entered the grand temple, asked about everything. Some one said, 'Who will say that the son of the man of Tsau knows the rules of propriety! He has entered the grand temple and asks about everything.' The Master heard the remark, and said, 'This is a rule of propriety.'

passages.g: Excellence is measured relative to ability and circumstance, not against an absolute standard.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In archery it is not going through the leather which is the principal thing; — because people's strength is not equal. This was the old way.'

passages.g: One should value the meaning of ritual over material economy.
passages.text: Tsze-kung wished to do away with the offering of a sheep connected with the inauguration of the first day of each month. The Master said, 'Ts'ze, you love the sheep; I love the ceremony.'

passages.g: People wrongly interpret strict propriety in service as flattery rather than respect.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The full observance of the rules of propriety in serving one's prince is accounted by people to be flattery.'

passages.g: Proper relationships require princes to employ ministers by propriety and ministers to serve with faithfulness.
passages.text: The Duke Ting asked how a prince should employ his ministers, and how ministers should serve their prince. Confucius replied, 'A prince should employ his minister according to according to the rules of propriety; ministers should serve their prince with faithfulness.'

passages.g: Art can express joy authentically without excess and sorrow without harm.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The Kwan Tsu is expressive of enjoyment without being licentious, and of grief without being hurtfully excessive.'

passages.g: Focus forward—don't dwell on remonstrating, blaming, or trying to change the past.
passages.text: The Duke Ai asked Tsai Wo about the altars of the spirits of the land. Tsai Wo replied, 'The Hsia sovereign planted the pine tree about them; the men of the Yin planted the cypress; and the men of the Chau planted the chestnut tree, meaning thereby to cause the people to be in awe.' When the Master heard it, he said, 'Things that are done, it is needless to speak about; things that have had their course, it is needless to remonstrate about; things that are past, it is needless to blame.'

passages.g: Performing external forms doesn't demonstrate understanding of their true significance.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Small indeed was the capacity of Kwan Chung!' Some one said, 'Was Kwan Chung parsimonious?' 'Kwan,' was the reply, 'had the San Kwei, and his officers performed no double duties; how can he be considered parsimonious?' 'Then, did Kwan Chung know the rules of propriety?' The Master said, 'The princes of States have a screen intercepting the view at their gates. Kwan had likewise a screen at his gate. The princes of States on any friendly meeting between two of them, had a stand on which to place their inverted cups. Kwan had also such a stand. If Kwan knew the rules of propriety, who does not know them?'

passages.g: Excellence in music requires unity at the start, distinction throughout, and continuity without interruption.
passages.text: The Master instructing the grand music-master of Lu said, 'How to play music may be known. At the commencement of the piece, all the parts should sound together. As it proceeds, they should be in harmony while severally distinct and flowing without break, and thus on to the conclusion.'

passages.g: Even without political power, a person of virtue can serve an important social awakening function.
passages.text: The border warden at Yi requested to be introduced to the Master, saying, 'When men of superior virtue have come to this, I have never been denied the privilege of seeing them.' The followers of the sage introduced him, and when he came out from the interview, he said, 'My friends, why are you distressed by your master's loss of office? The kingdom has long been without the principles of truth and right; Heaven is going to use your master as a bell with its wooden tongue.'

passages.g: Art can be technically excellent without moral substance.
passages.text: The Master said of the Shao that it was perfectly beautiful and also perfectly good. He said of the Wu that it was perfectly beautiful but not perfectly good.

passages.g: Outward forms without inner sincerity—high position without generosity, ritual without reverence, mourning without grief—are contemptible.
passages.text: The Master said, 'High station filled without indulgent generosity; ceremonies performed without reverence; mourning conducted without sorrow; — wherewith should I contemplate such ways?'

passages.g: One should live where virtuous neighbors dwell, as environment shapes character.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is virtuous manners which constitute the excellence of a neighborhood. If a man in selecting a residence, do not fix on one where such prevail, how can he be wise?'

passages.g: Virtue alone sustains character across all material circumstances, from hardship to comfort.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Those who are without virtue cannot abide long either in a condition of poverty and hardship, or in a condition of enjoyment. The virtuous rest in virtue; the wise desire virtue.'

passages.g: Only the truly virtuous can judge people fairly with genuine feeling.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is only the (truly) virtuous man, who can love, or who can hate, others.'

passages.g: Genuine commitment to virtue prevents wrongdoing.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the will be set on virtue, there will be no practice of wickedness.'

passages.g: Never abandon virtue for material gain or comfort, maintaining it through all circumstances.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Riches and honours are what men desire. If it cannot be obtained in the proper way, they should not be held. Poverty and meanness are what men dislike. If it cannot be avoided in the proper way, they should not be avoided. 'If a superior man abandon virtue, how can he fulfil the requirements of that name? 'The superior man does not, even for the space of a single meal, act contrary to virtue. In moments of haste, he cleaves to it. In seasons of danger, he cleaves to it.'

passages.g: True dedication to virtue is rare; most people lack the genuine commitment to sustain it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I have not seen a person who loved virtue, or one who hated what was not virtuous. He who loved virtue, would esteem nothing above it. He who hated what is not virtuous, would practise virtue in such a way that he would not allow anything that is not virtuous to approach his person. 'Is any one able for one day to apply his strength to virtue? I have not seen the case in which his strength would be insufficient. 'Should there possibly be any such case, I have not seen it.'

passages.g: The specific nature of someone's faults reveals their character and values.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The faults of men are characteristic of the class to which they belong. By observing a man's faults, it may be known that he is virtuous.'

passages.g: Gaining true understanding of the right path is worth any cost, even life itself.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If a man in the morning hear the right way, he may die in the evening without regret.'

passages.g: A true scholar should not be distracted by material concerns; focus on comfort suggests shallow commitment.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A scholar, whose mind is set on truth, and who is ashamed of bad clothes and bad food, is not fit to be discoursed with.'

passages.g: The virtuous person follows principle rather than preference or aversion.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man, in the world, does not set his mind either for anything, or against anything; what is right he will follow.'

passages.g: High-minded people focus on virtue and what's lawful; petty people focus on comfort and personal advantage.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man thinks of virtue; the small man thinks of comfort. The superior man thinks of the sanctions of law; the small man thinks of favours which he may receive.'

passages.g: Self-interested behavior breeds criticism and resentment.
passages.text: The Master said: 'He who acts with a constant view to his own advantage will be much murmured against.'

passages.g: Governance fundamentally requires virtuous conduct; propriety without it is pointless.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If a prince is able to govern his kingdom with the complaisance proper to the rules of propriety, what difficulty will he have? If he cannot govern it with that complaisance, what has he to do with the rules of propriety?'

passages.g: Focus on self-cultivation for a role rather than securing position, and earn recognition through worthy conduct.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A man should say, I am not concerned that I have no place, I am concerned how I may fit myself for one. I am not concerned that I am not known, I seek to be worthy to be known.'

passages.g: The fundamental principle is being authentic to one's nature and extending that virtue and benevolence to others.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Shan, my doctrine is that of an all-pervading unity.' The disciple Tsang replied, 'Yes.' The Master went out, and the other disciples asked, saying, 'What do his words mean?' Tsang said, 'The doctrine of our master is to be true to the principles of our nature and the benevolent exercise of them to others, — this and nothing more.'

passages.g: High-minded people deliberate about what's right; petty people deliberate about profit.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The mind of the superior man is conversant with righteousness; the mind of the mean man is conversant with gain.'

passages.g: Use others as mirrors—aspire to the virtuous and examine yourself through the faults of the vicious.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When we see men of worth, we should think of equalling them; when we see men of a contrary character, we should turn inwards and examine ourselves.'

passages.g: Filial piety requires respectfully persisting in guiding parents toward good, even when they resist.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In serving his parents, a son may remonstrate with them, but gently; when he sees that they do not incline to follow his advice, he shows an increased degree of reverence, but does not abandon his purpose; and should they punish him, he does not allow himself to murmur.'

passages.g: Filial duty requires staying near parents or maintaining accountability for one's whereabouts.
passages.text: The Master said, 'While his parents are alive, the son may not go abroad to a distance. If he does go abroad, he must have a fixed place to which he goes.'

passages.g: True filial piety means continuing the father's way even after his death.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the son for three years does not alter from the way of his father, he may be called filial.'

passages.g: One should constantly remember one's parents' advancing years as a source of both reverence and anxiety about mortality.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The years of parents may by no means not be kept in the memory, as an occasion at once for joy and for fear.'

passages.g: The ancient wise people were reluctant to speak because they feared their actions wouldn't match their words.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The reason why the ancients did not readily give utterance to their words, was that they feared lest their actions should not come up to them.'

passages.g: The virtuous person is thoughtful in speech but sincere and earnest in action.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man wishes to be slow in his speech and earnest in his conduct.'

passages.g: Virtue naturally attracts fellowship; isolation and genuine virtue are incompatible.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Virtue is not left to stand alone. He who practises it will have neighbors.'

passages.g: Constant criticism of authority figures or friends damages relationships.
passages.text: Tsze-yu said, 'In serving a prince, frequent remonstrances lead to disgrace. Between friends, frequent reproofs make the friendship distant.'

passages.g: Moral character is recognizable even in adverse circumstances.
passages.text: The Master said of Kung-ye Ch'ang that he might be wived; although he was put in bonds, he had not been guilty of any crime. Accordingly, he gave him his own daughter to wife. Of Nan Yung he said that if the country were well governed he would not be out of office, and if it were ill-governed, he would escape punishment and disgrace. He gave him the daughter of his own elder brother to wife.

passages.g: Exceptional virtue emerges from a society that cultivates it.
passages.text: The Master said of Tsze-chien, 'Of superior virtue indeed is such a man! If there were not virtuous men in Lu, how could this man have acquired this character?'

passages.g: You are refined and specialized rather than broadly versatile.
passages.text: Tsze-kung asked, 'What do you say of me, Ts'ze? The Master said, 'You are a utensil.' 'What utensil?' 'A gemmed sacrificial utensil.'

passages.g: Eloquence breeds resentment; virtue doesn't require it.
passages.text: Some one said, 'Yung is truly virtuous, but he is not ready with his tongue.' The Master said, 'What is the good of being ready with the tongue? They who encounter men with smartnesses of speech for the most part procure themselves hatred. I know not whether he be truly virtuous, but why should he show readiness of the tongue?'

passages.g: Caution about one's readiness for responsibility shows wisdom.
passages.text: The Master was wishing Ch'i-tiao K'ai to enter on official employment. He replied, 'I am not yet able to rest in the assurance of THIS.' The Master was pleased.

passages.g: Daring without judgment leads to recklessness.
passages.text: The Master said, 'My doctrines make no way. I will get upon a raft, and float about on the sea. He that will accompany me will be Yu, I dare say.' Tsze-lu hearing this was glad, upon which the Master said, 'Yu is fonder of daring than I am. He does not exercise his judgment upon matters.'

passages.g: This passage contains only a question without providing teaching.
passages.text: Mang Wu asked about Tsze-lu, whether he was perfectly virtuous.

passages.g: Competence in specific skills doesn't indicate complete moral virtue.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I do not know.' He asked again, when the Master replied, 'In a kingdom of a thousand chariots, Yu might be employed to manage the military levies, but I do not know whether he be perfectly virtuous.' 'And what do you say of Ch'iu?' The Master replied, 'In a city of a thousand families, or a clan of a hundred chariots, Ch'iu might be employed as governor, but I do not know whether he is perfectly virtuous.' 'What do you say of Ch'ih?' The Master replied, 'With his sash girt and standing in a court, Ch'ih might be employed to converse with the visitors and guests, but I do not know whether he is perfectly virtuous.'

passages.g: Some people instantly grasp underlying principles; others learn step-by-step.
passages.text: The Master said to Tsze-kung, 'Which do you consider superior, yourself or Hui?' Tsze-kung replied, 'How dare I compare myself with Hui? Hui hears one point and knows all about a subject; I hear one point, and know a second.' The Master said, 'You are not equal to him. I grant you, you are not equal to him.'

passages.g: Some people are beyond reformation; true character assessment requires observing actions, not merely hearing words.
passages.text: Tsai Yu being asleep during the daytime, the Master said, 'Rotten wood cannot be carved; a wall of dirty earth will not receive the trowel. This Yu! — what is the use of my reproving him?' The Master said, 'At first, my way with men was to hear their words, and give them credit for their conduct. Now my way is to hear their words, and look at their conduct. It is from Yu that I have learned to make this change.'

passages.g: True integrity means not being controlled by emotional reactions.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I have not seen a firm and unbending man.' Some one replied, 'There is Shan Ch'ang.' 'Ch'ang,' said the Master, 'is under the influence of his passions; how can he be pronounced firm and unbending?'

passages.g: Merely refraining from wrongdoing isn't the full measure of virtue.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'What I do not wish men to do to me, I also wish not to do to men.' The Master said, 'Ts'ze, you have not attained to that.'

passages.g: Lived example teaches better than abstract philosophy.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'The Master's personal displays of his principles and ordinary descriptions of them may be heard. His discourses about man's nature, and the way of Heaven, cannot be heard.'

passages.g: Fully mastering what you learn matters more than constantly seeking new knowledge.
passages.text: When Tsze-lu heard anything, if he had not yet succeeded in carrying it into practice, he was only afraid lest he should hear something else.

passages.g: True learning involves constant intellectual engagement and humility to learn from anyone, regardless of status.
passages.text: Tsze-kung asked, saying, 'On what ground did Kung-wan get that title of Wan?' The Master said, 'He was of an active nature and yet fond of learning, and he was not ashamed to ask and learn of his inferiors! — On these grounds he has been styled Wan.'

passages.g: Excellence as a superior person involves humility toward oneself, respect toward superiors, kindness toward people, and justice in governance.
passages.text: The Master said of Tsze-ch'an that he had four of the characteristics of a superior man: — in his conduct of himself, he was humble; in serving his superiors, he was respectful; in nourishing the people, he was kind; in ordering the people, he was just.'

passages.g: Long friendships require maintaining the same respect and care over time.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Yen P'ing knew well how to maintain friendly intercourse. The acquaintance might be long, but he showed the same respect as at first.'

passages.g: Elaborate external display without substance demonstrates poor judgment and wasted effort.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Tsang Wan kept a large tortoise in a house, on the capitals of the pillars of which he had hills made, and with representations of duckweed on the small pillars above the beams supporting the rafters. — Of what sort was his wisdom?'

passages.g: Loyalty and consistency in service are admirable, but assessing whether they constitute complete virtue is difficult.
passages.text: Tsze-chang asked, saying, 'The minister Tsze- wan thrice took office, and manifested no joy in his countenance. Thrice he retired from office, and manifested no displeasure. He made it a point to inform the new minister of the way in which he had conducted the government; — what do you say of him?' The Master replied. 'He was loyal.' 'Was he perfectly virtuous?' 'I do not know. How can he be pronounced perfectly virtuous?' Tsze-chang proceeded, 'When the officer Ch'ui killed the prince of Ch'i, Ch'an Wan, though he was the owner of forty horses, abandoned them and left the country. Coming to another State, he said, "They are here like our great officer, Ch'ui," and left it.

passages.g: Moral purity shown through avoiding complicity with evil is admirable, but may not constitute complete virtue.
passages.text: He came to a second State, and with the same observation left it also; — what do you say of him?' The Master replied, 'He was pure.' 'Was he perfectly virtuous?' 'I do not know. How can he be pronounced perfectly virtuous?'

passages.g: Excessive deliberation is unnecessary; two rounds of reflection suffice for good judgment.
passages.text: Chi Wan thought thrice, and then acted. When the Master was informed of it, he said, 'Twice may do.'

passages.g: Knowing when to withdraw from public office is a rare form of wisdom.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When good order prevailed in his country, Ning Wu acted the part of a wise man. When his country was in disorder, he acted the part of a stupid man. Others may equal his wisdom, but they cannot equal his stupidity.'

passages.g: Accomplishment without self-discipline is incomplete development.
passages.text: When the Master was in Ch'an, he said, 'Let me return! Let me return! The little children of my school are ambitious and too hasty. They are accomplished and complete so far, but they do not know how to restrict and shape themselves.'

passages.g: Not dwelling on others' past wrongdoings reduces ongoing conflict.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Po-i and Shu-ch'i did not keep the former wickednesses of men in mind, and hence the resentments directed towards them were few.'

passages.g: Appearing generous while relying on others' resources isn't genuine integrity.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Who says of Wei-shang Kao that he is upright? One begged some vinegar of him, and he begged it of a neighbor and gave it to the man.'

passages.g: Insincerity, excessive flattery, and pretended friendliness with hidden resentment are despicable.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Fine words, an insinuating appearance, and excessive respect; — Tso Ch'iu-ming was ashamed of them. I also am ashamed of them. To conceal resentment against a person, and appear friendly with him; — Tso Ch'iu-ming was ashamed of such conduct. I also am ashamed of it.'

passages.g: Relating to others rightly means providing comfort to the aged, sincerity to friends, and tender care to the young.
passages.text: Yen Yuan and Chi Lu being by his side, the Master said to them, 'Come, let each of you tell his wishes.' Tsze-lu said, 'I should like, having chariots and horses, and light fur dresses, to share them with my friends, and though they should spoil them, I would not be displeased.' Yen Yuan said, 'I should like not to boast of my excellence, nor to make a display of my meritorious deeds.' Tsze-lu then said, 'I should like, sir, to hear your wishes.' The Master said, 'They are, in regard to the aged, to give them rest; in regard to friends, to show them sincerity; in regard to the young, to treat them tenderly.'

passages.g: Self-awareness about one's own faults is extremely rare.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is all over! I have not yet seen one who could perceive his faults, and inwardly accuse himself.'

passages.g: Integrity without love of learning is more common than excellence.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In a hamlet of ten families, there may be found one honourable and sincere as I am, but not so fond of learning.'

passages.g: Effective leaders must distinguish between important and trivial matters; selective attention to detail is wise, but carelessness everywhere is excessive.
passages.text: The Master said, 'There is Yung! — He might occupy the place of a prince.' Chung-kung asked about Tsze-sang Po-tsze. The Master said, 'He may pass. He does not mind small matters.' Chung-kung said, 'If a man cherish in himself a reverential feeling of the necessity of attention to business, though he may be easy in small matters in his government of the people, that may be allowed. But if he cherish in himself that easy feeling, and also carry it out in his practice, is not such an easy mode of procedure excessive?' The Master said, 'Yung's words are right.'

passages.g: The ideal learner doesn't hold grudges and learns from mistakes, but such virtue is rare.
passages.text: The Duke Ai asked which of the disciples loved to learn. Confucius replied to him, 'There was Yen Hui; HE loved to learn. He did not transfer his anger; he did not repeat a fault. Unfortunately, his appointed time was short and he died; and now there is not such another. I have not yet heard of any one who loves to learn as he did.'

passages.g: Help should go to those in distress, not be distributed without judgment or given to the already fortunate.
passages.text: Tsze-hwa being employed on a mission to Ch'i, the disciple Zan requested grain for his mother. The Master said, 'Give her a fu.' Yen requested more. 'Give her an yu,' said the Master. Yen gave her five ping. The Master said, 'When Ch'ih was proceeding to Ch'i, he had fat horses to his carriage, and wore light furs. I have heard that a superior man helps the distressed, but does not add to the wealth of the rich.' Yuan Sze being made governor of his town by the Master, he gave him nine hundred measures of grain, but Sze declined them. The Master said, 'Do not decline them. May you not give them away in the neighborhoods, hamlets, towns, and villages?'

passages.g: Natural virtue is recognized regardless of conventional expectations.
passages.text: The Master, speaking of Chung-kung, said, 'If the calf of a brindled cow be red and horned, although men may not wish to use it, would the spirits of the mountains and rivers put it aside?'

passages.g: Constant virtue is far rarer than occasional virtue.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Such was Hui that for three months there would be nothing in his mind contrary to perfect virtue. The others may attain to this on some days or in some months, but nothing more.'

passages.g: Different aptitudes suit people for different roles.
passages.text: Chi K'ang asked about Chung-yu, whether he was fit to be employed as an officer of government. The Master said, 'Yu is a man of decision; what difficulty would he find in being an officer of government?' K'ang asked, 'Is Ts'ze fit to be employed as an officer of government?' and was answered, 'Ts'ze is a man of intelligence; what difficulty would he find in being an officer of government?' And to the same question about Ch'iu the Master gave the same reply, saying, 'Ch'iu is a man of various ability.'

passages.g: Virtuous people sometimes refuse power to maintain their independence and integrity.
passages.text: The chief of the Chi family sent to ask Min Tsze- ch'ien to be governor of Pi. Min Tsze-ch'ien said, 'Decline the offer for me politely. If any one come again to me with a second invitation, I shall be obliged to go and live on the banks of the Wan.'

passages.g: Even the most virtuous cannot escape suffering and mortality.
passages.text: Po-niu being ill, the Master went to ask for him. He took hold of his hand through the window, and said, 'It is killing him. It is the appointment of Heaven, alas! That such a man should have such a sickness! That such a man should have such a sickness!'

passages.g: True virtue maintains inner peace despite material hardship and deprivation.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui! With a single bamboo dish of rice, a single gourd dish of drink, and living in his mean narrow lane, while others could not have endured the distress, he did not allow his joy to be affected by it. Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui!'

passages.g: Self-imposed limitations are more culpable than actual inability.
passages.text: Yen Ch'iu said, 'It is not that I do not delight in your doctrines, but my strength is insufficient.' The Master said, 'Those whose strength is insufficient give over in the middle of the way but now you limit yourself.'

passages.g: How one pursues learning determines one's character.
passages.text: The Master said to Tsze-hsia, 'Do you be a scholar after the style of the superior man, and not after that of the mean man.'

passages.g: True virtue shows through meticulous conduct and respect for propriety.
passages.text: Tsze-yu being governor of Wu-ch'ang, the Master said to him, 'Have you got good men there?' He answered, 'There is Tan-t'ai Mieh-ming, who never in walking takes a short cut, and never comes to my office, excepting on public business.'

passages.g: Humble people credit circumstance rather than claiming credit for virtuous action.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Mang Chih-fan does not boast of his merit. Being in the rear on an occasion of flight, when they were about to enter the gate, he whipped up his horse, saying, "It is not that I dare to be last. My horse would not advance."'

passages.g: In decadent times, one needs either rhetorical skill or attractive appearance just to survive.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Without the specious speech of the litanist T'o and the beauty of the prince Chao of Sung, it is difficult to escape in the present age.'

passages.g: Proper conduct requires following established principles and methods.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Who can go out but by the door? How is it that men will not walk according to these ways?'

passages.g: Virtue requires balance between authentic substance and refined culture.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Where the solid qualities are in excess of accomplishments, we have rusticity; where the accomplishments are in excess of the solid qualities, we have the manners of a clerk. When the accomplishments and solid qualities are equally blended, we then have the man of virtue.'

passages.g: Living without integrity is contrary to human nature and depends on luck rather than virtue.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Man is born for uprightness. If a man lose his uprightness, and yet live, his escape from death is the effect of mere good fortune.'

passages.g: Joy in virtue exceeds mere knowledge of it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'They who know the truth are not equal to those who love it, and they who love it are not equal to those who delight in it.'

passages.g: Teaching should match the student's capacity to understand.
passages.text: The Master said, 'To those whose talents are above mediocrity, the highest subjects may be announced. To those who are below mediocrity, the highest subjects may not be announced.'

passages.g: Wisdom focuses on human responsibilities rather than supernatural concerns; virtue prioritizes overcoming difficulties over pursuing success.
passages.text: Fan Ch'ih asked what constituted wisdom. The Master said, 'To give one's self earnestly to the duties due to men, and, while respecting spiritual beings, to keep aloof from them, may be called wisdom.' He asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'The man of virtue makes the difficulty to be overcome his first business, and success only a subsequent consideration; — this may be called perfect virtue.'

passages.g: Wisdom seeks stimulation and activity while virtue achieves peace and longevity.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The wise find pleasure in water; the virtuous find pleasure in hills. The wise are active; the virtuous are tranquil. The wise are joyful; the virtuous are long-lived.'

passages.g: Minor adjustments in governance can cascade into fundamental improvement.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Ch'i, by one change, would come to the State of Lu. Lu, by one change, would come to a State where true principles predominated.'

passages.g: True usefulness may lie in flexibility and paradox rather than rigid form and conventional strength.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A cornered vessel without corners. — A strange cornered vessel! A strange cornered vessel!'

passages.g: True benevolence and superiority include wisdom about when not to sacrifice oneself recklessly; a superior person may be deceived but retains sound judgment.
passages.text: Tsai Wo asked, saying, 'A benevolent man, though it be told him, — 'There is a man in the well' will go in after him, I suppose.' Confucius said, 'Why should he do so?' A superior man may be made to go to the well, but he cannot be made to go down into it. He may be imposed upon, but he cannot be fooled.'

passages.g: Broad learning combined with respect for tradition prevents moral transgression.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man, extensively studying all learning, and keeping himself under the restraint of the rules of propriety, may thus likewise not overstep what is right.'

passages.g: One must sometimes affirm one's integrity despite others' suspicion.
passages.text: The Master having visited Nan-tsze, Tsze-lu was displeased, on which the Master swore, saying, 'Wherein I have done improperly, may Heaven reject me, may Heaven reject me!'

passages.g: Moderation and balance in virtue are ideal but uncommon among people.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Perfect is the virtue which is according to the Constant Mean! Rare for a long time has been its practise among the people.'

passages.g: Perfect virtue transcends mere good works—it involves mutual flourishing and the wisdom to understand others by analogy to oneself.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'Suppose the case of a man extensively conferring benefits on the people, and able to assist all, what would you say of him? Might he be called perfectly virtuous?' The Master said, 'Why speak only of virtue in connexion with him? Must he not have the qualities of a sage? Even Yao and Shun were still solicitous about this. 'Now the man of perfect virtue, wishing to be established himself, seeks also to establish others; wishing to be enlarged himself, he seeks also to enlarge others. 'To be able to judge of others by what is nigh in ourselves; — this may be called the art of virtue.'

passages.g: Faithfully transmitting tradition is itself a worthy role.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A transmitter and not a maker, believing in and loving the ancients, I venture to compare myself with our old P'ang.'

passages.g: A humble person does not claim mastery of silent knowledge, insatiable learning, or tireless teaching.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The silent treasuring up of knowledge; learning without satiety; and instructing others without being wearied: — which one of these things belongs to me?'

passages.g: Failing to cultivate virtue, understand what one learns, act on righteousness, or reform one's faults should concern any thoughtful person.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The leaving virtue without proper cultivation; the not thoroughly discussing what is learned; not being able to move towards righteousness of which a knowledge is gained; and not being able to change what is not good: — these are the things which occasion me solicitude.'

passages.g: When free from worry, one naturally appears content and at ease.
passages.text: When the Master was unoccupied with business, his manner was easy, and he looked pleased.

passages.g: Aging dims capacities one once possessed.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Extreme is my decay. For a long time, I have not dreamed, as I was wont to do, that I saw the duke of Chau.'

passages.g: A good life requires commitment to duty, pursuit of goodness, cultivation of virtue, and refinement through the arts.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Let the will be set on the path of duty. 'Let every attainment in what is good be firmly grasped. 'Let perfect virtue be accorded with. 'Let relaxation and enjoyment be found in the polite arts.'

passages.g: Knowledge should be shared freely with anyone willing to learn, regardless of their station.
passages.text: The Master said, 'From the man bringing his bundle of dried flesh for my teaching upwards, I have never refused instruction to any one.'

passages.g: Teaching requires students who are genuinely motivated and willing to think for themselves; a teacher can guide, not force, understanding.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I do not open up the truth to one who is not eager to get knowledge, nor help out any one who is not anxious to explain himself. When I have presented one corner of a subject to any one, and he cannot from it learn the other three, I do not repeat my lesson.'

passages.g: Behavior should be appropriate to circumstances; one should not indulge in pleasure during others' sorrow or while grieving.
passages.text: When the Master was eating by the side of a mourner, he never ate to the full. He did not sing on the same day in which he had been weeping.

passages.g: A good associate is someone who acts thoughtfully with careful planning, not someone who displays reckless courage.
passages.text: The Master said to Yen Yuan, 'When called to office, to undertake its duties; when not so called, to lie retired; — it is only I and you who have attained to this.' Tsze-lu said, 'If you had the conduct of the armies of a great State, whom would you have to act with you?' The Master said, 'I would not have him to act with me, who will unarmed attack a tiger, or cross a river without a boat, dying without any regret. My associate must be the man who proceeds to action full of solicitude, who is fond of adjusting his plans, and then carries them into execution.'

passages.g: One should pursue what is meaningfully worth pursuing—virtue and what one loves—rather than uncertain material gains.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the search for riches is sure to be successful, though I should become a groom with whip in hand to get them, I will do so. As the search may not be successful, I will follow after that which I love.'

passages.g: Confucius was especially cautious about fasting, war, and sickness.
passages.text: The things in reference to which the Master exercised the greatest caution were — fasting, war, and sickness.

passages.g: Great art moves us profoundly and can temporarily eclipse other concerns.
passages.text: When the Master was in Ch'i, he heard the Shao, and for three months did not know the taste of flesh. 'I did not think'' he said, 'that music could have been made so excellent as this.'

passages.g: One should not serve rulers who lack virtue; virtue gained through righteous action requires no vindication.
passages.text: Yen Yu said, 'Is our Master for the ruler of Wei?' Tsze-kung said, 'Oh! I will ask him.' He went in accordingly, and said, 'What sort of men were Po-i and Shu-ch'i?' 'They were ancient worthies,' said the Master. 'Did they have any repinings because of their course?' The Master again replied, 'They sought to act virtuously, and they did so; what was there for them to repine about?' On this, Tsze-kung went out and said, 'Our Master is not for him.'

passages.g: Contentment in simplicity is real joy; ill-gotten wealth should be valued no more than a passing cloud.
passages.text: The Master said, 'With coarse rice to eat, with water to drink, and my bended arm for a pillow; — I have still joy in the midst of these things. Riches and honours acquired by unrighteousness, are to me as a floating cloud.'

passages.g: Further study would reduce his errors; his main teaching covered the Odes, History, and proper conduct.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If some years were added to my life, I would give fifty to the study of the Yi, and then I might come to be without great faults.' CHAP. XVII The Master's frequent themes of discourse were — the Odes, the History, and the maintenance of the Rules of Propriety. On all these he frequently discoursed.

passages.g: Someone absorbed in learning forgets basic needs and the passage of time.
passages.text: The Duke of Sheh asked Tsze-lu about Confucius, and Tsze-lu did not answer him. The Master said, 'Why did you not say to him, — He is simply a man, who in his eager pursuit (of knowledge) forgets his food, who in the joy of its attainment forgets his sorrows, and who does not perceive that old age is coming on?'

passages.g: Knowledge comes from dedicated study and seeking truth, not from innate possession.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I am not one who was born in the possession of knowledge; I am one who is fond of antiquity, and earnest in seeking it there.'

passages.g: He avoided discussing extraordinary phenomena, feats of strength, chaos, and spiritual matters.
passages.text: The subjects on which the Master did not talk, were — extraordinary things, feats of strength, disorder, and spiritual beings.

passages.g: Every person offers lessons if one observes thoughtfully—imitate their strengths and avoid their weaknesses.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When I walk along with two others, they may serve me as my teachers. I will select their good qualities and follow them, their bad qualities and avoid them.'

passages.g: One endowed with virtue by heaven cannot be harmed by others.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Heaven produced the virtue that is in me. Hwan T'ui — what can he do to me?'

passages.g: He hides nothing from his students and conducts himself with complete transparency.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Do you think, my disciples, that I have any concealments? I conceal nothing from you. There is nothing which I do that is not shown to you, my disciples; — that is my way.'

passages.g: His teaching focused on four areas: letters, ethics, devotion of soul, and truthfulness.
passages.text: There were four things which the Master taught, — letters, ethics, devotion of soul, and truthfulness.

passages.g: True talent and virtue are rare; people who pretend to have what they lack cannot achieve constancy.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A sage it is not mine to see; could I see a man of real talent and virtue, that would satisfy me.' The Master said, 'A good man it is not mine to see; could I see a man possessed of constancy, that would satisfy me. 'Having not and yet affecting to have, empty and yet affecting to be full, straitened and yet affecting to be at ease: — it is difficult with such characteristics to have constancy.'

passages.g: In recreation, he observed ethical limits: fishing without nets and hunting without taking easy prey.
passages.text: The Master angled, — but did not use a net. He shot, — but not at birds perching.

passages.g: Wisdom comes from listening widely, choosing what is good, and retaining observations.
passages.text: The Master said, 'There may be those who act without knowing why. I do not do so. Hearing much and selecting what is good and following it; seeing much and keeping it in memory: — this is the second style of knowledge.'

passages.g: One should receive people without prejudgment and accept their genuine efforts at self-improvement.
passages.text: It was difficult to talk (profitably and reputably) with the people of Hu-hsiang, and a lad of that place having had an interview with the Master, the disciples doubted. The Master said, 'I admit people's approach to me without committing myself as to what they may do when they have retired. Why must one be so severe? If a man purify himself to wait upon me, I receive him so purified, without guaranteeing his past conduct.'

passages.g: Virtue is accessible—sincere intention and effort to be virtuous make it present.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Is virtue a thing remote? I wish to be virtuous, and lo! virtue is at hand.'

passages.g: It is fortunate when others point out one's mistakes.
passages.text: The minister of crime of Ch'an asked whether the duke Chao knew propriety, and Confucius said, 'He knew propriety.' Confucius having retired, the minister bowed to Wu-ma Ch'i to come forward, and said, 'I have heard that the superior man is not a partisan. May the superior man be a partisan also? The prince married a daughter of the house of Wu, of the same surname with himself, and called her, — "The elder Tsze of Wu." If the prince knew propriety, who does not know it?' Wu-ma Ch'i reported these remarks, and the Master said, 'I am fortunate! If I have any errors, people are sure to know them.'

passages.g: One should appreciate and encourage excellence in others.
passages.text: When the Master was in company with a person who was singing, if he sang well, he would make him repeat the song, while he accompanied it with his own voice.

passages.g: True character is measured by whether one's conduct matches one's professed values.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In letters I am perhaps equal to other men, but the character of the superior man, carrying out in his conduct what he professes, is what I have not yet attained to.'

passages.g: He doesn't claim perfection but pursues self-improvement relentlessly and teaches without tiring.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The sage and the man of perfect virtue; — how dare I rank myself with them? It may simply be said of me, that I strive to become such without satiety, and teach others without weariness.' Kung-hsi Hwa said, 'This is just what we, the disciples, cannot imitate you in.'

passages.g: Sincere spiritual practice throughout life matters more than desperate appeals at the moment of death.
passages.text: The Master being very sick, Tsze-lu asked leave to pray for him. He said, 'May such a thing be done?' Tsze-lu replied, 'It may. In the Eulogies it is said, "Prayer has been made for thee to the spirits of the upper and lower worlds."' The Master said, 'My praying has been for a long time.'

passages.g: Restraint and discipline are preferable to indulgence, even if they mean appearing miserly rather than generous.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Extravagance leads to insubordination, and parsimony to meanness. It is better to be mean than to be insubordinate.'

passages.g: Inner peace comes from virtue and righteousness; agitation and distress come from baseness.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man is satisfied and composed; the mean man is always full of distress.'

passages.g: A cultivated person can embody seemingly opposite qualities—gentleness with dignity, authority without harshness.
passages.text: The Master was mild, and yet dignified; majestic, and yet not fierce; respectful, and yet easy.

passages.g: The truest virtue often goes unrecognized because people don't understand its deeper motivation.
passages.text: The Master said, 'T'ai-po may be said to have reached the highest point of virtuous action. Thrice he declined the kingdom, and the people in ignorance of his motives could not express their approbation of his conduct.'

passages.g: All virtues require proper social form and context to express themselves well; leaders set moral tone through their treatment of family and social relations.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Respectfulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes laborious bustle; carefulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes timidity; boldness, without the rules of propriety, becomes insubordination; straightforwardness, without the rules of propriety, becomes rudeness. 'When those who are in high stations perform well all their duties to their relations, the people are aroused to virtue. When old friends are not neglected by them, the people are preserved from meanness.'

passages.g: One should live with constant vigilance and care, as if always on the brink of danger.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang being ill, he called to him the disciples of his school, and said, 'Uncover my feet, uncover my hands. It is said in the Book of Poetry, "We should be apprehensive and cautious, as if on the brink of a deep gulf, as if treading on thin ice," and so have I been. Now and hereafter, I know my escape from all injury to my person, O ye, my little children.'

passages.g: A person of rank should maintain dignity, sincerity, and propriety in conduct and speech.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang being ill, Meng Chang went to ask how he was. Tsang said to him, 'When a bird is about to die, its notes are mournful; when a man is about to die, his words are good. 'There are three principles of conduct which the man of high rank should consider specially important: — that in his deportment and manner he keep from violence and heedlessness; that in regulating his countenance he keep near to sincerity; and that in his words and tones he keep far from lowness and impropriety. As to such matters as attending to the sacrificial vessels, there are the proper officers for them.'

passages.g: Humility, openness to all people, and refusal to retaliate when wronged are marks of genuine excellence.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Gifted with ability, and yet putting questions to those who were not so; possessed of much, and yet putting questions to those possessed of little; having, as though he had not; full, and yet counting himself as empty; offended against, and yet entering into no altercation; formerly I had a friend who pursued this style of conduct.'

passages.g: A superior person maintains principles under all circumstances, even when trusted with great power.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Suppose that there is an individual who can be entrusted with the charge of a young orphan prince, and can be commissioned with authority over a state of a hundred li, and whom no emergency however great can drive from his principles: — is such a man a superior man? He is a superior man indeed.'

passages.g: Those in high positions must have breadth of mind and endurance, bearing the weight of virtue throughout life.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'The officer may not be without breadth of mind and vigorous endurance. His burden is heavy and his course is long. 'Perfect virtue is the burden which he considers it is his to sustain; — is it not heavy? Only with death does his course stop; — is it not long?

passages.g: Poetry awakens the mind, moral propriety shapes character, and music completes one's development.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is by the Odes that the mind is aroused. 'It is by the Rules of Propriety that the character is established. 'It is from Music that the finish is received.'

passages.g: People can be made to follow a course of action but not forced to understand it; true learning must be voluntary.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The people may be made to follow a path of action, but they may not be made to understand it.'

passages.g: Harsh treatment and material frustration can drive even capable people toward recklessness and insubordination.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The man who is fond of daring and is dissatisfied with poverty, will proceed to insubordination. So will the man who is not virtuous, when you carry your dislike of him to an extreme.'

passages.g: Virtue matters more than talent; exceptional ability without character is worthless.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Though a man have abilities as admirable as those of the Duke of Chau, yet if he be proud and niggardly, those other things are really not worth being looked at.'

passages.g: Sustained study and learning naturally transform character over time.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is not easy to find a man who has learned for three years without coming to be good.'

passages.g: Virtue requires sincere commitment, flexibility about visibility based on the times, and discernment about when to engage or withdraw.
passages.text: The Master said, 'With sincere faith he unites the love of learning; holding firm to death, he is perfecting the excellence of his course. 'Such an one will not enter a tottering State, nor dwell in a disorganized one. When right principles of government prevail in the kingdom, he will show himself; when they are prostrated, he will keep concealed. 'When a country is well-governed, poverty and a mean condition are things to be ashamed of. When a country is ill- governed, riches and honour are things to be ashamed of.'
