passages.g: The wise recognize that immortality cannot be found in perishable things.
passages.text: Children (the ignorant) pursue external pleasures; (thus) they fall into the wide- spread snare of death. But the wise, knowing the nature of immortality, do not seek the permanent among fleeting things.

passages.g: The Self is the unified ground through which all diverse forms, sensations, and experiences arise.
passages.text: That by which one knows form, taste, smell, sound, touch and sense enjoyments, by That also one knows whatever remains (to be known). This verily is That (which thou hast asked to know).

passages.g: Understanding the unchanging witness of all waking and dream states brings freedom from sorrow.
passages.text: That by which a mortal perceives, both in dream and in waking, by knowing that great all-pervading Atman the wise man grieves no more.

passages.g: Knowing the Self as the eternal perceiver and lord of time dissolves fear.
passages.text: He who knows this Atman, the honey-eater (perceiver and enjoyer of objects), ever near, as the lord of the past and future, fears no more. This verily is That.

passages.g: The Self pervades all creation, dwelling within the heart as the organizing principle of all elements.
passages.text: He who sees Him seated in the five elements, born of Tapas (fire of Brahman), born before water; who, having entered the cave of the heart, abides therein — this verily is That.

passages.g: The Self encompasses and knows all principles of existence as their unified source.
passages.text: He who knows Aditi, who rises with Prana (the Life Principle), existent in all the Devas; who, having entered into the heart, abides there; and who was born from the elements — this verily is That.

passages.g: The divine principle hidden in matter requires constant spiritual attention and reverence.
passages.text: The all-seeing fire which exists hidden in the two sticks, as the foetus is well-guarded in the womb by the mother, (that fire) is to be worshipped day after day by wakeful seekers (after wisdom), as well as by sacrificers. This verily is That.

passages.g: All existence depends on one transcendent source from which all principles arise.
passages.text: From whence the sun rises, and whither it goes at setting, upon That all the Devas depend. No one goes beyond That. This verily is That.

passages.g: Reality is unified; perceiving false separation between visible and invisible prevents spiritual progress.
passages.text: What is here (in the visible world), that is there (in the invisible); he who sees difference (between visible and invisible) goes from death to death.

passages.g: Direct realization that the visible and invisible are one is essential; dualistic perception blocks liberation.
passages.text: By mind alone this is to be realized. There is no difference whatever (between visible and invisible). He who sees difference here (between these) goes from death to death.

passages.g: The Self, though seemingly small, is the transcendent master of all time and freedom.
passages.text: The Purusha (Self), of the size of a thumb, resides in the middle of the body as the lord of the past and the future, (he who knows Him) fears no more. This verily is That.

passages.g: The Self is pure, unchanging consciousness—eternal, luminous, and the unchanging ground of all existence.
passages.text: That Purusha, of the size of a thumb, is like a light without smoke, lord of the past and the future. He is the same today and tomorrow. This verily is That.

passages.g: Perceiving divisions in visible forms causes the mind to scatter and chase many conflicting directions.
passages.text: As rain water, (falling) on the mountain top, runs down over the rocks on all sides; similarly, he who sees difference (between visible forms) runs after them in various directions.

passages.g: When individual consciousness becomes enlightened, it merges with universal consciousness.
passages.text: O Gautama (Nachiketas), as pure water poured into pure water becomes one, so also is it with the Self of an illumined Knower (he becomes one with the Supreme).

passages.g: Contemplating the eternal unchanging principle brings freedom from suffering and ignorance.
passages.text: The city of the Unborn, whose knowledge is unchanging, has eleven gates. Thinking on Him, man grieves no more; and being freed (from ignorance), he attains liberation. This verily is That.

passages.g: Ultimate reality pervades all phenomena, from celestial bodies to earthly life to human consciousness.
passages.text: He is the sun dwelling in the bright heaven; He is the air dwelling in space; He is the fire burning on the altar; He is the guest dwelling in the house. He dwells in man. He dwells in those greater than man. He dwells in sacrifice. He dwells in the ether. He is (all that is) born in water, (all that) is born in earth, (all that) is born in sacrifice, (all that) is born on mountains. He is the True and the Great.

passages.g: An inner consciousness orchestrates life functions and is the source from which all senses derive.
passages.text: He it is who sends the (in-coming) Prana (life-breath) upward and throws the (out-going) breath downward. Him all the senses worship, the adorable Atman, seated in the centre (the heart).

passages.g: The true self transcends the physical body and continues beyond death.
passages.text: When this Atman, which is seated in the body, goes out (from the body), what remains then? This verily is That.

passages.g: Life depends on a deeper principle that sustains biological breath itself.
passages.text: No mortal lives by the in-coming breath (Prana) or by the out-going breath (Apana), but he lives by another on which these two depend.

passages.g: The eternal absolute and the nature of the self after death can be known.
passages.text: O Gautama (Nachiketas), I shall declare unto thee the secret of the eternal Brahman and what happens to the Self after death.

passages.g: The form an individual takes after death is determined by past actions and knowledge.
passages.text: Some Jivas (individual Souls) enter wombs to be embodied; others go into immovable forms, according to their deeds and knowledge.

passages.g: An eternal, pure consciousness underlies all existence and transcends individual states of being.
passages.text: The Being who remains awake while all sleep, who grants all desires, That is pure, That is Brahman, That alone is said to be immortal. On That all the worlds rest. None goes beyond That. This verily is That.

passages.g: The universal self is one but appears diverse as it manifests in different beings and forms.
passages.text: As fire, though one, having entered the world, becomes various according to what it burns, so does the Atman (Self) within all living beings, though one, become various according to what it enters. It also exists outside.

passages.g: The universal self manifests as many while remaining fundamentally one, existing beyond all forms.
passages.text: As air, though one, having entered the world, becomes various according to what it enters, so does the Atman within all living beings, though one, become various according to what it enters. It also exists outside.

passages.g: The inner self remains untouched by worldly suffering, like the sun undefiled by what it illuminates.
passages.text: As the sun, the eye of the whole world, is not defiled by external impurities seen by the eyes, thus the one inner Self of all living beings is not defiled by the misery of the world, being outside it.

passages.g: Perceiving the one universal self within all beings and within oneself brings eternal happiness.
passages.text: There is one ruler, the Self of all living beings, who makes the one form manifold; the wise who perceive Him seated within their Self, to them belongs eternal bliss, not to others.

passages.g: Recognizing the eternal consciousness within oneself as the ground of all experience brings lasting peace.
passages.text: Eternal among the changing, consciousness of the conscious, who, though one, fulfils the desires of many: the wise who perceive Him seated within their Self, to them belongs eternal peace, not to others.

passages.g: The highest truth transcends description and ordinary means of knowing.
passages.text: They (the wise) perceive that indescribable highest bliss, saying, This is That. How am I to know It? Does It shine (by Its own light) or does It shine (by reflected light)?

passages.g: All visible light and consciousness derive from and depend on one fundamental source of being.
passages.text: The sun does not shine there, nor the moon, nor the stars; nor do these lightnings shine there, much less this fire. When He shines, everything shines after Him; by His light all is lighted.

passages.g: All existence grows from and rests in an eternal source from which nothing escapes.
passages.text: This ancient Aswattha tree has its root above and branches below. That is pure, That is Brahman, That alone is called the Immortal. All the worlds rest in That. None goes beyond That. This verily is That.

passages.g: All existence emanates from universal life force; knowing this brings freedom from death.
passages.text: Whatever there is in the universe is evolved from Prana and vibrates in Prana. That is a mighty terror, like an upraised thunderbolt. They who know That become immortal.

passages.g: All natural forces operate through reverence or obedience to an ultimate underlying principle.
passages.text: From fear of Him the fire burns, from fear of Him the sun shines. From fear of Him Indra and Vayu and Death, the fifth, speed forth.

passages.g: Failure to realize the ultimate before death results in rebirth into physical forms.
passages.text: If a man is not able to know Him before the dissolution of the body, then he becomes embodied again in the created worlds.

passages.g: The ultimate reality manifests differently depending on the level of consciousness perceiving it.
passages.text: As in a mirror, so is He seen within oneself; as in a dream, so (is He seen) in the world of the fathers (departed spirits); as in water, so (is He seen) in the world of Gandharvas (the angelic realm). As light and shadow, so (is He seen) in the world of Brahma (the Creator).

passages.g: Recognizing that consciousness is separate from sensory fluctuations brings freedom from suffering.
passages.text: Knowing that the senses are distinct (from the Atman) and their rising and setting separate (from the Atman), a wise man grieves no more.

passages.g: Consciousness has a hierarchy ascending from senses through mind and intellect to the unmanifested source.
passages.text: Higher than the senses is the mind, higher than the mind is the intellect, higher than the intellect is the great Atman, higher than the Atman is the Unmanifested.

passages.g: Beyond all manifest reality exists an imperceptible, all-pervading consciousness; knowing it brings liberation.
passages.text: Beyond the Unmanifested is the all-pervading and imperceptible Being (Purusha). By knowing Him, the mortal is liberated and attains immortality.

passages.g: The ultimate reality has no visible form but is known through the heart and intellect, not the senses.
passages.text: His form is not to be seen. No one can see Him with the eye. He is perceived by the heart, by the intellect and by the mind. They who know this become immortal.

passages.g: The highest state of consciousness arises when all sensory and mental activity stills completely.
passages.text: When the five organs of perception become still, together with the mind, and the intellect ceases to be active: that is called the highest state.

passages.g: Yoga is the practice of restraining the senses from distracting objects through constant vigilance.
passages.text: This firm holding back of the senses is what is known as Yoga. Then one should become watchful, for Yoga comes and goes.

passages.g: The ultimate cannot be reached through language, thought, or perception—only through direct recognition of being.
passages.text: He cannot be attained by speech, by mind, or by the eye. How can That be realized except by him who says "He is"?

passages.g: Realizing the ultimate as the ground of both visible and invisible existence reveals its nature.
passages.text: He should be realized as "He is" and also as the reality of both (visible and invisible). He who knows Him as "He is," to him alone His real nature is revealed.

passages.g: When all desires cease, the individual transcends death and realizes the absolute.
passages.text: When all desires dwelling in the heart cease, then the mortal becomes immortal and attains Brahman here.

passages.g: Severing all emotional attachments brings freedom from death and the cycle of rebirth.
passages.text: When all the ties of the heart are cut asunder here, then the mortal becomes immortal. Such is the teaching.

passages.g: Life force flows through pathways in the body; the upward path leads to liberation, others to rebirth.
passages.text: There are a hundred and one nerves of the heart. One of them penetrates the centre of the head. Going upward through it, one attains immortality. The other (hundred nerve-courses) lead, in departing, to different worlds.

passages.g: The eternal self dwells in the heart and must be consciously realized through persistent practice.
passages.text: The Purusha, the inner Self, of the size of a thumb, is ever seated in the heart of all living beings. With perseverance man should draw Him out from his body as one draws the inner stalk from a blade of grass. One should know Him as pure and deathless, as pure and deathless.

passages.g: Understanding the nature of the self through teachings and yogic practice brings freedom from death.
passages.text: Thus Nachiketas, having acquired this wisdom taught by the Ruler of Death, together with all the rules of Yoga, became free from impurity and death and attained Brahman (the Supreme). So also will it be with another who likewise knows the nature of the Self.

passages.g: A higher intelligence directs the mind, senses, speech, and vital forces toward their respective objects.
passages.text: By whom commanded and directed does the mind go towards its objects? Commanded by whom does the life-force, the first (cause), move? At whose will do men utter speech? What power directs the eye and the ear?

passages.g: The ultimate is the source and ground of all senses and faculties; realizing this brings liberation.
passages.text: It is the ear of the ear, the mind of the mind, the speech of the speech, the life of the life, the eye of the eye. The wise, freed (from the senses and from mortal desires), after leaving this world, become immortal.

passages.g: The ultimate transcends all sensory and mental faculties and lies beyond both the known and unknown.
passages.text: There the eye does not go, nor speech, nor mind. We do not know That; we do not understand how It can be taught. It is distinct from the known and also It is beyond the unknown. Thus we have heard from the ancient (teachers) who told us about It.

passages.g: That which enables speech cannot itself be grasped through speech—this is the ultimate, not what people ordinarily worship.
passages.text: That which speech does not illumine, but which illumines speech: know that alone to be the Brahman (the Supreme Being), not this which people worship here.

passages.g: That which enables thought transcends thought itself—this is the ultimate, not what people ordinarily worship.
passages.text: That which cannot be thought by mind, but by which, they say, mind is able to think: know that alone to be the Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: That which enables sight cannot itself be seen—this is the ultimate, not what people ordinarily worship.
passages.text: That which is not seen by the eye, but by which the eye is able to see: know that alone to be the Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: That which enables hearing cannot itself be heard—this is the ultimate, not what people ordinarily worship.
passages.text: That which cannot be heard by the ear, but by which the ear is able to hear: know that alone to be Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: That which enables breath is not itself breath—this is the ultimate, not what people ordinarily worship.
passages.text: That which none breathes with the breath, but by which breath is in-breathed: know that alone to be the Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: Thinking you thoroughly understand the ultimate is itself a sign of incomplete understanding.
passages.text: If thou thinkest "I know It well," then it is certain that thou knowest but little of the Brahman (Absolute Truth), or in what form He (resideth) in the Devas (minor aspects of Deity). Therefore I think that what thou thinkest to be known is still to be sought after.

passages.g: True understanding transcends the dualism of knowing versus not-knowing.
passages.text: The disciple said: I do not think I know It well, nor do I think that I do not know It. He among us who knows It truly, knows (what is meant by) "I know" and also what is meant by "I know It not."

passages.g: Those who recognize the infinite limits of understanding possess true knowledge; those certain of their knowledge lack it.
passages.text: He who thinks he knows It not, knows It. He who thinks he knows It, knows It not. The true knowers think they can never know It (because of Its infinitude), while the ignorant think they know It.

passages.g: Recognizing the unchanging self present throughout all states of consciousness brings strength and liberation.
passages.text: It (Brahman) is known, when It is known in every state of consciousness. (Through such knowledge) one attains immortality. By attaining this Self, man gains strength; and by Self-knowledge immortality is attained.

passages.g: Recognizing the same universal self in all beings brings liberation from the cycle of rebirth.
passages.text: If one knows It here, that is Truth; if one knows It not here, then great is his loss. The wise seeing the same Self in all beings, being liberated from this world, become immortal.

passages.g: Powers that enable worldly effects mistake their own capacity for the true source of causation.
passages.text: The Brahman once won a victory for the Devas. Through that victory of the Brahman, the Devas became elated. They thought, "This victory is ours. This glory is ours."

passages.g: The ultimate manifests in response to correct misapprehension about power and agency.
passages.text: The Brahman perceived this and appeared before them. They did not know what mysterious form it was.

passages.g: Fire is sent to investigate and comprehend the ultimate mystery.
passages.text: They said to Fire: "O Jataveda (All-knowing)! Find out what mysterious spirit this is." He said: "Yes."

passages.g: Fire identifies itself through its own distinctive power and nature.
passages.text: He ran towards it and He (Brahman) said to him: "Who art thou?" "I am Agni, I am Jataveda," he (the Fire-god) replied.

passages.g: Each power understands its own domain as all-encompassing within the world.
passages.text: Brahman asked: "What power resides in thee?" Agni replied: "I can burn up all whatsoever exists on earth."

passages.g: Even the greatest particular power cannot touch or affect the ultimate mystery.
passages.text: Brahman placed a straw before him and said: "Burn this." He (Agni) rushed towards it with all speed, but was not able to burn it. So he returned from there and said (to the Devas): "I was not able to find out what this great mystery is."

passages.g: Wind is then sent to investigate the ultimate source.
passages.text: Then they said to Vayu (the Air-god): "Vayu! Find out what this mystery is." He said: "Yes."

passages.g: Wind identifies itself through its own capacity and nature.
passages.text: He ran towards it and He (Brahman) said to him: "Who art thou?" "I am Vayu, I am Matarisva (traveller of Heaven)," he (Vayu) said.

passages.g: Wind boasts of its power to move all things on earth.
passages.text: Then the Brahman said: "What power is in thee?" Vayu replied: "I can blow away all whatsoever exists on earth."

passages.g: Wind cannot grasp the ultimate—no cosmic force can comprehend what transcends all particularity.
passages.text: Brahman placed a straw before him and said: "Blow this away." He (Vayu) rushed towards it with all speed, but was not able to blow it away. So he returned from there and said (to the Devas): "I was not able to find out what this great mystery is."

passages.g: Even consciousness itself cannot apprehend the ultimate—it transcends direct approach or comprehension.
passages.text: Then they said to Indra: "O Maghavan (Worshipful One)! Find out what this mystery is." He said: "Yes"; and ran towards it, but it disappeared before him.

passages.g: The ultimate reveals itself through beauty and mysterious forms that defy ordinary recognition.
passages.text: Then he saw in that very space a woman beautifully adorned, Uma of golden hue, daughter of Haimavat (Himalaya). He asked: "What is this great mystery?"

passages.g: Understanding the fundamental reality (Brahman) is the source of all power and success.
passages.text: She (Uma) said: "It is Brahman. It is through the victory of Brahman that ye are victorious." Then from her words, he (Indra) knew that it (that mysterious form) was Brahman.

passages.g: Those who gain the deepest understanding of the fundamental nature of reality excel over others.
passages.text: Therefore these Devas, — Agni, Vayu and Indra — excel other Devas, because they came nearer to Brahman. It was they who first knew this spirit as Brahman.

passages.g: Superiority comes from understanding the fundamental reality most fully and first.
passages.text: Therefore Indra excels all other Devas, because he came nearest to Brahman, and because he first (before all others) knew this spirit as Brahman.

passages.g: Ultimate reality appears and vanishes instantaneously, beyond normal perception.
passages.text: Thus the teaching of Brahman is here illustrated in regard to the Devas. He dashed like lightning, and appeared and disappeared just as the eye winks.

passages.g: The mind approaches ultimate reality through repeated contemplation and remembrance.
passages.text: Next (the teaching) is regarding Adhyatman (the embodied Soul). The mind seems to approach Him (Brahman). By this mind (the seeker) again and again remembers and thinks about Brahman.

passages.g: Understanding ultimate reality in its proper nature makes one universally loved.
passages.text: That Brahman is called Tadvanam (object of adoration). He is to be worshipped by the name Tadvanam. He who knows Brahman thus, is loved by all beings.

passages.g: The teaching has been transmitted.
passages.text: The disciple asked: O Master, teach me the Upanishad. (The teacher replied:) The Upanishad has been taught thee. We have certainly taught thee the Upanishad about Brahman.

passages.g: Spiritual wisdom rests on self-discipline, ethical conduct, and truth.
passages.text: The Upanishad is based on tapas (practice of the control of body, mind and senses), dama (subjugation of the senses), karma (right performance of prescribed actions). The Vedas are its limbs. Truth is its support.

passages.g: Knowledge of ultimate reality purifies the mind and establishes one in the highest state.
passages.text: He who knows this (wisdom of the Upanishad), having been cleansed of all sin, becomes established in the blissful, eternal and highest abode of Brahman, in the highest abode of Brahman.

passages.g: People should pursue philosophical wisdom throughout their entire lives, young and old alike.
passages.text: Let no one delay to study philosophy while he is young, and when he is old let him not become weary of the study; for no man can ever find the time unsuitable or too late to study the health of his soul. And he who asserts either that it is not yet time to philosophize, or that the hour is passed, is like a man who should say that the time is not yet come to be happy, or that it is too late. So that both young and old should study philosophy, the one in order that, when he is old, he may be young in good things through the pleasing recollection of the past, and the other in order that he may be at the same time both young and old, in consequence of his absence of fear for the future.

passages.g: Conceive of God as incorruptible and eternally happy, and let this shape your beliefs.
passages.text: It is right then for a man to consider the things which produce happiness, since, if happiness is present, we have everything, and when it is absent, we do everything with a view to possess it. Now, what I have constantly recommended to you, these things I would have you do and practise, considering them to be the elements of living well. First of all, believe that God is a being incorruptible and happy, as the common opinion of the world about God dictates; and attach to your idea of him nothing which is inconsistent with incorruptibility or with happiness; and think that he is invested with everything which is able to preserve to him this happiness, in conjunction with incorruptibility.

passages.g: Gods exist but popular beliefs about them are false; true piety is rejecting these false beliefs, not the divine itself.
passages.text: For there are Gods; for our knowledge of them is indistinct. But they are not of the character which people in general attribute to them; for they do not pay a respect to them which accords with the ideas that they entertain of them. And that man is not impious who discards the Gods believed in by the many, but he who applies to the Gods the opinions entertained of them by the many. For the assertions of the many about the Gods are not anticipations (προλήψεις), but false opinions (ὑπολήψεις). And in consequence of these, the greatest evils which befall wicked men, and the benefits which are conferred on the good, are all attributed to the Gods;

passages.g: People wrongly measure the divine by human qualities and reject what differs from human nature.
passages.text: for they connect all their ideas of them with a comparison of human virtues, and everything which is different from human qualities, they regard as incompatible with the divine nature.

passages.g: Fearing death before it comes is irrational since you'll experience nothing when it arrives.
passages.text: Accustom yourself also to think death a matter with which we are not at all concerned, since all good and all evil is in sensation, and since death is only the privation of sensation. On which account, the correct knowledge of the fact that death is no concern of ours, makes the mortality of life pleasant to us, inasmuch as it sets forth no illimitable time, but relieves us for the longing for immortality. For there is nothing terrible in living to a man who rightly comprehends that there is nothing terrible in ceasing to live; so that he was a silly man who said that he feared death, not because it would grieve him when it was present, but because it did grieve him while it was future.

passages.g: Death cannot harm us because we won't exist to experience it.
passages.text: For it is very absurd that that which does not distress a man when it is present, should afflict him when only expected. Therefore, the most formidable of all evils, death, is nothing to us, since, when we exist, death is not present to us; and when death is present, then we have no existence. It is no concern then either of the living or of the dead; since to the one it has no existence, and the other class has no existence itself. But people in general, at times flee from death as the greatest of evils, and at times wish for it as a rest from the evils in life. Nor is the not living a thing feared, since living is not connected with it: nor does the wise man think not living an evil;

passages.g: Value life by its quality rather than its length; living well and dying well are the same concern.
passages.text: but, just as he chooses food, not preferring that which is most abundant, but that which is nicest; so too, he enjoys time, not measuring it as to whether it is of the greatest length, but as to whether it is most agreeable. And he who enjoins a young man to live well, and an old man to die well, is a simpleton, not only because of the constantly delightful nature of life, but also because the care to live well is identical with the care to die well. And he was still more wrong who said:—

passages.g: Living briefly well is preferable to long life.
passages.text: ’Tis well to taste of life, and then when born To pass with quickness to the shades below.

passages.g: Belief about the future matters only insofar as it guides our choices among natural and empty desires.
passages.text: For if this really was his opinion why did he not quit life? for it was easily in his power to do so, if it really was his belief. But if he was joking, then he was talking foolishly in a case where it ought not to be allowed; and, we must recollect, that the future is not our own, nor, on the other hand, is it wholly not our own, I mean so that we can never altogether await it with a feeling of certainty that it will be, nor altogether despair of it as what will never be. And we must consider that some of the passions are natural, and some empty; and of the natural ones some are necessary, and some merely natural.

passages.g: Happiness is complete freedom of the body from pain and the mind from fear and confusion.
passages.text: And of the necessary ones some are necessary to happiness, and others, with regard to the exemption of the body, from trouble; and others with respect to living itself; for a correct theory, with regard to these things, can refer all choice and avoidance to the health of the body and the freedom from disquietude of the soul. Since this is the end of living happily; for it is for the sake of this that we do everything, wishing to avoid grief and fear; and when once this is the case, with respect to us, then the storm of the soul is, as I may say, put an end to;

passages.g: Once body and soul are well, nothing further can perfect them.
passages.text: since the animal is unable to go as if to something deficient, and to seek something different from that by which the good of the soul and body will be perfected.

passages.g: Pleasure is the natural good; we should choose only those pleasures whose negative consequences don't outweigh them.
passages.text: For then we have need of pleasure when we grieve, because pleasure is not present; but when we do not grieve, then we have no need of pleasure; and on this account, we affirm, that pleasure is the beginning and end of living happily; for we have recognized this as the first good, being connate with us; and with reference to it, it is that we begin every choice and avoidance; and to this we come as if we judged of all good by passion as the standard; and, since this is the first good and connate with us, on this account we do not choose every pleasure, but at times we pass over many pleasures when any difficulty is likely to ensue from them;

passages.g: We rightly endure pain when it leads to greater pleasure afterward.
passages.text: and we think many pains better than pleasures, when a greater pleasure follows them, if we endure the pain for a time.

passages.g: Living simply enables freedom, resilience, and equal satisfaction compared to luxury.
passages.text: Every pleasure is therefore a good on account of its own nature, but it does not follow that every pleasure is worthy of being chosen; just as every pain is an evil, and yet every pain must not be avoided. But it is right to estimate all these things by the measurement and view of what is suitable and unsuitable; for at times we may feel the good as an evil, and at times, on the contrary, we may feel the evil as good. And, we think, contentment a great good, not in order that we may never have but a little, but in order that, if we have not much, we may make use of a little, being genuinely persuaded that those men enjoy luxury most completely who are the best able to do without it;

passages.g: Habituation to simple living guarantees complete satisfaction and fearlessness toward fortune.
passages.text: and that everything which is natural is easily provided, and what is useless is not easily procured. And simple flavours give as much pleasure as costly fare, when everything that can give pain, and every feeling of want, is removed; and corn and water give the most extreme pleasure when any one in need eats them. To accustom one’s self, therefore, to simple and inexpensive habits is a great ingredient in the perfecting of health, and makes a man free from hesitation with respect to the necessary uses of life. And when we, on certain occasions, fall in with more sumptuous fare, it makes us in a better disposition towards it, and renders us fearless with respect to fortune.

passages.g: True pleasure means freedom of body from pain and mind from disturbance, not sensual excess.
passages.text: When, therefore, we say that pleasure is a chief good, we are not speaking of the pleasures of the debauched man, or those which lie in sensual enjoyment, as some think who are ignorant, and who do not entertain our opinions, or else interpret them perversely; but we mean the freedom of the body from pain, and of the soul from confusion.

passages.g: Rational contemplation that dispels false beliefs brings pleasure, not physical indulgence.
passages.text: For it is not continued drinkings and revels, or the enjoyment of female society, or feasts of fish and other such things, as a costly table supplies, that make life pleasant, but sober contemplation, which examines into the reasons for all choice and avoidance, and which puts to flight the vain opinions from which the greater part of the confusion arises which troubles the soul.

passages.g: Practical wisdom is the foundation of all virtue, and virtue and pleasant living are inseparable.
passages.text: Now, the beginning and the greatest good of all these things is prudence, on which account prudence is something more valuable than even philosophy, inasmuch as all the other virtues spring from it, teaching us that it is not possible to live pleasantly unless one also lives prudently, and honourably, and justly; and that one cannot live prudently, and honestly, and justly, without living pleasantly; for the virtues are connate with living agreeably, and living agreeably is inseparable from the virtues.

passages.g: The ideal life combines correct theology, freedom from fear of death, understanding nature's limits, and clarity about what you control versus what you don't.
passages.text: Since, who can you think better than that man who has holy opinions respecting the Gods, and who is utterly fearless with respect to death, and who has properly contemplated the end of nature, and who comprehends that the chief good is easily perfected and easily provided; and the greatest evil lasts but a short period, and causes but brief pain. And who has no belief in necessity, which is set up by some as the mistress of all things, but he refers some things to fortune, some to ourselves, because necessity is an irresponsible power, and because he sees that fortune is unstable, while our own will is free;

passages.g: Belief in gods you can influence is better than belief in inescapable fate because it preserves moral agency.
passages.text: and this freedom constitutes, in our case, a responsibility which makes us encounter blame and praise. Since it would be better to follow the fables about the Gods than to be a slave to the fate of the natural philosopher; for the fables which are told give us a sketch, as if we could avert the wrath of God by paying him honour; but the other presents us with necessity who is inexorable.

passages.g: Acting virtuously within your circumstances matters more than external success.
passages.text: And he, not thinking fortune a goddess, as the generality esteem her (for nothing is done at random by a God), nor a cause which no man can rely on, for he thinks that good or evil is not given by her to men so as to make them live happily, but that the principles of great goods or great evils are supplied by her; thinking it better to be unfortunate in accordance with reason, than to be fortunate irrationally; for that those actions which are judged to be the best, are rightly done in consequence of reason.

passages.g: Constant contemplation of these teachings brings undisturbed peace.
passages.text: Do you then study these precepts, and those which are akin to them, by all means day and night, pondering on them by yourself, and discussing them with any one like yourself, and then you will never be disturbed by either sleeping or waking fancies, but you will live like a God among men; for a man living amid immortal Gods, is in no respect like a mortal being.

passages.g: Divination either doesn't exist or is irrelevant to how we should live.
passages.text: In other works, he discards divination; and also in his Little Epitome. And he says divination has no existence; but, if it has any, still we should think that what happens according to it is nothing to us.

passages.g: These teachings are discussed at greater length elsewhere.
passages.text: These are his sentiments about the things which concern the life of man, and he has discussed them at greater length elsewhere.

passages.g: The eternal and imperishable is immune to suffering and cannot cause it; only the weak are subject to anger and gratitude.
passages.text: That which is happy and imperishable, neither has trouble itself, nor does it cause it to anything; so that it is not subject to the feelings of either anger or gratitude; for these feelings only exist in what is weak.

passages.g: Death causes no harm because the dead experience nothing.
passages.text: Death is nothing to us; for that which is dissolved is devoid of sensation, and that which is devoid of sensation is nothing to us.

passages.g: Perfect pleasure is simply the absence of pain.
passages.text: The limit of the greatness of the pleasures is the removal of everything which can give pain. And where pleasure is, as long as it lasts, that which gives pain, or that which feels pain, or both of them, are absent.

passages.g: Acute pain is brief; chronic illness is more bearable than acutely painful.
passages.text: Pain does not abide continuously in the flesh, but in its extremity it is present only a very short time. That pain which only just exceeds the pleasure in the flesh, does not last many days. But long diseases have in them more that is pleasant than painful to the flesh.

passages.g: Pleasure and moral virtue are inseparable; you cannot achieve one without the other.
passages.text: It is not possible to live pleasantly without living prudently, and honourably, and justly; nor to live prudently, and honourably, and justly, without living pleasantly. But he to whom it does not happen to live prudently, honourably, and justly, cannot possibly live pleasantly.

passages.g: Seeking power purely for protection from others succeeds only if it actually provides that security.
passages.text: For the sake of feeling confidence and security with regard to men, and not with reference to the nature of government and kingly power being a good, some men have wished to be eminent and powerful, in order that others might attain this feeling by their means; thinking that so they would secure safety as far as men are concerned. So that, if the life of such men is safe, they have attained to the nature of good; but if it is not safe, then they have failed in obtaining that for the sake of which they originally desired power according to the order of nature.

passages.g: Pleasure itself is never inherently bad, though its consequences sometimes are.
passages.text: No pleasure is intrinsically bad: but the efficient causes of some pleasures bring with them a great many perturbations of pleasure.

passages.g: Pleasures differ in duration and scope, not in their fundamental nature.
passages.text: If every pleasure were condensed, if one may so say, and if each lasted long, and affected the whole body, or the essential parts of it, then there would be no difference between one pleasure and another.

passages.g: If physical pleasures could eliminate existential fears and teach moderation, complete devotion to pleasure would be justified.
passages.text: If those things which make the pleasures of debauched men, put an end to the fears of the mind, and to those which arise about the heavenly bodies, and death, and pain; and if they taught us what ought to be the limit of our desires, we should have no pretence for blaming those who wholly devote themselves to pleasure, and who never feel any pain or grief (which is the chief evil) from any quarter.

passages.g: Scientific understanding is necessary to overcome fear of cosmic and natural phenomena.
passages.text: If apprehensions relating to the heavenly bodies did not disturb us, and if the terrors of death have no concern with us, and if we had the courage to contemplate the boundaries of pain and of the desires, we should have no need of physiological studies.

passages.g: Complete peace requires understanding nature; otherwise myths might seem true and generate fear.
passages.text: It would not be possible for a person to banish all fear about those things which are called most essential, unless he knew what is the nature of the universe, or if he had any idea that the fables told about it could be true; and therefore, it is, that a person cannot enjoy unmixed pleasure without physiological knowledge.

passages.g: Protection from other people is worthless without freedom from anxiety about cosmic forces.
passages.text: It would be no good for a man to secure himself safety as far as men are concerned, while in a state of apprehension as to all the heavenly bodies, and those under the earth, and in short, all those in the infinite.

passages.g: True security depends on mental tranquility and modest desires, not external power or wealth.
passages.text: Irresistible power and great wealth may, up to a certain point, give us security as far as men are concerned; but the security of men in general depends upon the tranquillity of their souls, and their freedom from ambition.

passages.g: Natural desires have limits and are easily satisfied; unnecessary desires are endless.
passages.text: The riches of nature are defined and easily procurable; but vain desires are insatiable.

passages.g: The wise achieve the greatest goods through reason, regardless of fortune's favor.
passages.text: The wise man is but little favoured by fortune; but his reason procures him the greatest and most valuable goods, and these he does enjoy, and will enjoy the whole of his life.

passages.g: Justice brings freedom from internal conflict; injustice brings perpetual disturbance.
passages.text: The just man is the freest of all men from disquietude; but the unjust man is a perpetual prey to it.

passages.g: Satisfying basic physical needs doesn't increase pleasure, only diversifies its forms.
passages.text: Pleasure in the flesh is not increased, when once the pain arising from want is removed; it is only diversified.

passages.g: Mental happiness depends on properly understanding and contemplating what causes fear.
passages.text: The most perfect happiness of the soul depends on these reflections, and on opinions of a similar character on all those questions which cause the greatest alarm to the mind.

passages.g: Duration of life doesn't matter; sound reasoning about time gives equal satisfaction whether life is short or long.
passages.text: Infinite and finite time both have equal pleasure, if any one measures its limits by reason.

passages.g: The body's capacity for pleasure is finite, so infinite time would be unnecessary.
passages.text: If the flesh could experience boundless pleasure, it would want to dispose of eternity.

passages.g: Understanding mortality eliminates fear of it and completes happiness; choosing to die well is simply ending a good life.
passages.text: But reason, enabling us to conceive the end and dissolution of the body, and liberating us from the fears relative to eternity, procures for us all the happiness of which life is capable, so completely that we have no further occasion to include eternity in our desires. In this disposition of mind, man is happy even when his troubles engage him to quit life; and to die thus, is for him only to interrupt a life of happiness.

passages.g: Wisdom about sufficiency frees you from struggling for unnecessary and difficult things.
passages.text: He who is acquainted with the limits of life knows, that that which removes the pain which arises from want, and which makes the whole of life perfect, is easily procurable; so that he has no need of those things which can only be attained with trouble.

passages.g: Clear judgment requires a clear grasp of the ultimate end.
passages.text: But as to the subsisting end, we ought to consider it with all the clearness and evidence which we refer to whatever we think and believe; otherwise, all things will be full of confusion and uncertainty of judgment.

passages.g: You cannot reject all sensory experience because sensation is the only standard by which to judge sensation.
passages.text: If you resist all the senses, you will not even have anything left to which you can refer, or by which you may be able to judge of the falsehood of the senses which you condemn.

passages.g: Distinguish carefully between raw sensation and inference based on sensation to preserve reliable judgment.
passages.text: If you simply discard one sense, and do not distinguish between the different elements of the judgment, so as to know on the one hand, the induction which goes beyond the actual sensation, or, on the other, the actual and immediate notion; the affections, and all the conceptions of the mind which lean directly on the sensible representation, you will be imputing trouble into the other sense, and destroying in that quarter every species of criterion.

passages.g: Treating provisional beliefs as equal to certain ones leads to error.
passages.text: If you allow equal authority to the ideas, which, being only inductive, require to be verified, and to those which bear about them an immediate certainty, you will not escape error; for you will be confounding doubtful opinions with those which are not doubtful, and true judgments with those of a different character.

passages.g: All actions must serve the chief natural end; inconsistency between words and deeds shows deviation from it.
passages.text: If, on every occasion, we do not refer every one of our actions to the chief end of nature, if we turn aside from that to seek or avoid some other object, there will be a want of agreement between our words and our actions.

passages.g: Friendship is the most important source of lasting happiness.
passages.text: Of all the things which wisdom provides for the happiness of the whole life, by far the most important is the acquisition of friendship.

passages.g: Friendship provides reliable protection and assurance that difficulties won't persist.
passages.text: The same opinion encourages man to trust that no evil will be everlasting, or even of long duration; as it sees that, in the space of life allotted to us, the protection of friendship is most sure and trustworthy.

passages.g: Desires divide into three kinds: those fulfilling real needs, those adding pleasure without relieving pain, and those arising purely from false beliefs.
passages.text: Of the desires, some are natural and necessary, some natural, but not necessary, and some are neither natural nor necessary, but owe their existence to vain opinions.” (Epicurus thinks that those are natural and necessary which put an end to pains, as drink when one is thirsty; and that those are natural but not necessary which only diversify pleasure, but do not remove pain, such as expensive food; and that these are neither natural nor necessary, which are such as crowns, or the erection of statues.)

passages.g: Desires that cause no pain if unfulfilled aren't necessary and are easy to ignore.
passages.text: Those desires which do not lead to pain, if they are not satisfied, are not necessary. It is easy to impose silence on them when they appear difficult to gratify, or likely to produce injury.

passages.g: When natural desires persist intensely despite causing no pain, they're actually driven by false beliefs.
passages.text: When the natural desires, the failing to satisfy which is, nevertheless, not painful, are violent and obstinate, it is a proof that there is an admixture of vain opinion in them; for then energy does not arise from their own nature, but from the vain opinions of men.

passages.g: Justice emerges from mutual agreements to prevent people from harming each other.
passages.text: Natural justice is a covenant of what is suitable, leading men to avoid injuring one another, and being injured.

passages.g: Justice exists only among beings capable of making and honoring mutual pacts to avoid harm.
passages.text: Those animals which are unable to enter into an argument of this nature, or to guard against doing or sustaining mutual injury, have no such thing as justice or injustice. And the case is the same with those nations, the members of which are either unwilling or unable to enter into a covenant to respect their mutual interests.

passages.g: Justice has no inherent existence; it arises entirely from mutual agreements between people.
passages.text: Justice has no independent existence; it results from mutual contracts, and establishes itself wherever there is a mutual engagement to guard against doing or sustaining mutual injury.

passages.g: Wrongdoing seems bad only because people fear punishment for it.
passages.text: Injustice is not intrinsically bad; it has this character only because there is joined with it a fear of not escaping those who are appointed to punish actions marked with that character.

passages.g: Someone who secretly violates an agreement can never be certain of escaping detection forever.
passages.text: It is not possible for a man who secretly does anything in contravention of the agreement which men have made with one another, to guard against doing, or sustaining mutual injury, to believe that he shall always escape notice, even if he have escaped notice already ten thousand times; for, till his death, it is uncertain whether he will not be detected.

passages.g: Justice serves the same purpose everywhere but takes different forms depending on local conditions.
passages.text: In a general point of view, justice is the same thing to every one; for there is something advantageous in mutual society. Nevertheless, the difference of place, and divers other circumstances, make justice vary.

passages.g: Laws that prove socially useful become genuinely just, whether or not everyone recognizes them as such.
passages.text: From the moment that a thing declared just by the law is generally recognized as useful for the mutual relations of men, it becomes really just, whether it is universally regarded as such or not.

passages.g: A law is truly just only as long as it serves social welfare; when it stops doing so, it ceases to be just.
passages.text: But if, on the contrary, a thing established by law is not really useful for the social relations, then it is not just; and if that which was just, inasmuch as it was useful, loses this character, after having been for some time considered so, it is not less true that, during that time, it was really just, at least for those who do not perplex themselves about vain words, but who prefer, in every case, examining and judging for themselves.

passages.g: Something claimed to be just but conflicting with reason or utility was never truly just.
passages.text: When, without any fresh circumstances arising, a thing which has been declared just in practice does not agree with the impressions of reason, that is a proof that the thing was not really just. In the same way, when in consequence of new circumstances, a thing which has been pronounced just does not any longer appear to agree with utility, the thing which was just, inasmuch as it was useful to the social relations and intercourse of mankind, ceases to be just the moment when it ceases to be useful.

passages.g: To live without fear, cultivate friendships where possible and avoid creating enemies.
passages.text: He who desires to live tranquilly without having any thing to fear from other men, ought to make himself friends; those whom he cannot make friends of, he should, at least, avoid rendering enemies; and if that is not in his power, he should, as far as possible, avoid all intercourse with them, and keep them aloof, as far as it is for his interest to do so.

passages.g: The happiest people are those with nothing to fear from others, enjoying mutual trust and the full benefits of friendship.
passages.text: The happiest men are they who have arrived at the point of having nothing to fear from those who surround them. Such men live with one another most agreeably, having the firmest grounds of confidence in one another, enjoying the advantages of friendship in all their fulness, and not lamenting, as a pitiable circumstance, the premature death of their friends.
